topics.title: Overview of material types: metals, polymers, composites, and ceramics. Criteria for material selection in the automotive industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of material types: metals, polymers, composites, and ceramics. Criteria for material selection in the automotive industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Materials** are defined as the engineered substances (metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites) selected to satisfy structural, functional, safety, and regulatory requirements of ground vehicles while optimizing the trade-off between **performance**, **mass**, **cost**, **manufacturability**, and **sustainability**.

Automotive manufacturing materials are conventionally grouped into **four primary families**:

| Family | Definition | Typical Automotive Role |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Metals** | Crystalline inorganic solids with metallic bonding, offering high ductility and electrical/thermal conductivity. | Engine blocks, chassis, body panels, drivetrain. |
| **Polymers** | Long-chain organic macromolecules (thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers) with covalent backbones. | Interior trim, fuel tanks, hoses, bumpers. |
| **Ceramics** | Inorganic, non-metallic, typically ionic/covalent brittle solids with high hardness and temperature resistance. | Spark plugs, sensors, brake discs, catalytic substrates. |
| **Composites** | Engineered combination of two or more constituents (matrix + reinforcement) yielding synergistic properties. | Body-in-white panels, leaf springs, drive shafts. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automotive Kitchen" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an automotive engineer as a master chef assembling a complex dish. **Metals** are the *sturdy bread* — reliable, structural, holding the whole sandwich together (chassis, engine). **Polymers** are the *fresh fillings and wrapping* — flexible, lightweight, mouldable into any shape (bumpers, dashboards). **Ceramics** are the *crispy toasted crust* — hard, heat-resistant, but brittle if dropped (brake discs, exhaust coatings). **Composites** are the *layered gourmet creation* — combining the strengths of multiple ingredients (carbon fibre + epoxy = strong, light, stiff). Each ingredient is chosen for a *function*, not for being "best" overall.

## 1.3 Selection Criteria in the Automotive Industry — The Five Pillars

Material selection in modern automotive engineering is governed by **five interdependent pillars**:

1. **Mechanical Performance** — yield strength $\sigma_y$, ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u$, Young's modulus $E$, fatigue limit $\sigma_e$, fracture toughness $K_{IC}$, hardness.
2. **Physical & Thermal Properties** — density $\rho$, thermal conductivity $k$, coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$, specific heat $c_p$, melting/glass-transition temperature.
3. **Chemical & Environmental Resistance** — corrosion resistance, oxidation resistance, UV stability, fluid compatibility (oil, fuel, coolant).
4. **Manufacturability** — castability, formability, machinability, weldability, joining compatibility, recyclability.
5. **Economic & Sustainability** — raw-material cost, processing cost, lifecycle cost, embodied energy, end-of-life recyclability, **CO₂ footprint**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Selection in the automotive sector is no longer a single-objective optimization. Modern design uses **Ashby's methodology** (constraints → objectives → material index → screening → ranking) coupled with **Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)** to balance lightweighting against cost and sustainability targets such as **CAFE / BS-VI / Euro-7** regulations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Four Material Families

### 2.1.1 Metals

Metals dominate the automotive structural mass share (typically **~70–75 %** of a conventional vehicle's mass). They are subdivided as:

- **Ferrous metals** — Iron-based alloys. *Examples*: Mild steel (body panels), HSLA steel (chassis), Advanced High-Strength Steel / AHSS (crash structures), Stainless steel (exhaust), Cast iron (engine blocks historically).
- **Non-ferrous metals** — Aluminium (Al-Si castings, body sheets), Magnesium (die-cast instrument panels, steering columns), Titanium (connecting rods, valves), Copper (wiring harness), Zinc (die casting, coatings).

**Why metals?** Combination of high ductility (energy absorption in crash), well-established manufacturing routes, isotropic behaviour, and competitive cost per kg.

### 2.1.2 Polymers

- **Thermoplastics** — linear chains, soften on heating. *Examples*: PP (bumpers), PA6/Nylon (under-hood), POM (gears), ABS (interior), PC (headlamp lenses), HDPE (fuel tanks). Density typically **0.9 – 1.4 g/cm³**.
- **Thermosets** — cross-linked, irreversible cure. *Examples*: Phenolic resin (brake pads), Epoxy (adhesives, composites matrix), Polyurethane (foam seating), SMC/BMC (body panels).
- **Elastomers** — large reversible elastic deformation. *Examples*: EPDM (seals), NBR (hoses), SBR (tyres), Silicone (gaskets).

### 2.1.3 Ceramics

- **Oxides** — Al₂O₃ (spark plug insulator), ZrO₂ (oxygen sensor).
- **Non-oxides** — SiC, Si₃N₄ (high-temp structural), AlN (heat sinks).
- **Glasses** — soda-lime (windshields), borosilicate (lamps).

Ceramics are **brittle** (low $K_{IC}$, near-zero ductility) but offer exceptional **hardness, wear resistance, high-temperature capability, and chemical inertness**.

### 2.1.4 Composites

- **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)** — Glass-fibre reinforced plastic (GFRP) for leaf springs; Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) for body-in-white and roof.
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** — Al-SiC for brake drums, pistons.
- **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)** — SiC/SiC for high-temp exhaust components in premium vehicles.

A composite achieves a **synergistic** combination — e.g., CFRP offers $\sigma/\rho$ ≈ **6–10× that of mild steel**, enabling dramatic lightweighting.

## 2.2 The Ashby Material-Selection Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this method?** It converts a qualitative design question ("Which material is best?") into a quantitative ranking using **material indices** derived from the design equation.

**Step 1 — Translation:** Express the design requirement as a function of material properties (e.g., $m = f(E, \rho, \sigma_y)$).

**Step 2 — Screening:** Apply constraints (e.g., $T_{service} < T_{max}$, $K_{IC} > K_{IC,min}$) to eliminate unsuitable candidates.

**Step 3 — Objectives & Material Index:** Identify what to *minimize* (mass, cost, volume) or *maximize* (energy storage, stiffness). The material index $M$ is the combination of properties that, when maximized, optimizes the objective subject to the constraints.

**Step 4 — Ranking:** Plot the material index on an **Ashby chart** (log-log property map) and identify the family that maximizes $M$.

**Step 5 — Secondary Criteria:** Apply additional considerations (manufacturing route, corrosion, recyclability) to select the final candidate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Automotive Material Indices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every key equation needed to solve KTU 2024 module-1 problems. Memorize these indices — they are examined almost every cycle.

| Design Objective | Functional Requirement | Design Equation | Material Index $M$ | Maximize $M$ to Minimize Mass |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Tie / Tension member** | Stiffness | $\delta = \dfrac{F L}{A E}$ | $M_1 = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $m \propto \dfrac{\rho}{E}$ |
| **Tie / Tension member** | Strength | $\sigma \le \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $M_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $m \propto \dfrac{\rho}{\sigma_y}$ |
| **Beam (Bending)** | Stiffness | $\delta \propto \dfrac{F L^3}{E I}$ | $M_3 = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | $m \propto \dfrac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}$ |
| **Beam (Bending)** | Strength | $\sigma_{max} \propto \dfrac{M y}{I}$ | $M_4 = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | $m \propto \dfrac{\rho}{\sigma_y^{2/3}}$ |
| **Column (Buckling)** | Stiffness (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ | $M_5 = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ | $m \propto \dfrac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}$ |
| **Flywheel / Disk** | Kinetic energy storage | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} J \omega^2$ | $M_6 = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Maximize $\sigma_y / \rho$ |
| **Spring (Elastic strain)** | Energy storage | $U/V = \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 / E$ | $M_7 = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{E \rho}$ | Maximize $\sigma_y^2 / (E\rho)$ |
| **Cost-effective selection** | Minimize cost for stiffness | $C = C_m \cdot m$ | $M_8 = \dfrac{E}{\rho \cdot C_m}$ | Maximize $E / (\rho \cdot C_m)$ |

where:
- $\rho$ = density $\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$
- $E$ = Young's modulus $\left[\text{GPa}\right]$
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength $\left[\text{MPa}\right]$
- $C_m$ = cost per unit mass $\left[\text{INR/kg}\right]$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering

| Application | Dominant Index | Why? |
|-------------|---------------|------|
| Body-in-white | $M_2 = \sigma_y / \rho$ | Crashworthiness + lightweighting. |
| Chassis frame rails | $M_3 = E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Bending stiffness during cornering. |
| Leaf spring | $M_7 = \sigma_y^2 / (E \rho)$ | Elastic energy absorption. |
| Connecting rod | $M_2 = \sigma_y / \rho$ | Fatigue + mass for reciprocating loads. |
| Suspension coil | $M_7 = \sigma_y^2 / (E \rho)$ | Spring constant per unit mass. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Index for a Lightweight Tensile Tie (Tie-Rod Style Component)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A horizontal **tie member** in a suspension sub-frame must carry a tensile axial force $F$ without yielding and without excessive elastic elongation. Find the material index that minimizes the mass $m$ of the tie.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the constraint equation (strength).**

The tie must not yield, so the axial stress must remain below yield:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \le \sigma_y$$

Solving for the minimum required cross-sectional area $A$:

$$A \ge \frac{F}{\sigma_y}$$

For the optimal (minimum-mass) design we take equality:

$$A = \frac{F}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 2 — Express the volume of the tie.**

If the tie has length $L$, then the volume is:

$$V = A \cdot L = \frac{F L}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Express the mass of the tie.**

The mass is density times volume:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \frac{\rho \, F \, L}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the mass proportionality.**

Since $F$ and $L$ are fixed by the design (function, not material), the mass is proportional to:

$$m \propto \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the material index.**

To **minimize mass**, we must **minimize** $\rho / \sigma_y$, which is equivalent to **maximizing** the reciprocal:

$$M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$

This is the **specific strength** — the material index for a minimum-mass tensile member. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Index for a Lightweight Stiff Beam (Bending Application)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A structural **beam** of square cross-section $b \times b$ and span $L$ must have a prescribed bending stiffness $S = F/\delta$ under a tip load $F$ at its free end. Find the material index that minimizes mass.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Standard tip-deflection of a cantilever beam.**

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, tip load $F$, Young's modulus $E$, and second moment of area $I$:

$$\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

Solving for stiffness $S$:

$$S = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{3 E I}{L^3}$$

**Step 2 — Second moment of area for a square section.**

For a square cross-section with side $b$:

$$I = \frac{b^4}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Impose the stiffness constraint.**

We require $S \ge S^{\ast}$ (a specified design stiffness). Using equality at the optimum:

$$\frac{3 E I}{L^3} = S^{\ast}$$

Substituting $I$:

$$\frac{3 E}{L^3} \cdot \frac{b^4}{12} = S^{\ast}$$

Solving for $b$:

$$b^4 = \frac{12 \, S^{\ast} \, L^3}{3 E} = \frac{4 S^{\ast} L^3}{E}$$

$$b = \left(\frac{4 S^{\ast} L^3}{E}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 — Express the volume and mass of the beam.**

Volume of the square cross-section beam:

$$V = b^2 \cdot L = \left(\frac{4 S^{\ast} L^3}{E}\right)^{1/2} \cdot L$$

$$V = L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{4 S^{\ast} L^3}{E}} = L^{5/2} \cdot \frac{2 \, (S^{\ast})^{1/2}}{E^{1/2}}$$

Mass:

$$m = \rho V = \frac{2 \, (S^{\ast})^{1/2} \, L^{5/2} \, \rho}{E^{1/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the proportionality.**

Since $S^{\ast}$ and $L$ are fixed by the design:

$$m \propto \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}$$

Wait — for a **square** section the index is $\rho/E^{1/2}$. However, for a **beam of free cross-section** (i.e., shape is also a design variable), the same derivation yields the classical Ashby index:

$$m \propto \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}$$

The general form for a beam where the **shape is free** to optimize is:

$$M_3 = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho} \quad \text{(specific stiffness, free-shape)}$$

For a **fixed square shape**, the index is $E^{1/2} / \rho$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $\rho/E^{1/2}$ for *all* bending problems. The correct exponent depends on whether the **cross-section shape is fixed or free** — examiners will deduct marks if you do not specify.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass-Reduction Quantitative Worked Example

### 3.3.1 Given
A steel brake pedal arm is currently made of mild steel (AISI 1020). The design requires replacing it with aluminium alloy (6061-T6) **without changing the stiffness**. Estimate the percentage mass saving.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant material index.**

For a bending-dominated beam with a fixed cross-section shape (i.e., the lever-arm geometry is preserved), stiffness is controlled by $EI$. With the **same geometry** we need $E_{\text{steel}} = E_{\text{Al}}$ for *isostiff* design only if the section is changed. Here, we keep the section the same, so the index for **mass** when stiffness is preserved:

$$m_{\text{new}} = m_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{\text{new}}}{\rho_{\text{old}}} \cdot \frac{E_{\text{old}}}{E_{\text{new}}}$$

Wait — if the *section* is preserved, the stiffness scales as $E$, so to keep stiffness constant, we would need to alter section. For **same shape, same length**, the mass reduction is purely due to density reduction:

$$\frac{m_{\text{Al}}}{m_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{Al}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}}$$

But to keep the *same stiffness* with the **same length**, the section must change. A common simplification: assume redesign compensates for the lower $E$ of Al.

**Step 2 — Insert typical values.**

- Mild steel: $\rho_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $E_s = 210 \text{ GPa}$.
- Al 6061-T6: $\rho_a = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $E_a = 69 \text{ GPa}$.

Mass ratio (same shape, same stiffness compensated via section redesign):

$$\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_s} \cdot \left(\frac{E_s}{E_a}\right)^{1/3}$$

Compute:

$$\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_s} = \frac{2700}{7850} \approx 0.344$$

$$\frac{E_s}{E_a} = \frac{210}{69} \approx 3.043$$

$$\left(\frac{E_s}{E_a}\right)^{1/3} = (3.043)^{1/3} \approx 1.450$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{m_a}{m_s} \approx 0.344 \times 1.450 \approx 0.499$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage mass reduction.**

$$\%\text{ saving} = \left(1 - \frac{m_a}{m_s}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.499) \times 100\% \approx 50.1\%$$

**Interpretation:** Replacing the steel brake pedal with an optimally redesigned aluminium equivalent achieves roughly a **50 % mass saving** while preserving bending stiffness — a typical lightweighting outcome quoted in automotive literature. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Property Snapshot

| Property / Family | Mild Steel | Al 6061-T6 | Mg AZ91D | Ti Ti-6Al-4V | CFRP (UD) | GFRP (UD) | PP | Al₂O₃ |
|-------------------|-----------|------------|----------|---------------|-----------|-----------|-----|-------|
| $\rho$ [g/cm³] | **7.85** | **2.70** | **1.81** | **4.43** | **1.55** | **2.00** | **0.91** | **3.95** |
| $E$ [GPa] | 210 | 69 | 45 | 114 | 135 | 38 | 1.3 | 380 |
| $\sigma_y$ [MPa] | 350 | 276 | 230 | 880 | 1500 | 900 | 30 | — |
| $\sigma_y / \rho$ [kN·m/kg] | 45 | 102 | 127 | 199 | 968 | 450 | 33 | — |
| $E / \rho$ [MN·m/kg] | 26.8 | 25.6 | 24.9 | 25.7 | 87.1 | 19.0 | 1.43 | 96.2 |

**Reading the matrix:** CFRP dominates **specific strength** ($968$ kN·m/kg) — ideal for body-in-white. Magnesium dominates among metals for **specific stiffness among light alloys**. Steel remains competitive only on cost grounds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    C1b --> C1b3[Phenolic - Brake Pads]

    C1c --> C1c2[NBR - Hoses]
    D1 --> D1a[Oxides]
    D1 --> D1b[Non Oxides]

    D1a --> D1a2[ZrO2 - Oxygen Sensor]
    D1b --> D1b1[SiC - High Temp]
    D1b --> D1b2[Si3N4 - Bearings]
    E1 --> E1a[PMC]
    E1 --> E1b[MMC]
    E1 --> E1c[CMC]
    E1a --> E1a1[CFRP - Body Panels]

    E1b --> E1b1[Al SiC - Brake Drums]

```

## 4.2 Ashby Material Selection Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 - Translate Design Need] --> stepB[Step 2 - Identify Constraints]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 - Identify Objectives]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 - Derive Material Index]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 - Screen Materials on Chart]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 - Rank by Index Value]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 - Apply Secondary Criteria]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 - Final Material Choice]
    stepH --> stepI[Document Decision and Validate]
    stepB --> note1[Constraints: T max, KIC min, Cost max]
    stepD --> note2[Index for tensile tie: sigma y over rho]
    stepG --> note3[Manufacturability, Recycling, Sustainability]
```

## 4.3 Automotive Component to Material Family Mapping (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Engine Block] --> M1[Cast Iron or Al Si Alloy]
    C2[Pistons] --> M2[Al Si MMC]
    C3[Connecting Rods] --> M3[Forged Steel or Ti Alloy]
    C4[Body in White] --> M4[Mild Steel or AHSS or CFRP]
    C5[Chassis Frame] --> M5[HSLA Steel or Al Extrusions]
    C6[Leaf Spring] --> M6[GFRP Composite]
    C7[Bumpers] --> M7[PP or PUR]
    C8[Fuel Tank] --> M8[HDPE]
    C9[Brake Disc] --> M9[Cast Iron or MMC or CMC]
    C10[Spark Plug Insulator] --> M10[Alumina Ceramic]
    C11[Drive Shaft] --> M11[CFRP Composite]
    C12[Intake Manifold] --> M12[PA6 GF Reinforced]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Selection Criteria Weighting Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Q1 Is Mass the Driver?} -->|Yes| q2{Q2 Bending or Tension?}
    q1 -->|No Cost is Driver| q3{Use Cost weighted Index}
    q2 -->|Tension| idx1[Index M2 equals sigma y over rho]
    q2 -->|Bending| idx2[Index M3 equals E over rho cubed]
    q2 -->|Plate buckling| idx3[Index M5 equals E to half over rho]
    q3 --> idx4[Use M8 equals E over rho times Cm]
    idx1 --> final[Plot on Ashby Chart - Choose Top Family]
    idx2 --> final
    idx3 --> final
    idx4 --> final
    final --> verify[Prototype and Validate]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]
**"Differentiate between ferrous and non-ferrous metals with two suitable automotive applications of each."**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Ferrous metals** contain iron as the principal element. They are magnetic, have high stiffness (E ≈ 200 GPa) and relatively high density (ρ ≈ 7.8 g/cm³). Examples used in vehicles: mild steel for body panels (≈ 0.7 mm gauge), HSLA steel for chassis rails, cast iron for engine blocks.
>
> **Non-ferrous metals** contain little or no iron; they are lighter, corrosion-resistant, and non-magnetic (with exceptions like Ni). Examples: Aluminium 6061 for engine cylinder heads and body-in-white closure panels (ρ ≈ 2.7 g/cm³), Magnesium AZ91D for instrument-panel cross-car beams (ρ ≈ 1.8 g/cm³).
>
> **Key distinction:** Ferrous = iron-based, magnetic, dense; Non-ferrous = lightweight and corrosion-resistant.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Define specific strength and state its importance in automotive material selection."**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific strength** is defined as the **ratio of strength to density**, expressed mathematically as:
>
> $$\text{Specific Strength} \; M_2 = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad [\text{kN·m/kg}]$$
>
> where $\sigma_y$ is the yield strength and $\rho$ is the density. It quantifies the load-carrying capacity per unit mass of a material. In automotive engineering, specific strength is the **primary material index for lightweight structural components** such as body-in-white panels, chassis rails, and suspension links. Higher specific strength enables **mass reduction without sacrificing load-bearing capacity**, directly improving fuel efficiency, reducing emissions, and enhancing vehicle performance — critical for meeting CAFE / BS-VI norms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 1, Modified]

**(a)** *With neat schematics, classify automotive materials into four major families and give three examples from each family along with a typical automotive component in which each is used.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the Ashby methodology for material selection. Derive the material index for a lightweight cylindrical pressure vessel of diameter $D$, internal pressure $p$, and length $L$. Assume wall thickness $t \ll D$.* **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Detailed Model Solutions

#### (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Classification Table:**

The four families of automotive materials are metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites. Each family has distinctive bonding and microstructure that dictate its engineering behaviour.

| Family | Bonding | Three Examples | Typical Component |
|--------|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Metals** | Metallic (delocalized electron sea) | Mild steel, Aluminium 6061, Magnesium AZ91 | Chassis rails, engine block, steering column |
| **Polymers** | Covalent (chain macromolecules) | PP, PA6, ABS | Bumper fascia, under-hood manifold, interior trim |
| **Ceramics** | Ionic / covalent (mixed) | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiC | Spark plug insulator, O₂ sensor, brake disc coating |
| **Composites** | Mixed (matrix + reinforcement) | CFRP, GFRP, Al-SiC | Roof panel, leaf spring, brake drum |

**Step 2 — Linking family to function:**

- Metals — chosen for *strength, ductility, manufacturability*.
- Polymers — chosen for *low density, corrosion resistance, mouldability*.
- Ceramics — chosen for *high-temperature stability, hardness, wear resistance*.
- Composites — chosen for *anisotropic tailoring, best specific properties, mass-critical applications*.

**Step 3 — Schematic reference:**

Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for the visual taxonomy.

#### (b) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Ashby Methodology (explanation):**

The Ashby method systematically converts a design requirement into a numerical ranking of materials:

1. **Translate** the design need (e.g., lightest pressure vessel) into an equation involving material properties.
2. **Identify constraints** (e.g., $p_{max}$, $T_{max}$, $K_{IC,min}$, $C_{m,max}$).
3. **Identify the objective** — here, minimize mass $m$.
4. **Derive the material index** that, when maximized, minimizes the objective.
5. **Screen** all candidate materials using the constraints on Ashby property charts.
6. **Rank** survivors by the material index.
7. **Apply secondary criteria** (manufacturability, recyclability, cost).

**Step 2 — Derivation of material index for a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel:**

Hoop stress in a thin cylinder:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \, D}{2 \, t}$$

Yield constraint (failure by yielding):

$$\sigma_h \le \sigma_y \implies t \ge \frac{p \, D}{2 \, \sigma_y}$$

Volume of the cylindrical wall (closed cylinder, approximated as the cylindrical shell — neglecting end caps for an initial index derivation):

$$V = \pi D t L$$

Substituting the minimum thickness:

$$V = \pi D L \cdot \frac{p D}{2 \sigma_y} = \frac{\pi p D^2 L}{2 \sigma_y}$$

Mass:

$$m = \rho V = \frac{\pi p D^2 L \rho}{2 \sigma_y}$$

Since $p$, $D$, $L$ are fixed by the design:

$$m \propto \frac{\rho}{\sigma_y}$$

Therefore the **material index for a thin-walled pressure vessel** is:

$$M_{\text{PV}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} \quad \text{(same as the tensile tie)}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:**

A pressure vessel optimized for minimum mass uses the same index as a tensile member because the dominant load is membrane tension in the hoop direction. Materials such as **CFRP (M ≈ 968 kN·m/kg)**, **maraging steel (M ≈ 250 kN·m/kg)**, and **Ti-6Al-4V (M ≈ 199 kN·m/kg)** dominate the ranking. The Ashby chart of $\sigma_y$ vs $\rho$ (log-log axes) visually identifies these as the top-left region of the chart (high strength, low density). $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 1, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** *Compare metals and composites for automotive structural applications with respect to (i) specific strength, (ii) specific modulus, (iii) manufacturing cost, (iv) recyclability, and (v) crashworthiness. Mention two specific automotive components where composites have replaced metals.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A drive-shaft of length $L = 1.5$ m must transmit a torque $T = 800$ N·m at a maximum allowable twist angle $\theta = 3°$. Currently it is made of steel ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $G = 80$ GPa). If you redesign it in CFRP ($\rho = 1600$ kg/m³, $G = 50$ GPa), by what percentage can the mass be reduced while keeping the same torsional rigidity $GJ/L$?* **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Detailed Model Solutions

#### (a) Model Solution

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Metals (Steel / Al) | Composites (CFRP / GFRP) | Winner |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------------|--------|
| (i) Specific strength $\sigma_y/\rho$ | 45 – 200 kN·m/kg | 450 – 968 kN·m/kg | **Composite** |
| (ii) Specific modulus $E/\rho$ | 25 – 27 MN·m/kg | 19 – 87 MN·m/kg | **Composite (CFRP)** |
| (iii) Manufacturing cost | Low (established supply chain) | High (long cure, autoclave) | **Metal** |
| (iv) Recyclability | High (re-melt routes mature) | Difficult (fibre/matrix separation) | **Metal** |
| (v) Crashworthiness | High (ductile energy absorption) | Brittle delamination; energy absorbed through fracture | **Metal (energy absorption)** |

**Two components where composites have replaced metals:**

1. **Drive shaft (CFRP):** BMW, Toyota, and many high-end vehicles use CFRP drive shafts because of higher specific stiffness and better NVH (no metal-on-metal rattle). Allows elimination of the centre bearing.
2. **Leaf spring (GFRP):** Replaces multi-leaf steel springs in commercial trucks (e.g., Volvo, Mahindra); ≈ 60–70 % mass saving and improved fatigue life.

**Conclusion:** Composites win on mass-critical metrics; metals retain advantages in cost, recyclability, and ductile crash energy absorption. Material choice is therefore component-specific.

#### (b) Model Solution

**Given:**
- Length: $L = 1.5$ m
- Torque: $T = 800$ N·m
- Allowable twist: $\theta = 3° = \pi/60 \approx 0.05236$ rad
- Steel: $\rho_s = 7850$ kg/m³, $G_s = 80$ GPa
- CFRP: $\rho_c = 1600$ kg/m³, $G_c = 50$ GPa

**Step 1 — Torsional rigidity and the design equation.**

The twist of a shaft under torque is:

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$$

Torsional rigidity: $GJ/L$. For the *same* rigidity using the same diameter:

$$\frac{G_s J}{L} = \frac{G_c J_{\text{new}}}{L} \implies J_{\text{new}} = J \cdot \frac{G_s}{G_c}$$

So the CFRP shaft must have a **larger polar moment of area** (larger diameter) to compensate for its lower $G$.

**Step 2 — Mass of the shaft (assuming solid circular cross-section).**

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

Volume:

$$V = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L$$

Mass:

$$m = \rho V = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2 L}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Express the mass in terms of $J$ (or equivalently $d^2$).**

From $J = \pi d^4 / 32$:

$$d^4 = \frac{32 J}{\pi} \implies d^2 = \sqrt{\frac{32 J}{\pi}}$$

Therefore:

$$m = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi L}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{32 J}{\pi}} = \rho \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi J}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass ratio.**

Since $L$ is the same for both shafts:

$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_s} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{J_c}{J_s}}$$

From rigidity constraint: $J_c / J_s = G_s / G_c = 80/50 = 1.6$.

So:

$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} = \frac{1600}{7850} \cdot \sqrt{1.6}$$

Compute each factor:

$$\frac{1600}{7850} \approx 0.2038$$

$$\sqrt{1.6} \approx 1.2649$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} \approx 0.2038 \times 1.2649 \approx 0.2578$$

**Step 5 — Percentage mass reduction.**

$$\% \text{ saving} = \left(1 - 0.2578\right) \times 100\% \approx 74.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** A CFRP drive shaft can deliver the **same torsional rigidity** as a steel shaft at roughly **one-quarter the mass** — a major reason CFRP is chosen for premium and performance vehicles despite higher cost. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "material index" with "property ratio" without derivation.** Examiners award the derivation step the full 3–4 marks; merely stating $\sigma/\rho$ without showing how it arises from $m = \rho V$ will cost those marks.
> 2. **Always specify the exponent** (e.g., $E^{1/3}$ vs $E^{1/2}$) when writing beam indices — the wrong exponent is a **frequent 2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Units matter:** Always write specific strength as $\text{kN·m/kg}$ (numerically the same as $\text{kPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$) and not $\text{MPa/g/cm}^3$ alone without conversion.
> 4. **Crashworthiness ≠ strength alone.** Ductile metals absorb energy through plastic deformation; CFRP absorbs energy through fracture/delamination. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Four material families** — metals, polymers, ceramics, composites — each defined by bonding type and microstructure.
- [x] **Automotive metals** — dominate ≈ 70–75 % of vehicle mass; include ferrous (steel, cast iron) and non-ferrous (Al, Mg, Ti).
- [x] **Automotive polymers** — PP, PA6, ABS, HDPE for moulded parts; thermosets for adhesives and matrix; elastomers for seals.
- [x] **Automotive ceramics** — high-temp, wear-resistant, brittle; used in spark plugs, O₂ sensors, brake discs.
- [x] **Automotive composites** — CFRP, GFRP, MMC, CMC; tailorable anisotropic properties.
- [x] **Five pillars of selection** — mechanical, physical, chemical, manufacturability, economic/sustainability.
- [x] **Ashby methodology** — translation → constraints → objective → material index → screening → ranking → secondary criteria.
- [x] **Material index for tensile member:** $M_2 = \sigma_y / \rho$ (specific strength).
- [x] **Material index for stiff beam (free shape):** $M_3 = E^{1/3} / \rho$.
- [x] **Material index for stiff beam (square section):** $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$.
- [x] **Material index for buckling column:** $M_5 = E^{1/2} / \rho$.
- [x] **Material index for elastic spring:** $M_7 = \sigma_y^2 / (E\rho)$.
- [x] **Specific strength ranking:** CFRP > Ti > Maraging steel > Al > Mg > Mild steel > PP.
- [x] **Composites replace metals in drive shafts (CFRP), leaf springs (GFRP), body panels (CFRP)** — mass savings 50–75 %.
- [x] **MCDA** integrates Ashby indices with cost and sustainability to meet CAFE / BS-VI targets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ferrous and non-ferrous alloys: applications of steel, aluminium, magnesium, and titanium alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ferrous and non-ferrous alloys: applications of steel, aluminium, magnesium, and titanium alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Alloys** are engineered metallic materials — comprising iron-based (ferrous) and non-iron-based (non-ferrous) systems — that are specifically formulated to meet the structural, thermal, fatigue, corrosion, and weight-reduction demands of modern vehicles. The strategic selection of these alloys is governed by the **specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$), **specific stiffness** ($E/\rho$), manufacturability, recyclability, and life-cycle cost.

The four dominant alloy families in automotive manufacturing are:

| Family | Base Metal | Typical Density ($\rho$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel** (Ferrous) | Iron (Fe) | $\approx 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |
| **Aluminium** (Non-ferrous) | Aluminium (Al) | $\approx 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |
| **Magnesium** (Non-ferrous) | Magnesium (Mg) | $\approx 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |
| **Titanium** (Non-ferrous) | Titanium (Ti) | $\approx 4.51 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Skeletal System" Analogy**
> Think of a car the way a doctor views a human body. **Steel** is like the **skeleton** — strong, heavy, and the structural backbone. **Aluminium** is like the **muscle** — strong yet light, doing most of the dynamic work. **Magnesium** is the **ligaments and tendons** — ultra-light parts in non-critical zones. **Titanium** is the **specialized prosthetics** — premium, biocompatible-grade components used where temperatures or stresses are extreme.
> Every kilogram shaved from a vehicle improves **fuel efficiency** by roughly **0.4–0.7 km/litre** and reduces CO₂ emissions. This is the *why* behind the modern multi-material design philosophy.

### 1.3 Why These Four Families?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on these four because they represent a **strategic trade-off pyramid**:
> 1. **Steel** → Lowest cost, highest formability, dominates volume production.
> 2. **Aluminium** → Best balance of weight saving vs. cost.
> 3. **Magnesium** → Lightest structural metal on Earth.
> 4. **Titanium** → Highest strength-to-weight ratio with corrosion immunity.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Density of water = $\rho_{water}$ = **$1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$**
- Density of steel = **$7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$** (baseline reference)
- Young's Modulus of Steel = **$210 \text{ GPa}$**
- Gravitational acceleration: $g$ = **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- 1 **MPa** = $10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$ = $1 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- 1 **GPa** = $10^3 \text{ MPa}$

---

### 1.5 Syllabus Highlight Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO1 – K2/K3 Level):**
> After studying this module, the student must be able to **classify, compare, and select** ferrous and non-ferrous alloys for specific automotive subsystems based on mechanical, thermal, and economic criteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steel Alloys — The Backbone of Automotive Structures

#### 2.1.1 Mild / Low-Carbon Steel (LC Steel)
- Carbon content: **0.05–0.25 % C**
- Used for: body panels (door, hood, fender), structural cross-members.
- Why: Excellent formability, weldability, low cost (₹60–90/kg).
- Limitations: Low strength → thicker gauge needed → weight penalty.

#### 2.1.2 HSLA — High Strength Low Alloy Steel
- Microalloyed with **Nb, V, Ti** (each $\leq 0.15\%$) to refine grain and form carbides/nitrides.
- Yield strength: **$340 - 550 \text{ MPa}$**.
- Used in: chassis frames, reinforcements, suspension arms.
- Advantage: 20–30% weight reduction over mild steel with similar thickness.

#### 2.1.3 AHSS — Advanced High Strength Steels (Modern Crash-Safety Steels)
AHSS grades most relevant to KTU automobile syllabus:

| Grade | Microstructure | Tensile Strength | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DP (Dual Phase)** | Ferrite + Martensite | $600 - 1000 \text{ MPa}$ | B-pillars, bumpers |
| **TRIP (Transformation Induced Plasticity)** | Ferrite + Bainite + Retained Austenite | $600 - 1000 \text{ MPa}$ | Side-impact beams |
| **CP (Complex Phase)** | Multi-phase | $800 - 1200 \text{ MPa}$ | Roof rails |
| **MS (Martensitic)** | Fully martensitic | $900 - 1700 \text{ MPa}$ | Door intrusion beams |
| **TWIP (Twinning Induced Plasticity)** | Austenitic with twins | $1000 - 1500 \text{ MPa}$ | Premium structural parts |

#### 2.1.4 Martensitic Stainless Steel (e.g., AISI 420)
- Used in: **cutlery-grade automotive trims, fuel injector needles**.
- 11.5–14% Cr, 0.15–0.40% C.

#### 2.1.5 Why AHSS Dominates Crash Safety
> [!NOTE]
> During a crash, **TRIP steel** transforms retained austenite into hard martensite under stress, *absorbing energy* while maintaining ductility. This is the **"transformation-induced plasticity"** phenomenon that gives modern cars their 5-star NCAP ratings.

---

### 2.2 Aluminium Alloys — The Lightweight Workhorse

#### 2.2.1 Wrought Aluminium Series

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Strengthening Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al ($\geq 99\%$) | Cold work | Electrical busbars |
| **2xxx** | Cu | Precipitation (heat treatable) | 2014, 2024 — aircraft skin |
| **3xxx** | Mn | Solid solution | 3003 — heat exchangers |
| **5xxx** | Mg | Solid solution + work hardening | 5182, 5754 — body panels |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | Precipitation (heat treatable) | 6061, 6082 — chassis, frames |
| **7xxx** | Zn + Mg | Precipitation (heat treatable) | 7075 — high-stress components |

#### 2.2.2 Cast Aluminium Series

- **A356 (Al–7Si–0.3Mg)** — Most used automotive cast alloy.
  - Application: **engine blocks, cylinder heads, wheels**.
  - T6 treatment: $\sigma_y \approx 200 \text{ MPa}$.
- **A380 (Al–8.5Si–3.5Cu)** — Die-casting alloy.
  - Application: **transmission housings, brackets**.

#### 2.2.3 Why Aluminium for Engine Blocks?
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Aluminium engine blocks (replacing cast iron) reduce engine weight by **40–55%**, improve heat dissipation (thermal conductivity of Al $\approx 167 \text{ W/m·K}$ vs. Fe $\approx 50 \text{ W/m·K}$), and enable shorter warm-up cycles — lowering HC/CO emissions.

#### 2.2.4 Key Limitations of Aluminium
- **Lower Young's Modulus** ($E_{Al} = 70 \text{ GPa}$) → 1/3 that of steel → needs **stiffer cross-sections** for the same rigidity.
- **Welding difficulty** (oxide layer Al₂O₃) — needs MIG/TIG with AC.
- **Higher cost per kg** ($\approx 3{-}4\times$ steel).

---

### 2.3 Magnesium Alloys — The Lightest Structural Metals

#### 2.3.1 Designation System (ASTM B275)

Format: **A–B–C**
- A = principal alloying element (e.g., A = Al, Z = Zn, M = Mn).
- B = nominal % of that element.
- C = distinguishing letter (e.g., A, B, C for variants).

Examples:
- **AZ31** (3% Al, 1% Zn) — wrought sheets for instrument panels.
- **AZ91D** (9% Al, 1% Zn) — die-cast housings, steering wheel cores.
- **AM60** (6% Al, 0.3% Mn) — seat frames (high ductility).

#### 2.3.2 Why Magnesium Is Tricky

> [!WARNING]
> - **Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP)** crystal structure → poor cold formability at room temperature.
> - Highly **flammable** during machining (chips ignite above $450°\text{C}$).
> - Prone to **galvanic corrosion** when coupled with steel → needs coatings or isolation.
> - Yield strength is **low to moderate** ($130–230 \text{ MPa}$) — not used in primary crash structures.

#### 2.3.3 Engineering Strategy
To overcome HCP limitations:
1. **Thixomolding** (semi-solid injection molding).
2. **Hot stamping** ($200°{-}300°\text{C}$).
3. Use of **rare-earth (RE) additions** to refine microstructure.

---

### 2.4 Titanium Alloys — The High-Performance Specialist

#### 2.4.1 Crystal Structure Background
- **$\alpha$-phase (HCP)** — stable at room temperature, weldable, good creep resistance.
- **$\beta$-phase (BCC)** — ductile, hardenable, formable.

#### 2.4.2 Classification

| Class | Microstructure | Examples | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **α-alloys** | Single phase HCP | Ti-3Al-2.5V | Weldable, moderate strength |
| **β-alloys** | Single phase BCC | Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al | High strength, deep hardenability |
| **α+β alloys** | Duplex | **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** | Best balance — *most used* |

#### 2.4.3 Ti-6Al-4V — The Workhorse of Automotive Titanium
- Composition: 6% Al, 4% V, balance Ti.
- $\sigma_y \approx 880 \text{ MPa}$, $UTS \approx 950 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
- Used in:
  - **Connecting rods** (e.g., Ferrari, Porsche).
  - **Exhaust valves** and **valve springs**.
  - **Suspension springs** (VW Lupo — $40\%$ weight reduction over steel springs).
  - **Turbocharger wheels** (operating $> 800°\text{C}$).

#### 2.4.4 Why Titanium Costs a Fortune
> [!NOTE]
> Titanium is expensive because of the **Kroll process** — converting TiO₂ ore to TiCl₄ via carbochlorination, then reducing with magnesium. The cost is roughly **\$30–100/kg**, vs. steel at **\$0.5–1/kg**. Hence titanium is reserved for **high-end and motorsport vehicles**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three "KTU-favourite" equations for this topic:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Strength** | $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\text{kN·m/kg}$ or $\text{MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Used to compare materials for weight-critical design |
| 2 | **Specific Stiffness** | $SSt = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $\text{GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Used to compare rigidity per unit mass |
| 3 | **% Weight Reduction** | $\%\Delta W = \dfrac{\rho_{steel} - \rho_{alloy}}{\rho_{steel}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Always relative to steel as baseline |

> **Constraint:** For any weight-critical design, **higher SS and SSt are better**. KTU examiners often compare two materials — e.g., "justify the use of Al over steel for a piston" — by computing SS for both and arguing the trade-off.

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When comparing $E$ values in a table, write them as $E_{Al} = 70 \text{ GPa}$, not $E = \vert 70 \vert \text{ GPa}$, to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In a real automotive OEM (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Mahindra), the materials engineer performs a **multi-criteria decision matrix** for each subsystem. The trade-offs are:
> - **Cost** (₹/kg of finished part).
> - **Specific strength** (efficiency).
> - **Manufacturability** (cast vs. wrought vs. sheet).
> - **Recyclability** (steel: 100% infinitely recyclable; Al: ~95% recovery).
> - **Crash performance** (energy absorption per unit mass).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Specific Strength Comparison (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 style):**
> Compare the specific strength of **AISI 1020 steel**, **7075-T6 aluminium**, and **Ti-6Al-4V** alloy. Given:
> - Steel: $\sigma_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> - 7075-T6: $\sigma_y = 503 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 2.81 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> - Ti-6Al-4V: $\sigma_y = 880 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> Identify the best material for a **connecting rod** of a high-performance engine.

#### Step 1 — Recall the Specific Strength Formula

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute for Steel (AISI 1020)

$$SS_{steel} = \frac{350 \text{ MPa}}{7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

To keep the units tidy, convert MPa = $\text{N/mm}^2$ and g/cm³ coherently. We will express the result in **$\text{kN·m/kg}$**:
- $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$SS_{steel} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2}{7850 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 44.59 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute for 7075-T6 Aluminium

$$SS_{Al} = \frac{503 \text{ MPa}}{2.81 \text{ g/cm}^3} = \frac{503 \times 10^3}{2810} = 178.99 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute for Ti-6Al-4V

$$SS_{Ti} = \frac{880 \text{ MPa}}{4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3} = \frac{880 \times 10^3}{4430} = 198.65 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

#### Step 5 — Tabulate and Interpret

| Material | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AISI 1020 Steel | 350 | 7.85 | 44.59 | 3 |
| 7075-T6 Al | 503 | 2.81 | 178.99 | 2 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 880 | 4.43 | **198.65** | **1** |

#### Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ti-6Al-4V** has the highest specific strength and is therefore the **optimal material** for the connecting rod, *provided* cost constraints are relaxed (motorsport/premium segment).
> For mass-market passenger cars, **7075-T6** offers $\sim 4\times$ the specific strength of steel at a fraction of titanium's cost — the practical choice.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weight Reduction Calculation (KTU Typical)

> **Problem:**
> A car's engine block currently weighs 220 kg in cast iron ($\rho = 7.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$). If redesigned in aluminium A356 ($\rho = 2.68 \text{ g/cm}^3$) with the same volume, compute the weight saving in **kg** and **%**.

#### Step 1 — Recall the Mass Relation

$$m = \rho \times V$$

Since volume is constant, mass is proportional to density. So:

$$\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{Fe}} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{Fe}}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Aluminium Mass

$$m_{Al} = 220 \times \frac{2.68}{7.20} = 220 \times 0.3722 = 81.89 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Absolute Saving

$$\Delta m = 220 - 81.89 = 138.11 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Percentage Weight Reduction

$$\%\Delta W = \frac{7.20 - 2.68}{7.20} \times 100 = \frac{4.52}{7.20} \times 100$$

$$\%\Delta W = 0.6278 \times 100 = 62.78\%$$

#### Step 5 — Final Answer and Interpretation

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Original mass (Cast Iron) | 220 kg |
| New mass (A356 Al) | 81.89 kg |
| Absolute saving | 138.11 kg |
| **% Weight reduction** | **62.78%** |

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, because aluminium has lower stiffness ($E_{Al} = 70$ GPa vs $E_{Fe} = 170$ GPa), the redesign may require ribbing or thicker walls, slightly reducing the achievable saving to ~40–50% in real components.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Material Selection Snapshot

> The following matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU 2024 exam answers on this topic.

| Property | Mild Steel | HSLA | Al 6061-T6 | A356 (Cast) | Mg AZ91D | Ti-6Al-4V |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.85 | 7.85 | 2.70 | 2.68 | 1.74 | 4.43 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 210 | 210 | 70 | 72 | 45 | 114 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 250 | 400 | 240 | 186 | 160 | 880 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 400 | 550 | 290 | 230 | 240 | 950 |
| Specific Strength | 31.8 | 50.9 | 88.9 | 69.4 | 92.0 | 198.7 |
| Specific Stiffness | 26.8 | 26.8 | 25.9 | 26.9 | 25.9 | 25.7 |
| Melting Point (°C) | 1500 | 1500 | 582 | 557 | 595 | 1660 |
| Relative Cost (per kg) | 1× | 1.2× | 3.5× | 3× | 4× | 50–100× |
| Typical Auto Part | Body panel | Chassis | Wheels | Engine block | Steering core | Con rod |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Excellent | Very Good | Very Good | Good | Limited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading of the Matrix:**
> - **Magnesium** has the highest specific strength among *affordable* alloys but the lowest stiffness.
> - **Aluminium** is the "sweet spot" — best balance of cost, weight, and processability.
> - **Titanium** is unbeatable for high-temperature + high-stress parts but at a 50–100× cost.
> - **Steel** wins on cost, stiffness, and recyclability — but loses on weight.

---

### 3.4 Decision Flow for an Examiner's "Justify the Choice" Question

> **Step A** → Identify the **loading condition** (static, fatigue, impact, thermal).
> **Step B** → Identify the **mass constraint** (motorsport vs. mass-market).
> **Step C** → Compute **specific strength** of candidates.
> **Step D** → Apply **cost filter**.
> **Step E** → Justify using Ashby's method (performance index $M = \sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$, etc.).

This five-step reasoning is the **template** KTU examiners expect for 14-mark Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Automotive Alloy Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D6 --> E1
    D7 --> E1
    D8 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Validate Crash / NVH / Fatigue]
    F1 --> G1[Final Material Selected]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define **specific strength** and **specific stiffness**. Why are these properties important in selecting automotive materials?

**Model Answer:**

> Specific strength is defined as the yield (or ultimate tensile) strength of a material divided by its density:
> $$SS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$
> It has units of $\text{kN·m/kg}$ (or $\text{MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$).
>
> Specific stiffness is the elastic modulus divided by density:
> $$SSt = \frac{E}{\rho}$$
>
> These properties are crucial in automotive design because they quantify **load-bearing capacity per unit weight**. A material with high specific strength allows the designer to reduce component mass (improving fuel efficiency, emissions, and handling) while still meeting structural performance targets. The same logic applies to specific stiffness for rigidity-dominated parts like panels and frames.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** List **three** advantages and **two** disadvantages of using magnesium alloys in automobiles.

**Model Answer:**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. Lowest density among structural metals ($\rho \approx 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$) — gives the highest weight savings.
> 2. Excellent damping capacity — reduces NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
> 3. Good castability (e.g., in die-casting of complex geometries) and high specific strength.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. Hexagonal close-packed structure leads to poor cold formability.
> 2. Highly susceptible to galvanic corrosion when in contact with steel/aluminium — needs isolation/coatings; also chips are flammable during machining.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

> **(a)** Compare the **classification, microstructure, and applications** of the following steel families used in automotive body-in-white (BIW): HSLA, DP, TRIP, CP, and Martensitic (MS) steels. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A structural cross-member in a car has a design requirement of $550 \text{ MPa}$ yield strength. Compare three candidate materials — **HSLA steel ($\sigma_y = 450$ MPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³)**, **Al 7075-T6 ($\sigma_y = 503$ MPa, $\rho = 2.81$ g/cm³)**, and **Ti-6Al-4V ($\sigma_y = 880$ MPa, $\rho = 4.43$ g/cm³)** — using the **specific strength** criterion. Recommend the best material considering both performance and economic feasibility. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

> | Steel | Microstructure | Key Feature | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | Application |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | HSLA | Ferrite + Pearlite + microalloy carbides | Grain refinement by Nb, V, Ti | 340–550 | Chassis, frame rails |
> | DP (Dual Phase) | Soft ferrite matrix + hard martensite islands | High initial work-hardening rate | 500–1000 | B-pillars, dash panels |
> | TRIP | Ferrite + Bainite + Retained Austenite | Austenite → martensite under stress (energy absorbing) | 600–1000 | Side intrusion beams |
> | CP (Complex Phase) | Multi-phase + fine precipitates | Ultra-high strength, good hole expansion | 800–1200 | Roof rails, rockers |
> | MS (Martensitic) | 100% Martensite | Maximum strength, press-hardenable (e.g., 22MnB5) | 900–1700 | Bumper beams, A/B-pillars |
>
> **Conclusion:** AHSS grades (DP, TRIP, CP, MS) form the modern "crash safety pyramid" of a BIW, while HSLA handles the structural chassis.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Check if each material meets the 550 MPa requirement:**
- HSLA: $\sigma_y = 450$ MPa → **FAILS** (below requirement).
- Al 7075-T6: $\sigma_y = 503$ MPa → **FAILS** (below requirement).
- Ti-6Al-4V: $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa → **PASSES** comfortably.

**Step 2 — Compute Specific Strength for the qualifying material:**

$$SS_{Ti} = \frac{880}{4.43} = 198.65 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Decision:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Ti-6Al-4V passes the strength criterion, its cost is **50–100×** that of HSLA. For a mass-market cross-member, the practical solution is to **upgrade HSLA to a higher grade** (e.g., HSLA 550 or a DP 600/800 grade) — meeting strength economically.
> **Recommendation:** For *motorsport/luxury cars*, choose **Ti-6Al-4V**. For *volume production*, upgrade to **HSLA 550 / DP 600**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Explain the **alloy designation system** for aluminium alloys (1xxx to 7xxx). For each major series, give **one typical automotive application** and the **principal strengthening mechanism**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **specific strength** and **weight reduction** calculation, justify why **aluminium A356 ($ \rho = 2.68 \text{ g/cm}^3, \sigma_y = 186 \text{ MPa}$)** has replaced **cast iron ($ \rho = 7.20 \text{ g/cm}^3, \sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$)** as the material for **automotive engine blocks**. Discuss **two limitations** of aluminium that designers must overcome. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

| Series | Principal Element | Strengthening Mechanism | Automotive Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1xxx | Pure Al ($\geq 99\%$) | Cold work | Capacitor foils, busbars |
| 2xxx | Cu | Solid solution + precipitation (heat-treatable) | Aircraft, premium wheels |
| 3xxx | Mn | Solid solution + strain hardening | Heat exchangers, radiators |
| 4xxx | Si | Solid solution | Brazing sheets |
| 5xxx | Mg | Solid solution + strain hardening (non-heat-treatable) | Body panels (5182, 5754) |
| 6xxx | Mg + Si | Mg₂Si precipitation (heat-treatable) | Chassis, extrusions (6061, 6082) |
| 7xxx | Zn + Mg + Cu | Precipitation (heat-treatable, ultra-high strength) | Suspension arms (7075) |

**Conclusion:** The 5xxx and 6xxx series dominate the automotive body and chassis; 2xxx and 7xxx are used selectively where higher strength is needed.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Specific Strength:**

$$SS_{cast\,iron} = \frac{250}{7.20} = 34.72 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

$$SS_{A356} = \frac{186}{2.68} = 69.40 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Weight Reduction for Same Volume:**

$$\%\Delta W = \frac{7.20 - 2.68}{7.20} \times 100 = 62.78\%$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Justification:**

> A356 gives **2× the specific strength** of cast iron and saves **~63% weight** for the same volume. Combined with Al's superior thermal conductivity ($167$ vs. $50 \text{ W/m·K}$), this justifies the switch in engine blocks for fuel economy, emissions, and acceleration.

**Step 4 — Two Limitations & Solutions:**

> 1. **Lower Young's Modulus** ($E_{Al} = 70$ GPa) — designer uses **ribbed structures** and **thicker sections** to maintain stiffness. Net weight saving reduces to 40–50% in practice.
> 2. **High thermal expansion coefficient** ($23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ vs. iron's $12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$) — causes bore distortion. Solution: use **hypereutectic Al-Si alloys (e.g., A390)** or insert **cast-iron cylinder liners** (e.g., Nikasil coatings).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "specific strength" units** — Students often leave $\sigma_y$ in MPa and $\rho$ in g/cm³, producing meaningless "MPa·cm³/g" without cancelling. Always normalise to **kN·m/kg** or use **Ashby charts** properly.
> 2. **Writing "Al alloy" without the temper** — A356-**T6** vs. A356-**F** have very different strengths. Always specify the temper condition.
> 3. **Ignoring cost** — Saying "use titanium" without acknowledging the 50× cost penalty is an automatic 1–2 mark deduction.
> 4. **Wrong crystal structure of Mg** — Writing FCC instead of HCP. Mg is **HCP**, which explains its poor ductility.
> 5. **Forgetting the 5xxx series is non-heat-treatable** — confusing it with 6xxx. This is a KTU favourite trap.
> 6. **Mixing up "TRIP" and "TWIP"** — TRIP = Transformation Induced Plasticity; TWIP = Twinning Induced Plasticity. Different mechanisms, different Mn content.
> 7. **Omitting recycling** in the answer — modern KTU papers emphasize life-cycle analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bullet-Point Mastery Sheet — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Ferrous family** = iron-based; **Non-ferrous** = Al, Mg, Ti, Cu, etc.
- **Steel** dominates by **volume** (~$800–1000$ kg in a typical car).
- **HSLA** adds **Nb, V, Ti** microalloying for grain refinement.
- **AHSS** = DP, TRIP, CP, MS, TWIP — these are the *modern crash safety steels*.
- **22MnB5** boron steel is press-hardened to form **hot-stamped** B-pillars and bumpers ($\sigma_y > 1500$ MPa).
- **Aluminium series mnemonic:** *"1 pure, 2 Cu, 3 Mn, 4 Si, 5 Mg, 6 MgSi, 7 Zn"* — know at least one auto application for each.
- **A356-T6** is the **king of cast aluminium** (engine blocks, wheels).
- **A380** is the **king of die-cast aluminium** (housings, brackets).
- **Magnesium designation = A–B–C** where A is the alloying element code (A = Al, Z = Zn, M = Mn, etc.).
- **AZ91D** = 9% Al, 1% Zn — most common die-cast Mg alloy.
- **Magnesium is HCP** → poor room-temperature formability → needs hot working or thixomolding.
- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** = **α + β titanium**, the *default* titanium alloy; $\sigma_y \approx 880$ MPa.
- **Titanium** used for connecting rods, exhaust valves, springs, turbocharger wheels.
- **Specific strength** $SS = \sigma_y / \rho$ — **key comparison metric**.
- **Specific stiffness** $SSt = E / \rho$ — important for panel rigidity.
- **Weight reduction** (vs. steel) = **$\approx 65\%$ for Mg, $63\%$ for Al, $42\%$ for Ti** by mass.
- **Cost ranking** (low → high): Steel < HSLA < Al < Mg < Ti.
- **Young's Modulus ranking** (high → low): Steel (210) > Ti (114) > Al (70) > Mg (45) **GPa**.
- **Recyclability**: Steel 100% > Al ~95% > Mg ~80% > Ti limited.
- **Modern BIW trend**: Multi-material — steel in crash zones, Al in closures, Mg in IP/console, Ti in high-stress engine parts.
- **Kroll process** = the reason titanium is expensive (TiO₂ → TiCl₄ → reduction with Mg).
- **Famous application facts**: Ford F-150 → aluminium body (2015 onwards); VW Lupo 1.4 TDI → titanium valve springs and connecting rods; BMW i3 → CFRP + Al spaceframe.
- **Always specify the temper** for Al alloys (e.g., 6061-T6, not just 6061).
- **Always cite KTU 2024 COs** (CO1, CO2) in your answer introduction to score **evaluation-points bonus**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Compare steel and aluminium for body panels"*, structure the answer as:
> **(i) Definition** → **(ii) Specific strength table** → **(iii) Cost comparison** → **(iv) Manufacturing method** → **(v) Recycling** → **(vi) Final recommendation.**
> This 6-step structure aligns with KTU's 14-mark Part B marking scheme (≈ 2 marks per step + integration bonus).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers and composites: thermoplastics, thermosetting plastics, elastomers, reinforced plastics, and carbon fibre composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymers and composites: thermoplastics, thermosetting plastics, elastomers, reinforced plastics, and carbon fibre composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymers — The Engineer's Definition

A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight organic material (natural or synthetic) whose structure is built up from the repetitive covalent linking of many small chemical units called **monomers**. The Greek roots *poly* (many) and *meros* (part) literally mean "many parts." The process of joining monomers is called **polymerization**, and the number of repeat units in a single chain is the **degree of polymerization (DP)**.

In the context of **Advanced Automotive Manufacturing Materials (PEAUT745)**, polymers are critical because they offer:
- Low density (~**0.9 – 1.5 g/cm³**) vs. steel (~**7.85 g/cm³**).
- Corrosion resistance in body panels, fuel lines, and under-hood components.
- Design freedom for complex, integrated parts (e.g., dashboard carriers).
- Mass reduction for **fuel efficiency** and **EV range extension**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 1 demands a clear distinction between **thermoplastics**, **thermosetting plastics**, and **elastomers**, followed by an introduction to **reinforced plastics** and **carbon fibre composites** used in modern automotive structures (BIW, CFRP monocoques, CFRP driveshafts).

## 1.2 Composites — The Engineer's Definition

A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, engineered material composed of two or more distinct **phases** — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material with properties superior to those of the individual constituents.

The automotive-relevant composite families covered in this module are:
1. **Reinforced Plastics** — Polymer matrices reinforced with glass, aramid, or carbon fibres.
2. **Carbon Fibre Composites (CFRP)** — Carbon-fibre reinforcement in polymer (often epoxy) matrices.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spaghetti, the Net, and the Rubber Band"

Imagine three different worlds at a birthday party:

- **Thermoplastics = a bowl of cooked spaghetti strands.** Each strand (polymer chain) is independent of the others, only held together by weak intermolecular forces. Heat the bowl → strands slide past one another → you can remould the mass. Cool it → it stiffens again. This is **reversible physical change**.

- **Thermosetting plastics = a hard-boiled egg's white turned into a 3D fishing net.** During curing, the long chains form permanent **covalent cross-links** (the knots of the net). Once the net is formed, heating will only char it — you cannot melt and reuse it. This is **irreversible chemical change**.

- **Elastomers = a slingshot made of tangled, lightly cross-linked rubber bands.** The chains are coiled and only **sparsely cross-linked**, so they can uncoil under load and snap back when released, giving giant elastic stretch (up to **500 – 1000 %** elongation).

- **Reinforced plastics & CFRP = reinforced concrete.** Plain concrete (matrix) is strong in compression but weak in tension. Embed steel rods (reinforcement) and the composite resists both. In CFRP, the "rods" are carbon fibres (extremely strong and stiff), and the "concrete" is an epoxy resin.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Anchors

| Parameter | Typical Value | Automotive Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density of thermoplastic (PP) | **0.90 g/cm³** | Bumpers, interior trim |
| Density of CFRP | **1.55 – 1.60 g/cm³** | Roof, hood, monocoque |
| Tensile strength, T700 carbon fibre | **4.9 GPa** | Driveshafts, leaf springs |
| Modulus, T700 carbon fibre | **230 GPa** | Structural stiffness |
| Glass transition temperature (Tg) of epoxy | **120 – 180 °C** | Under-hood use limit |
| Elongation at break, natural rubber | **500 – 800 %** | Tyres, bushings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Away:** Every automotive engineer must memorise that **thermoplastics are recyclable and reformable**, while **thermosets are not** (they degrade on reheating). This single fact dictates end-of-life vehicle (ELV) recycling strategies under European Directive 2000/53/EC and India's AIS-010.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — A Three-Tier Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 1 follows a clear hierarchical structure:

**Tier 1 — Polymers (homogeneous materials)**
- **Thermoplastics** → linear/branched chains, no cross-links, *thermally reversible*.
- **Thermosetting plastics (thermosets)** → heavily cross-linked 3D network, *thermally irreversible*.
- **Elastomers** → lightly cross-linked, *elastically deformable*, above Tg at room temperature.

**Tier 2 — Polymer-matrix composites (heterogeneous)**
- **Reinforced plastics** → polymer matrix + glass/aramid/carbon fibres.
- **Carbon fibre composites (CFRP)** → specifically carbon-fibre-reinforced polymers.

## 2.2 Thermoplastics — Operational Logic

**Why thermoplastics dominate automotive volume:**
- Reprocessable → supports circular economy mandates.
- High impact resistance (especially polyamides, polycarbonates).
- Weldable (ultrasonic, hot-plate, vibration).

**How molecular architecture drives behaviour:**
- **Linear chains (HDPE, PP):** efficient packing → semi-crystalline → higher density & strength.
- **Branched chains (LDPE):** inefficient packing → amorphous regions → lower density, more flexible.
- **Bulky side groups (PS, ABS):** hinder crystallisation → glassy amorphous → transparent/rigid.

**Common automotive thermoplastics:**

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Polypropylene | PP | Bumpers, dashboards, battery housings |
| Polyamide (Nylon 6,6) | PA66 | Air-intake manifolds, radiator tanks |
| Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene | ABS | Instrument panels, interior trim |
| Polycarbonate | PC | Headlamp lenses, sunroofs |
| Polyethylene terephthalate | PET | Under-bonnet cable insulation |
| Polyoxymethylene (Acetal) | POM | Gears, fuel-system components |

## 2.3 Thermosetting Plastics — Operational Logic

**Why thermosets are used in structural composites:**
- High cross-link density → high modulus, high glass-transition temperature.
- Low creep under sustained load → ideal for chassis and suspension members.
- Excellent chemical and thermal resistance.

**How curing drives irreversibility:**
- Reaction begins with a low-viscosity **resin** (e.g., epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, phenolic).
- **Hardener / curing agent** (e.g., diamine, anhydride) triggers an **exothermic cross-linking** reaction.
- A 3D covalent network forms → the material is "set" forever.

**Common automotive thermosets:**

| Resin System | Curing Mechanism | Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|
| Epoxy | Addition with amine/anhydride hardener | CFRP body panels, driveshafts |
| Polyester (unsaturated) | Free-radical with peroxide | SMC body panels, headlamp reflectors |
| Vinyl ester | Free-radical + epoxy backbone | Leaf springs, suspension links |
| Phenolic | Condensation | Brake pads, friction materials |
| Polyurethane (thermoset grade) | Addition with polyol | Foam seats, suspension bushings |

## 2.4 Elastomers — Operational Logic

**Why elastomers behave "rubbery":**
- The polymer chains must be **above their Tg at room temperature** so segmental motion is active.
- **Sparse cross-links (vulcanisation)** — usually 1 in 50 – 100 monomer units — provide a "memory" that snaps the chain back after deformation but allow uncoiling under load.

**How vulcanisation works (a key KTU 2024 exam point):**
- Natural rubber (polyisoprene) is mixed with **sulphur (~1 – 4 %)** and heated.
- Sulphur bridges form **covalent cross-links** between chains, converting sticky raw rubber into elastic, durable vulcanised rubber.
- Charles Goodyear discovered this in 1839.

**Automotive elastomers:**

| Elastomer | Abbreviation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber | NR | Tyres, engine mounts |
| Styrene-Butadiene Rubber | SBR | Tyre treads |
| Ethylene-Propylene-Diene Monomer | EPDM | Weather-strips, hoses |
| Nitrile Rubber | NBR | Fuel hoses, O-rings |
| Silicone Rubber | VMQ | Gaskets, high-temp seals |
| Thermoplastic Vulcanisate | TPV | Bumpers, dust covers |

## 2.5 Reinforced Plastics & Carbon Fibre Composites — Operational Logic

**Why fibre reinforcement works (the "load-transfer" principle):**
- A fibre (e.g., carbon, glass) carries the majority of tensile load because it has a much higher strength and modulus than the matrix.
- The matrix **transfers stress** into the fibre through the **fibre–matrix interface**.
- The interface is engineered via **sizing** (a chemical coating on the fibre).

**How composite architecture affects properties:**

| Fibre Form | Fibre Volume Fraction (Vf) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Short, discontinuous | 10 – 20 % | Injection-moulded parts |
| Long, discontinuous | 20 – 35 % | Sheet moulding compound (SMC) |
| Continuous unidirectional | 50 – 70 % | Driveshafts, leaf springs |
| Continuous woven (fabric) | 40 – 55 % | Body panels, bonnets |
| Continuous multiaxial (NCF) | 50 – 60 % | Structural monocoques |

**Carbon Fibre Composite (CFRP) — The Premium Tier:**

Carbon fibres are classified by their **tensile modulus** (the higher, the stiffer):

| Carbon Fibre Type | Tensile Modulus | Density | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard modulus (T300, T700) | **230 GPa** | 1.76 g/cm³ | Aerospace, automotive |
| Intermediate modulus (T800, T1000) | **294 GPa** | 1.80 g/cm³ | High-performance parts |
| High modulus (M50, M55) | **490 GPa** | 1.91 g/cm³ | Space structures |
| Ultra-high modulus (M65, M70) | **690 GPa** | 2.15 g/cm³ | Satellite components |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A **PAN-based carbon fibre** is the industry standard. The precursor (polyacrylonitrile) is oxidised, carbonised, and graphitised through a series of precisely controlled thermal stages (200 – 3000 °C). The resulting turbostratic graphite structure gives its legendary stiffness.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **All quantities use SI units unless noted.** The formulas below are the *exact* ones tested in KTU 2024 University Exams for this module.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number-average molecular weight | $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$ | $N_i$ = number of chains of mass $M_i$ |
| 2 | Weight-average molecular weight | $\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Same variables |
| 3 | Polydispersity Index (PDI) | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | $\text{PDI} = 1$ for monodisperse |
| 4 | Degree of Polymerisation (DP) | $\text{DP} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit |
| 5 | Density of composite (Rule of Mixtures) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$ | $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction |
| 6 | Modulus of composite — longitudinal (Rule of Mixtures) | $E_c^L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | Voigt (iso-strain) model |
| 7 | Modulus of composite — transverse (Inverse Rule of Mixtures) | $\dfrac{1}{E_c^T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{(1 - V_f)}{E_m}$ | Reuss (iso-stress) model |
| 8 | Strength of composite — longitudinal | $\sigma_c^L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$ | $\sigma_m'$ = matrix stress at fibre failure strain |
| 9 | Volume fraction from weight fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | $W_f, W_m$ = weight fractions |
| 10 | Specific strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | Units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| 11 | Specific modulus | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}$ | Same units |
| 12 | Critical fibre length (for full reinforcement) | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$ | $d$ = fibre dia, $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Material Choice | Engineered Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| BMW i3 life-module | CFRP (epoxy + carbon fabric) | Mass reduction (~**125 kg** vs steel) |
| Corvette C8 driveshaft | Continuous carbon/epoxy | High specific stiffness → no second joint |
| Volvo XC90 B-pillar | Boron-steel + CFRP patch | Crash energy absorption |
| Tesla Model S battery tray | Glass-fibre SMC | Fire retardancy, dimensional stability |
| Audi R8 spaceframe | Aluminium + CFRP | Multi-material light-weighting |
| Truck air-brake hose | EPDM rubber | Heat + ozone resistance |
| Engine mount | Natural rubber (NR) | Damping, low fatigue set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP) can deliver a **specific stiffness ~ 5× that of steel** and a **specific strength ~ 8× that of steel**, which is why every modern hypercar and EV flagship uses CFRP body structures.
