## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define eco-design. Mention any four eco-design principles.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Eco-design, also known as Design for Environment (DfE), is the systematic integration of environmental considerations into product design and development to reduce adverse environmental impacts throughout a product's life cycle (per **ISO 14006:2020**).

Four eco-design principles are:
1. Selection of low-impact materials.
2. Minimization of material usage (lightweighting).
3. Optimization of production techniques.
4. Design for end-of-life (recycling/disassembly).

---

#### **Question 2: What is Design for Disassembly (DfD)? Why is it important in automotive eco-design?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**Design for Disassembly (DfD)** is an eco-design strategy that enables products to be taken apart efficiently at the end of their useful life, allowing components and materials to be reused, remanufactured, or recycled.

Importance in automotive eco-design:
- Facilitates compliance with the **EU ELV Directive** (≥85% recyclability, ≥95% recoverability).
- Reduces dismantling time and cost at Authorized Treatment Facilities (ATFs).
- Enables recovery of high-value components (catalytic converters, battery modules, ECUs).
- Supports **Cradle-to-Cradle** material loops.
- Reduces landfill burden and environmental toxicity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: (a)** Explain the EU End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC and its impact on automotive material selection. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The **EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC**, enforced from 2003, is a cornerstone regulatory framework governing the end-of-life treatment of road vehicles. Its primary objectives are:

1. **Prevention of Waste from Vehicles** — Encourage manufacturers to design vehicles with reduced hazardous substance content and increased recyclability.
2. **Collection and Treatment Standards** — Mandate that all End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs) be collected by **Authorized Treatment Facilities (ATFs)** free of charge to the last owner.
3. **Recovery and Recycling Targets** — Set quantitative recovery benchmarks:
   - By **1 January 2006**: 85% recyclability + 95% recoverability (by mass).
   - Long-term goal: 95% reuse/recovery + 85% recycling.
4. **Hazardous Substance Restrictions** — Prohibition of **lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), and hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** in vehicle components (with limited exemptions).
5. **Producer Responsibility** — OEMs bear the financial and operational responsibility for end-of-life management.

**Impact on Automotive Material Selection:**
- **Lead Substitution** → Replacement of Pb in wheel weights, solders, and battery electrodes with bismuth, tin-silver alloys, and lithium chemistries.
- **Cadmium Replacement** → Cd coatings on fasteners replaced by zinc-nickel or zinc-iron coatings.
- **Mercury Elimination** → Mercury switches in trunk and hood lighting removed entirely.
- **Polymer Restructuring** → Shift from PC/ABS blends (hard to recycle) to mono-material polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE) systems.
- **Coding and Identification** → All plastic components >100 g must be marked per **ISO 11469** for material identification during dismantling.
- **Lightweighting Push** → Drives adoption of **AHSS, aluminum, magnesium, and CFRP** to reduce use-phase emissions.

---

#### **Question A: (b)** With neat diagrams, explain the four phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) as per ISO 14040/14044. Discuss its application in selecting sustainable automotive materials. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**LCA (Life Cycle Assessment)** is the standardized, quantitative methodology for evaluating the environmental impacts of a product throughout its entire life cycle. Per **ISO 14040:2006** and **ISO 14044:2006**, LCA comprises **four iterative phases**:

**Phase 1: Goal and Scope Definition**
- Define the **functional unit** (e.g., "transport one passenger over 1 km").
- Set **system boundaries** (Cradle-to-Gate, Cradle-to-Grave, or Cradle-to-Cradle).
- Identify assumptions and limitations.

**Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)**
- Compile all **input flows** (raw materials, energy, water) and **output flows** (emissions to air/water/soil, waste).
- Data collected from databases (e.g., Ecoinvent, GaBi).

**Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)**
- Translate inventory data into **impact categories**: Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidification Potential (AP), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP), and Cumulative Energy Demand (CED).
- Includes **normalization** and **weighting** steps.

**Phase 4: Interpretation**
- Identify **hotspots** (life cycle stages with highest impact).
- Conduct **sensitivity analysis** and **uncertainty assessment**.
- Generate actionable recommendations for designers.

**Application in Automotive Material Selection:**

LCA enables data-driven material choices, e.g.:
- Comparing **steel vs. aluminum** for body panels — aluminum has higher production-phase GWP but offers 30–40% mass reduction, yielding net lifecycle benefits.
- Evaluating **bio-based composites** (e.g., natural fiber-reinforced PP) vs. traditional glass-fiber composites — bio-composites often show 20–30% lower embodied energy.
- Assessing **lithium-ion battery chemistry** variants (LFP vs. NMC) for electric vehicle battery packs based on production-phase carbon footprint and end-of-life recyclability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *skip Phase 4 (Interpretation)* and *omit diagrams*. Each LCA phase carries roughly **1.5 marks**, with 2 marks reserved for the neat labeled **flow diagram** and 1 mark for the application case. Missing any one phase or the diagram can cost up to **4 marks**.

---

#### **Question B: (a)** Discuss the 6R strategy in automotive eco-design with one example for each. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

The **6R Strategy** is the operational hierarchy guiding automotive eco-design decisions, ordered from highest to lowest environmental benefit:

| # | Strategy | Definition | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rethink** | Reframe the product concept to fundamentally reduce environmental impact | Replacing ICE with **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)** to eliminate tailpipe emissions |
| 2 | **Refuse** | Eliminate hazardous or non-essential materials | Banning **Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺** under ELV Directive |
| 3 | **Reduce** | Minimize material and energy use | **Lightweighting** BIW using AHSS reduces vehicle mass by 15–25% |
| 4 | **Reuse** | Direct reuse of components without reprocessing | Remanufactured **alternators, starters, ECUs** in service exchange |
| 5 | **Recycle** | Material reprocessing into new products | Shredded **steel scrap** fed into EAF steelmaking for new vehicle components |
| 6 | **Recover** | Energy recovery from non-recyclable waste | **Pyrolysis** of end-of-life tyres to recover fuel oil and carbon black |

**Key Insight:** Strategies 1–3 (upstream interventions) yield 5–10× greater environmental benefit than strategies 5–6 (downstream waste management). KTU examiners expect students to articulate this hierarchy explicitly.

---

#### **Question B: (b)** Explain the role of **Lightweighting** and **Material Substitution** in automotive eco-design. Provide two real-world case studies. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Lightweighting:**
Lightweighting is the deliberate reduction of vehicle mass without compromising structural integrity, safety, or performance. It is the most impactful eco-design lever for the use-phase, since a 10% mass reduction typically yields a 6–8% fuel economy improvement in ICE vehicles.

Techniques include:
- **Down-gauging** (using thinner high-strength steel).
- **Material substitution** (steel → aluminum, magnesium, CFRP).
- **Topology optimization** (removing material from low-stress regions via CAE).
- **Hollow section design** and **integrated castings** (e.g., Tesla's single-piece rear underbody).

**Material Substitution:**
Material substitution involves replacing conventional, high-environmental-impact materials with lower-impact alternatives. Common automotive substitutions:
- Mild steel → **Aluminum alloys** (A356, A6082) for body panels.
- Cast iron engine blocks → **Aluminum** (better strength-to-weight ratio).
- Petroleum-based plastics → **Bio-polymers** (Bio-PA, Bio-PP, PLA blends).
- Petroleum-based rubber → **Natural rubber + silica compounds**.
- Rare-earth permanent magnets in EV motors → **Ferrite or switched-reluctance** alternatives.

**Case Study 1: Tesla Model Y — Unibody Casting**
Tesla's Model Y uses a single-piece **Mega-cast aluminum rear underbody** replacing ~70 stamped steel parts. Benefits:
- 20% mass reduction in rear structure.
- 300+ part count reduction → fewer joints, less adhesive, lower assembly energy.
- ~10% improvement in range for equivalent battery size.

**Case Study 2: BMW i3 — CFRP Passenger Cell**
BMW's i3 electric vehicle features a **Carbon-Fiber Reinforced Plastic (CFRP)** Life Module passenger cell paired with an aluminum Drive Module.
- ~250–350 kg mass reduction vs. equivalent steel BIW.
- 50% lower lifecycle CO₂ vs. comparable ICE vehicles.
- Demonstrates that eco-design decisions must always be validated at the **full vehicle lifecycle** level, not just production phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Errors:**
> 1. Students frequently present **Lightweighting** and **Material Substitution** as the *same* concept. They are **distinct**: Lightweighting is the **goal**, Material Substitution is one of several **methods** to achieve it. Conflating them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. Case studies must include **quantitative data** (e.g., mass saved, % CO₂ reduction). Generic statements like "BMW i3 is eco-friendly" score zero on the application sub-part.
> 3. Neglecting to mention **Cradle-to-Grave** vs. **Cradle-to-Gate** boundary in LCA context is a recurring KTU 2024 penalty trigger.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- ✅ **Eco-design = DfE = Environmentally Conscious Design** — defined per **ISO 14006:2020**.
- ✅ **Five-dimensional design equation:** Cost + Performance + Aesthetics + **Environment** + **Social Impact**.
- ✅ **EU ELV Directive targets:** **85% recyclability + 95% recoverability** by mass.
- ✅ **4 Banned Substances (ELV):** **Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺** (memorize the symbol and full name).
- ✅ **6R Hierarchy (priority order):** Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover.
- ✅ **8 Eco-Design Principles** span material, production, distribution, use, EoL, and serviceability.
- ✅ **LCA standard:** **ISO 14040:2006** (principles) + **ISO 14044:2006** (requirements). Four phases: **Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation**.
- ✅ **LCA system boundaries:** Cradle-to-Gate, Cradle-to-Grave, Cradle-to-Cradle.
- ✅ **Lightweighting rule of thumb:** **6–8% fuel economy improvement per 100 kg mass saved** (ICE).
- ✅ **DfD (Design for Disassembly)** uses **snap-fits, modular bolts, and standardized fasteners** to enable fast EoL separation.
- ✅ **DfR (Design for Recycling)** prioritizes **mono-material components** and **ISO 11469 polymer coding** (>100 g).
- ✅ **Kerala grid emission factor:** ~**0.16–0.20 kg CO₂/kWh** (relevant for EV LCA discussion).
- ✅ **Two flagship automotive case studies:** **Tesla Model Y (mega-cast aluminum)** and **BMW i3 (CFRP + bio-interior)**.
- ✅ **Bio-based automotive materials:** Kenaf, flax, hemp, jute, sisal, natural rubber, bio-PP, bio-PA.
- ✅ **LCA software platforms in industry:** **SimaPro, GaBi (now Sphera), openLCA, Granta MI**.
- ✅ **Future regulatory trend:** **Digital Product Passport (DPP)** mandated by the EU from **2027** — track for KTU current-affairs questions.
- ✅ **Common KTU marking traps:** Missing diagrams in LCA answers, conflating Lightweighting with Material Substitution, omitting numerical data in case studies, forgetting the 4 banned substances.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental regulations and sustainability standards for the automotive industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental regulations and sustainability standards for the automotive industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Environmental regulations** in the automotive industry refer to the codified legal frameworks, emission standards, and conformity-of-production protocols imposed by governmental and intergovernmental bodies to limit the environmental footprint of motor vehicles across their entire life cycle. These include tailpipe emission norms (Euro VI/BS-VI, EPA Tier 3, China VI), fuel-economy regulations (CAFE – Corporate Average Fuel Economy), and end-of-life directives (ELV Directive 2000/53/EC).

**Sustainability standards** are voluntary or mandatory structured frameworks—such as **ISO 14001** (Environmental Management Systems), **ISO 50001** (Energy Management), **ISO 14040/14044** (Life Cycle Assessment), and the **UN SDG-9 & SDG-12** principles—that govern material selection, manufacturing energy intensity, recyclability, and circular-economy integration in automotive production.

Together, these two pillars form the **Regulatory-Sustainability Nexus** that drives material substitution (e.g., from ferrous to aluminium, composites, or bio-based polymers) and process innovation (e.g., near-net-shape forming, additive manufacturing) in modern vehicle platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 3 of PEAUT745, students must connect *every* material covered (AHSS, aluminium alloys, magnesium, polymers, composites) to at least one environmental regulation and one sustainability standard. Examiners specifically test this linkage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child playing with a chemistry set in a closed room. Now imagine the parent walking in and saying, "You can keep playing, but you can only release **X grams of smoke per hour**, you must clean up **95% of your materials** when you're done, and your energy bill cannot exceed **Y joules per experiment." That parent is the **regulator**. The rulebook they hand you—complete with checklists, audits, and certification stamps—is the **sustainability standard**.

In the automotive world, the "child" is the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)—Toyota, Tata, Volkswagen, or Tata Motors. The "room" is the planet. The "smoke" is tailpipe CO₂, NOₓ, PM, and hydrocarbons. The "clean-up" is the ELV recycling mandate. The "energy bill" is the Life-Cycle CO₂ budget enforced by CAFE or the EU Fleet CO₂ regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize (Bold = Numerical Constant):**
> - **ELV Directive recovery target: 95% by mass (85% recyclability + 10% energy recovery)**
> - **EU 2025 Fleet CO₂ target: 93.6 g CO₂/km** (cars); **Euro 6d NOₓ limit for diesel: 80 mg/km**
> - **BS-VI (India) PM limit for diesel: 4.5 mg/km; NOₓ: 80 mg/km**
> - **CAFE Phase-II (India 2027): 91.7 g CO₂/km for 4-wheelers ≤ 3.5 t**

## 1.3 The Two Pillars – A Visual Distinction

| Aspect | Environmental Regulation | Sustainability Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | **Mandatory / Legal** | Mostly **Voluntary / Market-driven** |
| Enforced by | Government / EU Commission / EPA | ISO, Industry Consortia, OEMs |
| Penalty for non-compliance | Fines, sales bans, type-approval revocation | Loss of market access, brand damage |
| Focus | Emission limits, end-of-life recovery | Process management, LCA, ESG reporting |
| Example | Euro VI, BS-VI, CAFE, ELV | ISO 14001, ISO 14040, GRI Standards |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tiered Framework of Automotive Environmental Regulations

The regulations operate in **four nested layers**, each with progressively wider scope:

1. **Vehicle-Level (Tailpipe) Regulations**
   - Govern exhaust emissions of CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, PN (particle number).
   - Examples: **Euro 6d-TEMP**, **BS-VI Stage-II**, **EPA Tier 3 Bin 30**, **China 6b**.
   - Test cycles: WLTP (Worldwide harmonized Light vehicles Test Procedure), NEDC (legacy), FTP-75 (US).

2. **Fleet-Level (Average) Regulations**
   - Govern the *average* emissions of an OEM's entire product portfolio.
   - Examples: **CAFE (India & US)**, **EU Fleet CO₂ Regulation (EU/2019/631)**, **China CAFC**.

3. **Material & End-of-Life Regulations**
   - Govern the *substance* of the vehicle and its post-use phase.
   - Examples: **ELV Directive 2000/53/EC**, **REACH Regulation (EC 1907/2006)**, **RoHS 2011/65/EU**, **Battery Directive 2006/66/EC**.

4. **Process & Lifecycle Standards**
   - Govern the *manufacturing* and *use-phase* energy/carbon intensity.
   - Examples: **ISO 14001:2015**, **ISO 50001:2018**, **ISO 14040/14044 (LCA)**, **PAS 402:2013** (sustainable manufacturing).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Regulation Layer

- **Tailpipe norms** exist because the WHO classifies urban air pollution (PM2.5, NO₂) as a **Group 1 carcinogen**. The health cost of inaction exceeds industry compliance costs by a factor of ~10 (WHO, 2021 estimates).
- **Fleet-level norms** prevent "regulatory gaming" where an OEM sells one EV and 99 petrol SUVs to meet a fleet average.
- **ELV/REACH** address the *hidden* environmental cost: a typical 1,400 kg vehicle contains **~200 kg of polymers, fluids, and hazardous substances** (lead, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, mercury) that can leach into groundwater in landfills.
- **LCA/ISO 14040** addresses the *upstream* cost: producing 1 kg of virgin aluminium releases **~11 kg CO₂e**, while recycled aluminium releases only **~0.5 kg CO₂e** (IEA, 2022 data).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Units | Meaning & Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$ | g CO₂/km | CO₂ emission from fuel (EF: gasoline ≈ 3.18, diesel ≈ 3.20 kg CO₂/L) |
| 2 | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100\%$ | % | ELV recovery efficiency (target **≥ 95%**) |
| 3 | $\eta_{recycle} = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{vehicle}} \times 100\%$ | % | Material recycling rate (target **≥ 85%**) |
| 4 | $E_{tank-to-wheel} = \dfrac{m_{fuel} \times LHV}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | MJ/km | Energy delivered at wheels |
| 5 | $C_{specific} = \dfrac{CO_2}{d \times \rho}$ | g CO₂/km | Specific carbon intensity per unit distance |
| 6 | $GWP = \dfrac{Emission_{gas} \times RF_{gas}}{Emission_{CO_2} \times RF_{CO_2}}$ | dimensionless | Global Warming Potential (CO₂ = 1; CH₄ = 28; N₂O = 265) |
| 7 | $C_{LCA} = C_{mat} + C_{mfg} + C_{use} + C_{EoL}$ | kg CO₂e | Life Cycle Assessment total carbon |
| 8 | $\eta_{ELV} = w_{steel} R_{steel} + w_{Al} R_{Al} + w_{poly} R_{poly}$ | % | Mass-weighted ELV recyclability |
| 9 | $m_{CO_2, fleet} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \times CO_{2,i}}{\sum n_i}$ | g/km | OEM fleet average (CAFE) |
| 10 | $PN = \dfrac{N_{particles}}{km}$ | #/km | Particle Number limit (Euro 6: **6.0 × 10¹¹ /km**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and "such that" brackets in the table use `\vert` (rendered as a vertical bar without breaking markdown). For example, $\vert GWP - 1 \vert \le 0.05$ means *the deviation of GWP from 1 must be ≤ 0.05*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulae drive **every** decision in an OEM's advanced materials strategy:

- A 100 kg mass reduction in a passenger car saves **~9 g CO₂/km** over its life.
- Switching from virgin to recycled aluminium in a hood saves **~250 kg CO₂e per vehicle**.
- Achieving **95% ELV recovery** is mathematically impossible if the vehicle contains > **5%** non-recoverable thermoset composites or bonded dissimilar joints that cannot be separated → drives selection of **monomaterial designs** and **reversible adhesive bonding**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) Compliance Equation

The **fleet-average CO₂ emission** that an OEM must demonstrate in any compliance year $y$ is governed by a harmonic-mean-like weighted sum. We derive the general form.

### Step 1 – Define the Fleet Composition

Let an OEM sell $N$ vehicle variants in a market during year $y$. For variant $i$:

- Sales volume: $n_i$ (units)
- Specific CO₂ emission measured over WLTP: $CO_{2,i}$ (g/km)
- Mass of vehicle $i$ in running order: $m_i$ (kg)
- Footprint (wheelbase × track): $A_i$ (m²)

### Step 2 – Write the Unweighted Average

The arithmetic mean of the fleet emissions is:

$$
\overline{CO_2} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} CO_{2,i}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This treats every variant as equally important, which is wrong—high-volume vehicles matter more.

### Step 3 – Introduce Volume Weighting

The **volume-weighted fleet CO₂** is the proper CAFE input:

$$
CO_{2, fleet} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot CO_{2,i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Now a high-selling SUV pulling 200 g/km drags the average up just as it should.

### Step 4 – Introduce the Mass/Utility Adjustment (EU 2025+)

The EU regulation (EU/2019/631) applies a **utility factor** $u_i$ based on mass $m_i$ and footprint $A_i$:

$$
u_i = 1 + a \left( m_i - m_0 \right) + b \left( A_i - A_0 \right)
$$

where $a = 0.0303$, $b = 1106.39$, and $m_0, A_0$ are reference values. The **target** for the OEM is:

$$
CO_{2,target} = 93.6 \cdot \dfrac{\sum n_i \cdot u_i}{\sum n_i}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Heavier or larger cars get a *more lenient* target, reflecting their functional use (e.g., family haulers).

### Step 5 – Compliance Gap and Penalty

The gap is $\Delta = CO_{2,fleet} - CO_{2,target}$. For each gram above the target in year $y$:

$$
\text{Penalty} = \Delta \cdot n_{sold} \cdot P_{g}
$$

where $P_g = $ **€95 per g/km per vehicle** (EU penalty rate since 2019).

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose an OEM sells 3 variants:

| Variant | $n_i$ | $CO_{2,i}$ (g/km) | $m_i$ (kg) | $A_i$ (m²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (small EV) | 50,000 | 0 | 1,400 | 2.30 |
| B (mid SUV) | 30,000 | 140 | 1,650 | 2.70 |
| C (large SUV) | 20,000 | 180 | 1,900 | 2.95 |

With $m_0 = 1379$ kg, $A_0 = 2.50$ m², and $a = 0.0303$, $b = 1106.39$:

#### Step 1: Compute the unweighted average (purely illustrative)

$$
\overline{CO_2} = \dfrac{0 + 140 + 180}{3} = 106.67 \text{ g/km}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute volume-weighted fleet average

$$
CO_{2,fleet} = \dfrac{50{,}000(0) + 30{,}000(140) + 20{,}000(180)}{100{,}000}
$$

$$
CO_{2,fleet} = \dfrac{0 + 4{,}200{,}000 + 3{,}600{,}000}{100{,}000} = \dfrac{7{,}800{,}000}{100{,}000}
$$

$$
CO_{2,fleet} = 78.0 \text{ g/km}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute utility factors

For A: $u_A = 1 + 0.0303(1400 - 1379) + 1106.39(2.30 - 2.50)$
$$
u_A = 1 + 0.0303(21) + 1106.39(-0.20) = 1 + 0.636 - 221.28 = -219.64
$$

The negative value is a *flag*: the EU target is not active for ZEVs; their value is set to a floor. The correct EU regulation uses a piecewise scheme. For this pedagogical example, we *clamp* the floor to 1.0.

For A (clamped): $u_A = 1.0$
For B: $u_B = 1 + 0.0303(1650 - 1379) + 1106.39(2.70 - 2.50)$
$$
u_B = 1 + 0.0303(271) + 1106.39(0.20) = 1 + 8.21 + 221.28 = 230.49
$$

For C: $u_C = 1 + 0.0303(1900 - 1379) + 1106.39(2.95 - 2.50)$
$$
u_C = 1 + 0.0303(521) + 1106.39(0.45) = 1 + 15.79 + 497.88 = 514.67
$$

#### Step 4: Compute weighted target

$$
CO_{2,target} = 93.6 \times \dfrac{50{,}000(1.0) + 30{,}000(230.49) + 20{,}000(514.67)}{100{,}000}
$$

$$
CO_{2,target} = 93.6 \times \dfrac{50{,}000 + 6{,}914{,}700 + 10{,}293{,}400}{100{,}000}
$$

$$
CO_{2,target} = 93.6 \times \dfrac{17{,}258{,}100}{100{,}000} = 93.6 \times 172.58
$$

$$
CO_{2,target} = 16{,}154.7 \text{ g/km (clearly unrealistic without normalisation)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Normalisation note:** In the official regulation, the target is normalised to fleet-average utility, and the 93.6 figure is the *fleet target value*, not a multiplier. The above shows the *structural* form. In a KTU exam, students are expected to write the framework and explain the role of $u_i$, not compute absurdly large numbers.

#### Step 5: Compliance Check

For the exam, simply state:

$$
\Delta = CO_{2,fleet} - CO_{2,target}; \quad \text{If } \Delta > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Penalty applies}
$$

The penalty for our fleet (simplified, using $n_{sold} = 100{,}000$ and $P_g = €95$):

$$
Penalty = \Delta \times 100{,}000 \times 95 = 9{,}500{,}000 \times \Delta \text{ euros}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: ELV Material Recyclability Mass-Balance

Let a vehicle have mass fractions $w_j$ for material $j$ (steel, Al, plastic, rubber, glass, fluids). Each has a recycling yield $R_j$. The total recyclability is:

$$
\eta_{recycle} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j \cdot R_j \times 100\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is a mass-weighted average of individual recyclabilities, which is the metric reported to the ELV registration authority.

**Boundary conditions:**
- $0 \le R_j \le 1$
- $\sum w_j = 1$
- ELV 2000/53/EC requires $\eta_{recycle} \ge 85\%$, $\eta_{recovery} \ge 95\%$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material ↔ Regulation ↔ Standard

| Material | Dominant Regulation Driving Its Use | Dominant Sustainability Standard | Quantitative Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AHSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel)** | Euro NCAP, ELV | ISO 14001, ISO 50001 | +15% strength allows 20% mass reduction → 9 g CO₂/km saved |
| **Aluminium (primary)** | EU 2025 CO₂ | ISO 14040 LCA | 11 kg CO₂e/kg production burden |
| **Aluminium (recycled)** | ELV 95% target | ISO 14021 (recycled content) | 0.5 kg CO₂e/kg, 95% energy saving |
| **Magnesium alloys** | CAFE Phase-II | PAS 402 | 33% lighter than Al, but RECYCLABILITY < 50% (challenge) |
| **Carbon-fibre composites (CFRP)** | WLTP CO₂ reduction | ISO 14040 (Life Cycle) | Excellent in-use, but **recycling cost 20× virgin**; ELV blocker |
| **Natural-fibre composites (NFC)** | Bio-based content rules | ISO 14040, EU Bio-based Content Standard | Negative CO₂ (sequestered), but moisture durability issue |
| **Engineering plastics (PP, PA6, PBT)** | REACH, RoHS, ELV | ISO 11469 (resin coding) | Recyclability ~ 60–80%, hazardous additives must be < 0.1% w/w |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Regulatory Pyramid for Automotive Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UNFCCC Paris Agreement] --> B[EU Green Deal 2050]
    A --> C[UN SDG 9 and 12]
    B --> D[EU Fit for 55 Package]
    C --> E[ISO 14001 EMS]
    B --> F[EU Fleet CO2 2019 631]
    D --> F
    D --> G[Euro 6d Emission Norm]
    F --> H[CAFE Phase II India]
    F --> I[OEM Material Strategy]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    E --> J[ISO 50001 EnMS]
    E --> K[ISO 14040 LCA]
    J --> I
    K --> I
    I --> L[Material Selection: AHSS Al Mg CFRP NFC]
    I --> M[Process Innovation: Additive Mfg Net Shape Forming]
    I --> N[End of Life: ELV 95 percent Recycling]
    L --> O[Sustainable Vehicle Platform]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Life Cycle Assessment Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Material Extraction] --> P2[Material Processing]
    P2 --> P3[Component Manufacturing]
    P3 --> P4[Vehicle Assembly]
    P4 --> P5[Use Phase 200000 km]
    P5 --> P6[End of Life ELV]
    P6 --> P7[Recycling Stream]
    P6 --> P8[Energy Recovery]
    P6 --> P9[Landfill Disposal]
    P7 --> P2
    P8 --> P2
    subgraph LCA_ISO14040
    P1
    P2
    P3
    P4
    P5
    P6
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Compliance Decision Flow for Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Material Candidate] --> S1{Substance on REACH SVHC List?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S1A[REJECT or Apply Authorisation]
    S1 -- No --> S2{Recyclability R sub j Ge 85 percent}
    S2 -- No --> S2A[Seek Substrate or Reversible Joining]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{LCA Carbon Footprint acceptable}
    S3 -- No --> S3A[Increase Recycled Content or Switch to Bio-based]
    S3 -- Yes --> S4{Eligible for OEM Lightweight Strategy}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[APPROVE for Production]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Re-evaluate Vehicle Architecture]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ISO 14001 EMS in an OEM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Top Management Commitment] --> T2[Environmental Policy]
    T2 --> T3[Planning: Aspect Impact Register]
    T3 --> T4[Legal and Other Requirements Register]
    T4 --> T5[Objectives Targets and KPIs]
    T5 --> T6[Operational Controls: Material Selection Process Control ETP]
    T6 --> T7[Monitoring and Measurement: Stack Testing ELV Reporting]
    T7 --> T8[Internal Audit]
    T8 --> T9[Management Review]
    T9 --> T10[Continual Improvement]
    T10 --> T1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "draw a flowchart of EMS," always include the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle in the labels. ISO 14001:2015 is explicitly a PDCA-aligned standard—showing only the boxes without PDCA loses 2 marks.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Material-Process-Regulation Triple Linkage

