```mermaid
    subgraph MAT[Material Layer]
    M1[AHSS]
    M2[Aluminium Alloy]
    M3[Magnesium]
    M4[Polymer Composite]
    M5[Bio Fibre Composite]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Process Layer]
    P1[Hot Stamping]
    P2[High Pressure Die Casting]
    P3[Thixomolding]
    P4[RTM and SMC]
    P5[Compression Moulding]
    end
    subgraph REG[Regulatory Layer]
    R1[Euro 6d WLTP]
    R2[EU Fleet CO2 2019 631]
    R3[ELV 2000 53 EC]
    R4[REACH 1907 2006]
    R5[ISO 14001 50001]
    end
    M1 --> P1
    M2 --> P2
    M3 --> P3
    M4 --> P4
    M5 --> P5
    P1 --> R1
    P2 --> R2
    P3 --> R2
    P4 --> R3
    P5 --> R5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the ELV Directive and state its 2015 recovery and recyclability targets.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC is a European Union regulation governing the design, take-back, and recovery of motor vehicles at the end of their service life. It applies to all M1 and N1 category vehicles. The 2015 targets are: (i) **95% reuse and recovery** by mass, and (ii) **85% reuse and recycling** by mass, with the remaining 10% permissible only as energy recovery. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for reuse+recovery target, 1 for recyclability target.)

### Q2. What is CAFE Phase-II in India, and what is the fleet CO₂ target for 4-wheelers ≤ 3.5 t?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) Phase-II is the second stage of India's fuel-economy regulation notified by the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, applicable from **1 April 2027**. It mandates a fleet-average CO₂ target of **91.7 g/km** for 4-wheeled vehicles with gross vehicle weight ≤ 3.5 tonnes. The compliance metric is measured on the **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)**. Non-compliance attracts a penalty of ₹25,000 per g/km per vehicle. (3 marks: 1 for full form, 1 for the 91.7 g/km number, 1 for penalty.)

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the EU Fleet CO₂ Regulation (EU/2019/631). Discuss how mass and footprint influence the OEM-specific target, with a clear mathematical formulation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vehicle platform uses 1,500 kg of materials with the following breakdown. Compute the mass-weighted recyclability and comment on ELV compliance.

| Material | Mass (kg) | Recyclability Yield $R_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| Mild steel | 700 | 0.95 |
| AHSS | 350 | 0.90 |
| Aluminium | 200 | 0.85 |
| Polypropylene | 150 | 0.70 |
| EPDM rubber | 80 | 0.30 |
| PUR foam | 20 | 0.10 |

(7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) – Structure of EU/2019/631 (7 marks):**

1. **Scope (1 mark):** Applies to all new passenger cars (M1) and light commercial vehicles (N1) registered in the EU.

2. **Targets (1 mark):** From 2021, fleet average target = **95 g CO₂/km** (cars) and 147 g/km (vans), tightening to **93.6 g/km** in 2025 and **49 g/km in 2030** (proposed Fit-for-55).

3. **Formula (3 marks):** The OEM-specific target $CO_{2,target}$ uses the utility function. We write:

$$
CO_{2,target,OEM} = 95 + a \left( \bar{m} - m_0 \right) + b \left( \bar{A} - A_0 \right)
$$

where $\bar{m} = \dfrac{\sum n_i m_i}{\sum n_i}$ and $\bar{A} = \dfrac{\sum n_i A_i}{\sum n_i}$ are volume-weighted fleet averages. Constants: $a = 0.0333$, $b = 0.0288$, $m_0 = 1379.88$ kg, $A_0 = 2.50$ m² (cars). The compliance gap $\Delta$ incurs a penalty of **€95 per g/km per vehicle**.

4. **Why mass/footprint adjustment (2 marks):** Without it, OEMs would be incentivised to downsize the fleet (selling only small cars) or upsize (selling only luxury SUVs) to escape average. The utility function normalises by use-case: larger family cars get a more lenient target reflecting passenger-carrying utility. The ZEV/EEV factor (super-credits) was phased out after 2022 and replaced by the 2025–2030 phase-out trajectory.

**Part (b) – Mass-Weighted Recyclability (7 marks):**

Step 1: Total mass = $700 + 350 + 200 + 150 + 80 + 20 = 1500$ kg ✓
Step 2: Mass fractions:
- $w_{steel} = 700/1500 = 0.4667$
- $w_{AHSS} = 350/1500 = 0.2333$
- $w_{Al} = 200/1500 = 0.1333$
- $w_{PP} = 150/1500 = 0.1000$
- $w_{EPDM} = 80/1500 = 0.0533$
- $w_{PUR} = 20/1500 = 0.0133$

Step 3: Apply recyclability equation

$$
\eta_{recycle} = \sum w_j R_j
$$

$$
\eta_{recycle} = (0.4667)(0.95) + (0.2333)(0.90) + (0.1333)(0.85) + (0.1000)(0.70) + (0.0533)(0.30) + (0.0133)(0.10)
$$

Step 4: Evaluate each term:
- Term 1: $0.4434$
- Term 2: $0.2100$
- Term 3: $0.1133$
- Term 4: $0.0700$
- Term 5: $0.0160$
- Term 6: $0.0013$

Step 5: Sum

$$
\eta_{recycle} = 0.4434 + 0.2100 + 0.1133 + 0.0700 + 0.0160 + 0.0013
$$

$$
\eta_{recycle} = 0.8540 = 85.40\%
$$

Step 6: Adding energy recovery: assume the remaining $1 - 0.8540 = 0.1460$ (14.60%) fraction can be **partially** recovered as energy from EPDM and PUR incineration. If 60% of the non-recycled fraction is sent to energy recovery:

$$
\eta_{recovery} = 0.8540 + (0.60)(0.1460) = 0.8540 + 0.0876 = 0.9416 = 94.16\%
$$

Step 7: **Compliance comment (1 mark):** The vehicle **meets the ELV recyclability target (85.40% > 85%) but narrowly fails the recovery target (94.16% < 95%)**. To achieve 95% recovery, the OEM must either:
- Increase PP recyclability to 0.80 (e.g., by mono-material design), or
- Replace EPDM with TPV (thermoplastic vulcanizate) at 0.60 recyclability, or
- Use closed-loop PUR foam chemistry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss ISO 14001:2015 and ISO 50001:2018. Explain how they interlink in an automotive manufacturing plant, with specific reference to the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle. (7 marks)

**(b)** A steel B-pillar weighs 12 kg. Replacing it with an aluminium extrusion reduces the mass to 5.4 kg. Compute the CO₂ saving over a vehicle life of 200,000 km, given:
- Material production: steel = 1.85 kg CO₂e/kg; Al (recycled) = 0.5 kg CO₂e/kg
- Use-phase mass-decarbonisation factor: 0.06 kg CO₂e per kg-mass per 100,000 km
Comment on whether the switch is environmentally beneficial overall. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) – ISO 14001 & ISO 50001 in an OEM Plant (7 marks):**

1. **ISO 14001:2015 (Environmental Management System)** (2 marks): Specifies the requirements for an EMS based on the PDCA cycle. Core elements: Context of the Organization, Leadership, Planning (Aspect-Impact Register), Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement. It mandates that an organization identify *environmental aspects* (e.g., VOC emissions from paint shop, metal fines in stamping, hazardous sludge) and commit to *legal compliance* (linked to REACH, ELV).

2. **ISO 50001:2018 (Energy Management System)** (2 marks): Specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an **EnMS**. Core elements: Energy Review, Energy Baseline (EnB), Energy Performance Indicators (EnPI), Objectives, Action Plans. In an OEM, the EnPI is typically **kWh per vehicle produced** or **MJ per kg of body-in-white painted**.

3. **Interlink (3 marks):** Both systems use PDCA and share the "Management Review" and "Continual Improvement" clauses. An OEM can implement a **single integrated management system (IMS)** with shared:
- Documented information
- Internal audit programme (one audit covers both)
- Management review (single agenda item, dual KPIs)

   In practice, ISO 50001 feeds the energy data (EnPI) into the ISO 14001 Aspect-Impact register, making the EMS "energy-aware." Example: A paint shop's VOC abatement energy use is monitored under ISO 50001 (EnPI) and reported under ISO 14001 as an environmental aspect.

**Part (b) – B-Pillar CO₂ Saving (7 marks):**

Step 1: Production CO₂ for steel

$$
CO_{2,steel,prod} = 12 \text{ kg} \times 1.85 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg} = 22.20 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

Step 2: Production CO₂ for aluminium

$$
CO_{2,Al,prod} = 5.4 \text{ kg} \times 0.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg} = 2.70 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

Step 3: Production-phase saving

$$
\Delta CO_{2,prod} = 22.20 - 2.70 = 19.50 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e (saving from Al)}
$$

Step 4: Mass saving

$$
\Delta m = 12 - 5.4 = 6.6 \text{ kg}
$$

Step 5: Use-phase saving. The factor is 0.06 kg CO₂e per kg per 100,000 km. Over 200,000 km:

$$
CO_{2,use,save} = 6.6 \text{ kg} \times 0.06 \times \dfrac{200{,}000}{100{,}000} = 6.6 \times 0.06 \times 2
$$

$$
CO_{2,use,save} = 6.6 \times 0.12 = 0.792 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

Step 6: Total saving (production + use)

$$
\Delta CO_{2,total} = 19.50 + 0.792 = 20.292 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

Step 7: **Comment (1 mark):** The aluminium substitution is *strongly* environmentally beneficial in this case because the use-phase factor is very small (0.06) relative to material production difference. The dominant saving comes from the **much lower carbon footprint of recycled aluminium** (0.5 vs 1.85). Note: if the aluminium were *primary* (11 kg CO₂e/kg), the calculation would reverse:

$$
CO_{2,Al,primary} = 5.4 \times 11 = 59.4 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} \Rightarrow \Delta CO_{2,prod} = -37.2 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e (LOSS)}
$$

This illustrates **why recycled-content aluminium is critical** for sustainability arguments. **Conclusion:** With recycled aluminium, the switch saves ~20.3 kg CO₂e per vehicle—**environmentally beneficial**. With primary aluminium, it would be **environmentally detrimental**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating CO₂ (in g/km) with CO₂e (kg-eq in LCA):** The two use *different* denominators and time-scales. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the "utility factor" in EU/2019/631:** Writing $CO_{2,target} = 95$ g/km flat is wrong; it is OEM-specific.
> 3. **Mixing up "recyclability" and "recovery"** in ELV: 85% recycling, 95% recovery—different things.
> 4. **Ignoring the recycled-vs-primary material distinction** in LCA questions—costs you 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 5. **Not closing the loop in numerical answers:** Always end with a one-line **compliance comment** ("meets/fails/just passes the target")—examiners award 1 mark for this synthesis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **ELV Directive (2000/53/EC) — 95% recovery / 85% recycling** (numerical anchors to memorise).
- **EU Fleet CO₂ Regulation 2019/631 — 93.6 g/km in 2025**, with utility factor $a = 0.0333$, $b = 0.0288$, $m_0 = 1379.88$ kg, $A_0 = 2.50$ m² for cars; penalty **€95/g/km/vehicle**.
- **CAFE Phase-II (India, 2027) — 91.7 g CO₂/km** for ≤ 3.5 t vehicles; penalty ₹25,000/g/km/vehicle.
- **Euro 6d (WLTP) — NOₓ ≤ 80 mg/km (diesel), PN ≤ 6.0 × 10¹¹/km**.
- **BS-VI (India) — PM 4.5 mg/km (diesel), NOₓ 80 mg/km, PN 6.0 × 10¹¹/km**.
- **REACH — SVHC content < 0.1% w/w**; Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr⁶⁺ banned or restricted in automotive parts.
- **LCA stages:** Raw Material → Processing → Manufacturing → Use → EoL (ISO 14040/14044).
- **Material-specific burden:** Steel = 1.85, Al recycled = 0.5, Al primary = 11, Mg = 8, CFRP = 24, PP = 1.7 kg CO₂e/kg.
- **Lightweighting rule-of-thumb:** 100 kg mass reduction → **~9 g CO₂/km** in-use, plus material-production delta.
- **Mass-weighted recyclability formula:** $\eta_{recycle} = \sum w_j R_j$; for compliance: $\eta_{recycle} \ge 85\%$, $\eta_{recovery} \ge 95\%$.
- **ISO 14001 & ISO 50001 share the PDCA cycle**—integrated management systems are the industry norm.
- **Sustainability is NOT just "being green"**—it is the mathematically traceable management of (1) substance (REACH), (2) recyclability (ELV), (3) carbon (CAFE/EU fleet), and (4) energy (ISO 50001) across the life cycle.
- **Most-tested concept in KTU PEAUT745 Module 3:** Link each material covered in earlier modules (AHSS, Al, Mg, polymer, composite) to (a) one emission/CO₂ regulation and (b) one sustainability standard with a *quantitative* number.
- **Examiner's "wow factor" phrase:** "Monomaterial design enables ELV recyclability while reducing joining complexity and improving LCA score" — drops 2 extra marks if used correctly in a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements for lightweighting and energy efficiency in electric vehicles (EVs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Requirements for lightweighting and energy efficiency in electric vehicles (EVs) # ⚡ Module 4 — Requirements for Lightweighting and Energy Efficiency in Electric Vehicles (EVs) ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formal Definition (Syllabus-Grade):** > *Lightweighting in Electric Vehicles refers to the systematic reduction of vehicle curb mass through the strategic substitution of conventional mild-steel and cast-iron components with advanced engineering materials (AHSS, aluminum alloys, magnesium alloys, CFRP composites, and hybrid multi-material systems), executed without compromising structural integrity, crashworthiness, NVH performance, or passenger safety, in order to maximize gravimetric energy density utilization, extend single-charge driving range, reduce electrical energy consumption per kilometre, and improve overall powertrain efficiency.* In plainer KTU-board language: **lightweighting = "doing more km per kWh by using smarter, lighter materials"**, while **energy efficiency = "spending fewer joules to move the vehicle by one metre"**. Together, they form the *coupled design objective* that defines modern EV engineering. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🚲 The Bicycle-and-Backpack Analogy** > Imagine two students cycling the same 10 km to college. Student A carries a **20 kg backpack** of textbooks; Student B carries only **5 kg**. Even on a flat road, Student B pedals less hard, arrives less sweaty, and his/her energy bar lasts the whole trip. The "backpack" in an EV is the **battery pack, chassis, body-in-white, drivetrain, and interior**. Every kilogram saved reduces the *demand* on the battery, which in turn allows either a smaller battery (cost saving) or a longer range (utility gain). This is the *core feedback loop* between lightweighting and energy efficiency. The EV is essentially a "**rolling battery**" — and a heavier rolling battery needs *more energy just to roll itself*, which is why lightweighting is not optional but *thermodynamically mandatory*. ## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU 2024 | Reason | KTU Board Examiner Perspective | |---|---| | **Range Anxiety** is the #1 EV consumer concern | Examiners test whether you can link mass → range quantitatively | | **Battery = 25–40% of EV mass** | Mass reduction *outside* the battery amplifies the effective energy density | | **Regulatory Pressure** (CAFE / Euro 7 / Bharat Stage VI+) | Lightweighting is a *compliance lever* alongside electrification | | **Cost-Per-kWh Economics** | Less mass → smaller battery → lower BOM cost | ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold as Mandated) - **Gravitational acceleration: g = 9.81 m/s²** - **Air density at STP: ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** - **Coefficient of rolling resistance (passenger tyre): C_rr ≈ 0.008 – 0.015** - **Target EV curb mass reduction (next-gen vs current): Δm ≥ 10–15 %** - **Typical EV battery specific energy: 150–250 Wh/kg** (NMC), **80–120 Wh/kg** (LFP) - **Energy consumption benchmark: 15–20 kWh / 100 km** (mid-size sedan) - **Power density target (drivetrain): ≥ 5 kW/kg** - **Vehicle glider-to-battery mass ratio target: ≤ 1.5** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Coupled Lightweighting–Energy-Efficiency System ## 2.1 The Five Functional Demands on an EV Platform An EV's lightweighting strategy must satisfy **five simultaneously-binding constraints**. Failing any one collapses the engineering case: 1. **Mechanical Structural Demand** — Carry static + dynamic + crash loads. 2. **Energy Efficiency Demand** — Minimize Wh consumed per km travelled. 3. **Thermal Management Demand** — Dissipate motor & battery heat; survive -30 °C to +60 °C ambient. 4. **Manufacturing & Joining Demand** — Be formable, weldable, bondable at high-volume (≥ 50 units/hour). 5. **End-of-Life & Sustainability Demand** — Recyclability ≥ 85 % by mass (EU ELV Directive). > [!NOTE] > **The KTU Board Mantra:** > *"A material that is light but cannot be welded is useless on a 50,000-unit-per-year production line."* ## 2.2 Energy Flow in an EV — Where Does Every kWh Go? The traction battery's stored chemical energy is distributed as follows: $$E_{\text{battery}} = E_{\text{rolling}} + E_{\text{aero}} + E_{\text{acceleration}} + E_{\text{climb}} + E_{\text{aux}} + E_{\text{losses}}$$ Breaking each term: - **E_rolling** — energy to overcome tyre rolling resistance (scales with mass). - **E_aero** — energy to overcome aerodynamic drag (mass-independent). - **E_acceleration** — kinetic energy supplied (scales with mass). - **E_climb** — gravitational potential energy gain (scales with mass). - **E_aux** — HVAC, lights, infotainment, battery cooling (mass-independent). - **E_losses** — drivetrain, inverter, motor copper & iron losses (~10–18 %). > The **first three** are *mass-sensitive*; **lightweighting directly attacks E_rolling and E_acceleration**. ## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown — Why Each Demand Drives Lightweighting ### 2.3.1 Driving-Range Extension The single-charge range R is governed by: $$R = \frac{E_{\text{usable}}}{\frac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{\text{km}}}$$ Reducing vehicle mass by Δm reduces the energy consumed per km by approximately: $$\Delta E_{\text{consumed/km}} \approx \Delta m \cdot (g \cdot C_{rr} + a_{\text{equiv}})$$ For every **100 kg** removed, mid-size EV range improves by roughly **5–7 %** in urban WLTP cycles. ### 2.3.2 Acceleration & Power-to-Weight Ratio Required tractive force: $$F_{\text{tractive}} = m \cdot a + \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$ For a 0–100 km/h sprint time t: $$t_{0\text{–}100} = \int_{0}^{v_f} \frac{m \cdot v}{P_{\text{wheel}} - F_{\text{losses}}(v)} \, dv$$ Lightness = agility = better 0–100 km/h even with the *same* motor. ### 2.3.3 Battery-Pack Sizing (Down-Sizing Effect) Because range must be preserved when mass is reduced, the battery pack can be *downsized* proportionally, giving a **double dividend**: 1. Direct mass saving (no battery). 2. Indirect mass saving (smaller pack housing, cooling, mounting brackets). ### 2.3.4 Crash Energy Management Modern EV crash structures must absorb: $$E_{\text{crash}} = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot v_{\text{impact}}^2$$ Lower mass = less crash energy to dissipate, allowing **thinner-walled** (lighter) crash rails — but designers must still maintain *intrusion protection*, hence the rise of **multi-material hybrid structures**. ### 2.3.5 NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Lightweight materials often have **lower damping** than steel. The KTU-mandated compromise: - Use **sandwich composites**, **constrained-layer damping**, or **AHSS-reinforced panels** to recover NVH performance lost by mass reduction. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd Law | N ; fundamental traction law | | 2 | $P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{tractive}} \cdot v$ | Power demand at wheels | W ; mass-dependent | | 3 | $E_{\text{kinetic}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy stored in vehicle | J ; halved if mass halved | | 4 | $F_{\text{rolling}} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$ | Rolling resistance force | N ; scales **linearly** with m | | 5 | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N ; **mass-independent** | | 6 | $E_{\text{consumed/km}} = g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 / m + \dots$ | Energy per km | Wh/km ; **first term mass-sensitive** | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = P_{\text{wheel}}/P_{\text{battery}}$ | Drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless ; target ≥ 0.90 | | 8 | $R = E_{\text{battery}} / E_{\text{per-km}}$ | Driving range | km ; **doubly improved by mass reduction** | | 9 | $\text{Power-to-weight} = P_{\text{motor}}/m_{\text{vehicle}}$ | Performance metric | W/kg ; sport-EV target ≥ 200 | | 10 | $\rho_{\text{specific, comp}} = \rho_{\text{steel}}/\rho_{\text{comp}}$ | Specific gravity advantage | ratio ; e.g. CFRP ≈ 5× | | 11 | $E_{\text{crash,absorbed}} = \int F \cdot ds$ | Crash energy absorption | J ; reduced with mass | | 12 | $\Delta R / R \approx \alpha \cdot (\Delta m / m)$ | Sensitivity of range to mass | dimensionless ; α ≈ 0.6–0.8 urban | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notational Safeguard:** All table cells use the LaTeX vertical bar equivalent `\vert` only when absolute-value is required; otherwise raw notation is preferred to preserve KTU-style markdown rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This theoretical framework directly governs: - **Tesla Model Y** structural battery pack (mass-centralized floor → improved handling + mass reduction). - **BMW i3** — first mass-produced CFRP-bodied EV; LifeDrive architecture. - **Mahindra eVerito** / **Tata Nexon EV** — Indian EV lightweighting using high-tensile steel + aluminium closures. - **Audi e-tron GT** — multi-material (steel + Al + CFRP) body-in-white. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tractive Force Equation **Starting Point:** An EV of total mass m moving at velocity v on a road of slope θ must overcome rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and gravity, while accelerating at rate a. **Step 1 — Resolve forces along the direction of motion:** The vehicle must apply a forward tractive force F_t equal to the sum of resisting forces plus the inertia force. $$F_t = F_{\text{inertia}} + F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{climb}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Newton's 2nd Law for inertia:** The inertia force to accelerate mass m at rate a is: $$F_{\text{inertia}} = m \cdot a$$ **Step 3 — Substitute rolling resistance (semi-empirical):** A tyre under load N = mg cosθ has rolling resistance force: $$F_{\text{rolling}} = N \cdot C_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$ **Step 4 — Substitute aerodynamic drag:** Newtonian drag with frontal area A, drag coefficient C_d, air density ρ: $$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$ **Step 5 — Substitute climbing force:** Component of weight along slope: $$F_{\text{climb}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 6 — Assemble the master traction equation:** $$F_t = m a + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta$$ > This is the **canonical KTU equation**; commit it to memory verbatim. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Sensitivity of Energy Consumption (Wh/km) **Step 1 — Define energy per unit distance as the dot product of force and displacement:** $$E_{\text{per km}} = \int_0^{1000\,\text{m}} F_{\text{resistive}} \cdot dx$$ **Step 2 — Split force into mass-sensitive (M) and mass-insensitive (A) parts:** $$F_{\text{resistive}} = \underbrace{m(gC_{rr}\cos\theta + a + g\sin\theta)}_{F_M} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2}_{F_A}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate over 1 km assuming steady-state (a = 0, θ = 0):** $$E_{\text{per km}} = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$ **Step 4 — Convert to Wh/km (divide by 3600):** $$\boxed{\,E_{\text{Wh/km}} = \frac{m g C_{rr}}{3600} + \frac{\rho C_d A v^2}{7200}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Partial derivative w.r.t. mass (sensitivity):** $$\frac{\partial E}{\partial m} = \frac{g C_{rr}}{3600}$$ For g = 9.81 m/s² and C_rr = 0.010: $$\frac{\partial E}{\partial m} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.010}{3600} \approx 2.73 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kWh/(km·kg)}$$ **Interpretation:** Every additional kilogram adds ~2.7 × 10⁻⁵ kWh per km, i.e., for a 500 km trip, +0.0136 kWh per kg. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Range Improvement from Mass Reduction **Step 1 — Original range:** $$R_1 = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{E_{\text{per km, 1}}}$$ **Step 2 — Range after mass reduction Δm:** $$R_2 = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{E_{\text{per km, 1}} - \Delta m \cdot g C_{rr} / 3600}$$ **Step 3 — Ratio of ranges:** $$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{1}{1 - \Delta m \cdot g C_{rr} / (3600 \, E_{\text{per km,1}})}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical baseline (typical mid-size EV):** Let E_per_km,1 = 0.17 kWh/km, Δm = 100 kg, g = 9.81, C_rr = 0.010. $$\frac{\Delta m \cdot g C_{rr}}{3600 \, E_{\text{per km,1}}} = \frac{100 \times 9.81 \times 0.010}{3600 \times 0.17}$$ $$= \frac{9.81}{612} \approx 0.01603$$ **Step 5 — Final ratio:** $$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.01603} = \frac{1}{0.98397} \approx 1.0163$$ **Result:** A 100 kg mass reduction yields ~**1.63 % range increase** in steady-state cruising. In stop-and-go urban driving (where a ≠ 0), the gain rises to **5–7 %**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kinetic Energy & Regenerative Braking Synergy **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of vehicle at speed v:** $$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$ **Step 2 — At v = 50 km/h = 13.89 m/s, with m = 1800 kg:** $$KE = 0.5 \times 1800 \times (13.89)^2 = 0.5 \times 1800 \times 192.9 = 173{,}610 \,\text{J} = 48.2 \,\text{Wh}$$ **Step 3 — If mass is reduced by 200 kg:** $$\Delta KE = 0.5 \times 200 \times 192.9 = 19{,}290 \,\text{J} = 5.36 \,\text{Wh per stop}$$ In a typical urban commute with ~6 stops/km, this saves **~32 Wh/km** in regen-recoverable kinetic energy — non-trivial. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mass-Sensitive vs Mass-Insensitive Demands | Demand | Mass-Sensitive? | Lightweighting Lever | Energy-Efficiency Lever | |---|---|---|---| | Rolling resistance | ✅ Yes (linear) | Reduce m | Reduce C_rr via tyre choice | | Aerodynamic drag | ❌ No | Reduce frontal area A | Streamline body (lower C_d) | | Hill climbing | ✅ Yes | Reduce m | Route planning / regen downhill | | 0–100 km/h sprint | ✅ Yes (inertia) | Reduce m | Higher motor power | | Crashworthiness | ✅ Yes (KE ∝ m) | Crumple-zone design | Speed limiters / AEB | | Battery thermal load | ✅ Yes (proportional) | Reduce m → smaller pack | Better cell chemistry | | HVAC load | ❌ No (mass-indep) | n/a | Heat-pump, insulated cabin | | NVH | ✅ Yes (panel mass) | Damping patches | Active noise cancellation | ## 3.6 Quantitative Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical **Problem:** An EV of mass **2000 kg** (including 500 kg battery) cruises at **80 km/h (22.22 m/s)** on a flat road. C_d = 0.28, A = 2.3 m², C_rr = 0.012, drivetrain efficiency η = 0.92. Battery usable energy = **60 kWh**. Determine (a) tractive power, (b) energy consumption per km, (c) single-charge range. Then repeat for a light-weighted variant of **1700 kg**. **(a) Tractive force at 80 km/h, flat road:** $$F_t = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$$ $$F_t = 2000 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times (22.22)^2$$ $$F_t = 235.44 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 493.8$$ $$F_t = 235.44 + 194.84 \approx 430.3 \,\text{N}$$ **(b) Power at wheels = F_t · v:** $$P_{\text{wheel}} = 430.3 \times 22.22 \approx 9560 \,\text{W} = 9.56 \,\text{kW}$$ **Battery power needed (account for η):** $$P_{\text{bat}} = P_{\text{wheel}} / \eta = 9.56 / 0.92 \approx 10.39 \,\text{kW}$$ **(c) Energy per km:** $$E_{\text{per km}} = F_t \times 1 / \eta / 1000 = (430.3 \times 22.22 / 3600) / 0.92 \approx 0.156 \,\text{kWh/km}$$ **(d) Range:** $$R = 60 / 0.156 \approx 384.6 \,\text{km}$$ **Now for 1700 kg variant:** $$F_t = 1700 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 + 194.84 = 200.12 + 194.84 = 394.96 \,\text{N}$$ $$E_{\text{per km}} = (394.96 \times 22.22 / 3600) / 0.92 \approx 0.143 \,\text{kWh/km}$$ $$R = 60 / 0.143 \approx 419.6 \,\text{km}$$ **Gain:** Range improves from 384.6 km to 419.6 km → **+9.1 % range** for a **15 % mass reduction**. This validates the KTU principle that lightweighting *amplifies* battery investment. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lightweighting ↔ Energy Efficiency Coupling in an EV The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *causal flow* between vehicle subsystems, mass-reduction levers, and energy-efficiency outputs. It is intentionally architecture-level rather than pictorial, since the *physical geometry* of an EV is multi-faceted and best illustrated in a CAD drawing outside the scope of these notes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Battery Pack
25 to 40 percent of EV mass] --> B[Total Curb Mass m] C[Body in White
Steel Al CFRP] --> B D[Chassis and Suspension
Al Castings Forged Links] --> B E[Drivetrain Housing
Al Mg Alloys] --> B F[Interior Trim
Foam Plastics Composites] --> B G[Glazing and Closures
Polycarbonate Al Hood] --> B B --> H[Tractive Force Demand F equals m a plus m g Crr plus aero] H --> I[Wheel Power P equals F times v] J[Battery Usable Energy E bat] --> K[Range R equals E bat over E per km] I --> L[Energy per km E per km] L --> K B --> L K --> M[Customer Range Satisfaction] L --> N[Operating Cost per km] I --> O[0 to 100 km per h Sprint Time] M --> P[Lightweighting Justified] N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Material Selection Decision] Q --> C Q --> D Q --> E Q --> F Q --> G style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000 style H fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style I fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style K fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 style P fill:#e7c9ea,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#000 style Q fill:#f3d9fa,stroke:#862e9c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Subsystem-Level Mass-Allocation Map (Hierarchical View) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[EV Total Mass 100 percent] --> B1[Battery Pack 30 percent] A0 --> B2[Body in White 25 percent] A0 --> B3[Chassis Suspension Brakes 15 percent] A0 --> B4[Drivetrain E Motor Inverter Gearbox 10 percent] A0 --> B5[Interior Seats HVAC Trim 10 percent] A0 --> B6[Closures Glass Hood Doors 7 percent] A0 --> B7[Misc Fluids Wiring 3 percent] B1 --> C1[Energy Density 250 Wh per kg] B2 --> C2[Material Substitution Steel to Al to CFRP] B3 --> C3[Forged Al Control Arms Hollow Knuckles] B4 --> C4[Integrated Die Cast E Axle Housing] B5 --> C5[Natural Fibre Composites Foam Reduction] B6 --> C6[Al Hood Polycarbonate Side Windows] B7 --> C7[Optimised Wiring Harness Al Busbars] style A0 fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B1 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B2 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B3 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B4 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B5 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B6 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style B7 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style C1 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C2 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C3 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C4 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C5 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C6 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style C7 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Lightweighting Strategy Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Target Mass Reduction 10 to 15 percent] --> S1{Cost Per kg Saved Budget} S1 -->|lt 8 USD per kg| S2[Use AHSS Steel Advanced High Strength Steel] S1 -->|8 to 25 USD per kg| S3[Aluminium Alloys Closures Hood Doors] S1 -->|gt 25 USD per kg| S4[Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer CFRP Body Panels] S2 --> S5[Verify Formability Weldability Crash Performance] S3 --> S6[Use Self Piercing Rivets Adhesive Bonding] S4 --> S7[Resin Transfer Molding or Compression Molding] S5 --> S8[Validate on Full Vehicle CAE Simulation] S6 --> S8 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[Confirm Mass Range Cost Targets Achieved] style S0 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000 style S1 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000 style S2 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 style S3 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 style S4 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 style S5 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style S6 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style S7 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 style S8 fill:#e7c9ea,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#000 style S9 fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]** **"List the three most mass-sensitive energy losses in an electric vehicle and explain why lightweighting improves each."** (3 marks) > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **Rolling resistance loss** — Force = m·g·C_rr; energy/km is directly proportional to mass. Reducing mass lowers rolling resistance linearly, thus saving Wh/km. *(1 mark)* > 2. **Acceleration / kinetic-energy loss** — Kinetic energy = ½·m·v²; for the same target 0–100 km/h time, lighter vehicle needs lower peak motor power, and during regen braking recovers more Wh per stop. *(1 mark)* > 3. **Hill-climbing (gravitational) loss** — Force = m·g·sinθ; lighter EV requires less battery energy to ascend a slope and recovers more on descent. *(1 mark)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, S1 Pattern]** **"Define 'power-to-weight ratio' for an EV and state its typical target value for a sport-segment electric passenger car."** (3 marks) > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Definition:** Power-to-weight ratio is the peak continuous motor power (in kW) divided by the vehicle curb mass (in kg or tonnes), expressed as **W/kg**. *(1 mark)* > - **Formula:** PWR = P_motor / m_vehicle *(1 mark)* > - **Target value:** For sport-segment EVs, **PWR ≥ 200 W/kg** (e.g., a 2-tonne EV needing ≥ 400 kW motor). For economy EVs, the target is 50–100 W/kg. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8(a)]** **(a)** Derive the complete tractive-force equation for an electric vehicle moving at velocity *v* on a road inclined at angle θ, identifying each term. Show how the equation reduces for a flat road at constant speed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electric car has a mass of 1900 kg including battery. The aerodynamic drag coefficient is 0.29 and frontal area 2.25 m². Tyre rolling resistance coefficient is 0.011. Drivetrain efficiency is 91 %. Estimate: (i) the wheel power required to cruise at 90 km/h on a flat road, (ii) the battery energy consumed per km, and (iii) the additional power required to climb a 6 % gradient at the same speed. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Start with Newton's 2nd Law applied along the vehicle's longitudinal axis: $$F_t - F_{\text{rolling}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{climb}} = m \cdot a$$ Solving for tractive force F_t: $$F_t = m \cdot a + F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{climb}}$$ Substitute standard sub-expressions: $$F_t = m a + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta$$ *(2 marks for assembly; 2 marks for identifying each term — inertia, rolling, aero, climb)* **Term identification:** - m·a — **inertia force** *(0.5 mark)* - m·g·C_rr·cosθ — **rolling resistance** *(0.5 mark)* - ½ρC_dAv² — **aerodynamic drag** *(0.5 mark)* - m·g·sinθ — **gravity / hill-climb component** *(0.5 mark)* **Flat road, constant speed** (a = 0, θ = 0, cos 0 = 1, sin 0 = 0): $$F_t = m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$ *(2 marks for correct reduction)* **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** Given: m = 1900 kg, C_d = 0.29, A = 2.25 m², C_rr = 0.011, η = 0.91, v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. **(i) Wheel power at 90 km/h flat road (2 marks):** $$F_{\text{rolling}} = 1900 \times 9.81 \times 0.011 = 205.07 \,\text{N}$$ $$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.25 \times (25)^2$$ $$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.25 \times 625$$ $$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 1406.25 = 249.8 \,\text{N}$$ $$F_t = 205.07 + 249.8 = 454.87 \,\text{N}$$ $$P_{\text{wheel}} = F_t \times v = 454.87 \times 25 = 11{,}372 \,\text{W} \approx 11.37 \,\text{kW}$$ **(ii) Battery energy per km (2 marks):** $$E_{\text{wheel, per km}} = F_t \times 1 \,\text{km} = 454.87 \times 1000 = 454{,}870 \,\text{J} = 0.1264 \,\text{kWh}$$ $$E_{\text{bat, per km}} = 0.1264 / 0.91 \approx 0.1389 \,\text{kWh/km}$$ **(iii) Additional power for 6 % gradient (3 marks):** 6 % gradient → tan θ ≈ 0.06 → sin θ ≈ 0.0599, cos θ ≈ 0.9982. $$F_{\text{climb}} = m g \sin\theta = 1900 \times 9.81 \times 0.0599 = 1116.5 \,\text{N}$$ $$F_{\text{rolling, incline}} = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta = 1900 \times 9.81 \times 0.011 \times 0.9982 = 204.6 \,\text{N}$$ $$F_{\text{aero}} = 249.8 \,\text{N (unchanged)}$$ $$F_{t,\text{incline}} = 204.6 + 249.8 + 1116.5 = 1570.9 \,\text{N}$$ $$P_{\text{addl}} = (F_{t,\text{incline}} - F_{t,\text{flat}}) \times v = (1570.9 - 454.87) \times 25$$ $$= 1116.0 \times 25 = 27{,}900 \,\text{W} \approx 27.9 \,\text{kW additional battery power}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Numerical:** > *Common student errors that cost marks:* > - Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s before plugging into v². > - Applying η in the wrong direction (energy *increases* when battery-to-wheel efficiency < 1). > - Confusing cos θ with sin θ for rolling resistance (rolling uses cos, climb uses sin). > - Using g = 10 instead of 9.81 — examiners *do* deduct half a mark. --- ### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Module 4, Q9(b)]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the five coupled engineering demands that any EV lightweighting programme must simultaneously satisfy, with one example material choice for each demand. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A current-generation EV has a curb mass of 2100 kg and delivers a single-charge WLTP range of 380 km using a 75 kWh battery pack. The design team proposes two alternative lightweighting strategies: - **Strategy A:** Reduce mass by 150 kg using AHSS body panels at a cost of ₹6/kg saved. - **Strategy B:** Reduce mass by 250 kg using CFRP closures at a cost of ₹35/kg saved. For each strategy, estimate the new range, the additional km obtained per kg saved, and the cost-per-km-of-range-gained. Recommend which strategy gives better engineering value. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Five coupled demands (7 marks):** | # | Demand | Explanation | Example Material | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Crash & Structural Safety** (1.5 marks) | Must absorb crash energy via controlled plastic deformation; intrusion protection; battery pack integrity. | **AHSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel)** in crumple zones — high strength-to-mass ratio. | | 2 | **Energy Efficiency / Range** (1.5 marks) | Lower mass → lower Wh/km → longer range OR smaller battery. | **Aluminium alloys (e.g., 6xxx series)** for closures, saving 40–50 % mass vs steel. | | 3 | **Thermal Management** (1 mark) | Battery, motor and power electronics must operate in -30 to +60 °C; lightweight thermal interfaces needed. | **Aluminium cold plates** with high thermal conductivity (≈ 200 W/m·K). | | 4 | **Manufacturing & Joining** (1.5 marks) | High-volume production needs formable, weldable, bondable materials. | **Magnesium alloy AZ31** die-castings for seat frames — easiest to cast at scale. | | 5 | **End-of-Life Recyclability** (1.5 marks) | EU ELV Directive requires ≥ 85 % recyclability by mass; design for disassembly. | **Mono-material aluminium spaceframes** (e.g., Audi A8) — single alloy stream is fully recyclable. | **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** Baseline: m = 2100 kg, R = 380 km, E_bat = 75 kWh. **Step 1 — Original energy consumption per km:** $$E_0 = 75 / 380 = 0.1974 \,\text{kWh/km}$$ **Step 2 — Sensitivity factor (assume rolling-dominated urban, α ≈ 0.7):** Use the rule of thumb that a Δm mass reduction yields a fractional range improvement of: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \alpha \cdot \frac{\Delta m}{m}$$ with α = 0.7 for mixed WLTP cycle. **Strategy A — Δm = 150 kg:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.7 \times \frac{150}{2100} = 0.05 = 5 \,\%$$ $$R_A = 380 \times 1.05 = 399 \,\text{km (gain ≈ 19 km)}$$ Cost = 150 × 6 = ₹900. Cost per extra km = 900 / 19 ≈ **₹47.4 / km** *(2 marks)* **Strategy B — Δm = 250 kg:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.7 \times \frac{250}{2100} = 0.0833 = 8.33 \,\%$$ $$R_B = 380 \times 1.0833 = 411.7 \,\text{km (gain ≈ 31.7 km)}$$ Cost = 250 × 35 = ₹8,750. Cost per extra km = 8,750 / 31.7 ≈ **₹276 / km** *(2 marks)* **Engineering value recommendation (3 marks):** | Metric | Strategy A (AHSS) | Strategy B (CFRP) | |---|---|---| | Range gain | 19 km | 31.7 km | | Total cost | ₹900 | ₹8,750 | | Cost per km gained | ₹47.4/km | ₹276/km | | Manufacturing ease | Excellent (existing lines) | Difficult (autoclave/PCM, joining) | | Recyclability | High | Low (thermoset issue) | | Repairability | Widely supported | Specialized only | **Recommendation:** **Strategy A (AHSS)** offers a *14× better cost-per-km-gained ratio*, is compatible with existing high-volume steel-body manufacturing infrastructure, and is fully recyclable. Strategy B should be reserved for **premium segments** where the CFRP cachet justifies the cost premium. The KTU-engineering verdict is *"Strategy A wins on mass-economy; Strategy B wins only on mass-performance."* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Long Answer:** > - In (a), students often skip *example materials* and lose up to 2 marks — always pair each demand with a named material. > - In (b), failing to **convert unit cost to per-km-of-range** is the single most common mistake; examiners explicitly look for normalized metrics. > - Omitting the *recyclability* row in the recommendation table costs half a mark — sustainability is a KTU 2024 scheme CO. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Revision Bullets (Module 4, EV Lightweighting & Energy Efficiency)** - **EV-specific reason for lightweighting:** Battery packs are 25–40 % of total mass → mass outside the battery is doubly expensive. - **Three mass-sensitive energy losses:** Rolling resistance, kinetic-energy (acceleration), and hill-climbing (gravitational) losses. - **Two mass-insensitive energy losses:** Aerodynamic drag and auxiliary loads (HVAC, electronics). - **Master traction equation to memorize:** $$F_t = m a + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta$$ - **Sensitivity rule of thumb:** 100 kg mass reduction → ~5–7 % range gain (urban) or ~1.6 % (steady-state cruise). - **Specific gravity ladder:** Steel (1.0) → AHSS (≈ 0.95 effective) → Al (≈ 0.5) → Mg (≈ 0.35) → CFRP (≈ 0.2). - **Five coupled design demands:** Crash safety, energy efficiency, thermal management, manufacturing/joining, end-of-life recyclability. - **Cost-per-kg-saved benchmarks:** Steel ~\$1–2, AHSS ~\$3–8, Al ~\$8–25, CFRP ~\$25–60, Mg ~\$15–30 per kg saved. - **Glider-to-battery mass ratio target:** ≤ 1.5 for next-gen EVs. - **Drivetrain efficiency benchmark:** η ≥ 0.90 (Tesla/BMW i-class achieve 0.92–0.94). - **Battery-specific-energy baseline:** 150–250 Wh/kg (NMC), 80–120 Wh/kg (LFP). - **Key dimensionless numbers:** g = 9.81 m/s² ; ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³ ; C_rr (passenger) = 0.008–0.015. - **Range equation:** R = E_battery / E_per-km ; both numerator (via smaller required battery) and denominator (via lower Wh/km) improve with lightweighting. - **Crash energy:** E_crash = ½ m v² ; lighter vehicle = less energy to dissipate, but designer must maintain *controlled deformation* zones. - **NVH caveat:** Lightweight panels often have lower damping → use **constrained-layer damping patches** or sandwich composites. - **EU ELV Directive benchmark:** ≥ 85 % recyclability by mass — influences material choice toward mono-material aluminium spaceframes. - **Regulatory tailwinds:** CAFE (USA), Euro 7 (EU), CAAR (India 2027) — all mandate *gCO₂/km* reduction, where **mass reduction is one of three levers** (alongside electrification and efficiency). - **Thermal management lever:** Lighter battery → smaller thermal mass → faster cabin pre-conditioning → auxiliary energy saving. - **Power-to-weight target for sport EVs:** ≥ 200 W/kg ; for economy EVs: 50–100 W/kg. - **Joining technology hierarchy (mass-lightest to heaviest):** Adhesive bonding + SPR → FSW → RSW → MIG → mechanical fasteners. - **Always pair a lightweighting claim with the qualifying phrase** "*without compromising crash, NVH, or manufacturability*" — this is the KTU-board way of acknowledging the coupled demands.

topics.title: Battery materials: lithium-ion, solid-state batteries, and future alternatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery materials: lithium-ion, solid-state batteries, and future alternatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Battery Materials — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Battery materials* refer to the specialized classes of electrochemically active and passive substances (electrodes, electrolytes, separators, and current collectors) that collectively govern the energy storage, power delivery, cycle life, and safety characteristics of electrochemical cells used in electric and hybrid vehicles.

In the context of automotive applications, **Advanced Automotive Battery Materials (AABM)** are engineered to satisfy three simultaneous demands:

1. **High gravimetric/volumetric energy density** — to maximize driving range per unit mass/volume.
2. **High power density** — to enable rapid charge/discharge (acceleration and regenerative braking).
3. **Long cycle life and thermal/structural stability** — to survive >1000–3000 deep cycles under automotive abuse conditions (vibration, temperature extremes, crash loads).

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Modern Automotive Battery Chemistries

| Family | Representative Chemistry | Status in EV Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Lithium-ion (LiB)** | Graphite/NMC, Graphite/LFP, Si-anode/NMC | **Mature & Commercial** (>70% EV market share) |
| **Solid-State Batteries (SSB)** | Li-metal/LLZO, Li-metal/sulfide glass | **Pilot/Pre-commercial (2025–2028)** |
| **Sodium-ion (NaB)** | Hard carbon/Na₃V₂(PO₄)₃ | **Emerging (low-cost EVs)** |
| **Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S)** | Li-metal/S₈-carbon | **R&D Stage (aviation/grid)** |
| **Metal-Air (Zn/Li-Air)** | Zn/Li-metal + O₂ from air | **Lab Stage (high theoretical energy)** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Bucket" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a rechargeable battery as a **two-storey water tank system** connected by a pump (the electrolyte + circuit).
> - The **anode** (negative electrode) is the **upper tank** that stores lithium ions when charging.
> - The **cathode** (positive electrode) is the **lower tank** that receives them during discharge.
> - The **electrolyte** is the **connecting pipe** that allows ions (water) to flow selectively.
> - The **separator** is a **one-way valve** preventing the tanks from short-circuiting (direct contact).
> - **Charging = pumping water up**, **Discharging = letting water flow down** (which spins a turbine = electric current).

When the pipe (electrolyte) is replaced with a **solid ceramic or polymer**, the system becomes a **solid-state battery** — equivalent to a "no-leak, no-corrosion" pipe, dramatically improving safety.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Exam Weightage)

- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** — Energy stored per kg of cell.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L)** — Energy per liter.
- **Specific Power (W/kg)** — Rate of energy delivery.
- **Nominal Voltage (V)** — Operating cell voltage.
- **Theoretical Capacity (mAh/g)** — Maximum Li⁺ that can be (de)intercalated per gram.
- **Coulombic Efficiency (%)** — Ratio of discharge to charge capacity.
- **Cycle Life** — Number of full charge/discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80%.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Lithium-Ion Cell (Intercalation Mechanism)

A lithium-ion cell operates on the principle of **reversible intercalation** — the reversible insertion/extraction of Li⁺ ions into the crystal lattice of host electrode materials without destroying their structure.

### Half-Reactions (Discharge)

**At the Anode (oxidation — releases Li⁺):**

$$\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6 \;+\; x\,\text{Li}^+ \;+\; x\,e^-$$

**At the Cathode (reduction — accepts Li⁺):**

$$\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{MO}_2 \;+\; x\,\text{Li}^+ \;+\; x\,e^- \;\longrightarrow\; \text{LiMO}_2$$

**Overall Cell Reaction (NMC example):**

$$\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \;+\; \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{Ni}_{1/3}\text{Mn}_{1/3}\text{Co}_{1/3}\text{O}_2 \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{C}_6 \;+\; \text{LiNi}_{1/3}\text{Mn}_{1/3}\text{Co}_{1/3}\text{O}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in automobiles:** During **regenerative braking**, the cell runs *in reverse* (Li⁺ goes back into graphite). The ability to absorb Li⁺ quickly without plating metallic Li (which causes dendrites and fires) determines the maximum regenerative current and the vehicle's energy recovery efficiency.

## 2.2 Electrochemistry of Major Cathode Chemistries

| Cathode | Formula | Nominal Voltage (V vs Li/Li⁺) | Specific Capacity (mAh/g) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LCO** | LiCoO₂ | 3.7 | 140 | High energy | Cobalt cost/toxicity |
| **NMC 811** | LiNi₀.₈Mn₀.₁Co₀.₁O₂ | 3.8 | 200 | High energy, lower Co | Thermal instability |
| **NCA** | LiNi₀.₈Co₀.₁₅Al₀.₀₅O₂ | 3.7 | 200 | High power | Safety margins |
| **LFP** | LiFePO₄ | 3.2–3.3 | 170 | Safe, long life, cheap | Lower voltage/energy |
| **LMFP** | LiMn₀.₆Fe₀.₄PO₄ | 3.4 | ~160 | Energy + safety tradeoff | Still developing |
| **Sulfur (Li-S)** | S₈ | 2.1 | 1672 (theor.) | Very high energy | Polysulfide shuttle |

## 2.3 Anode Material Evolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **anode** is the limiting electrode for next-gen EVs because graphite (372 mAh/g theoretical) is already near saturation.

| Anode | Theoretical Capacity (mAh/g) | Status | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graphite | **372** | Commercial | Limited capacity, Li plating |
| Hard Carbon | 250–300 | Na-ion only | Low voltage curve |
| **Silicon (Si)** | **4200** (Li₂₂Si₅) | Commercial (SiOx, nano-Si) | **300% volume expansion** → pulverization |
| **Lithium Metal** | **3862** | SSB target | Dendrite formation |
| Tin (Sn) | 994 | R&D | Volume expansion |
| Lithium Titanate (LTO) | 175 | Niche EVs | Low voltage → low energy |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the relationships below; numerical problems frequently combine (a) Faraday's law with (b) energy density and (c) pack-level mass efficiency.

### Core Electrochemical Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical specific capacity | $Q_{sp} = \dfrac{n\,F}{M}$ | mAh/g | $n$ = electrons transferred per mole, $F = 96485$ C/mol |
| 2 | Cell energy (theoretical) | $E = V_{nom} \times Q_{sp}$ | Wh/kg | Multiply by 1000/3600 for unit conversion |
| 3 | Nernst equation | $E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $Q$ = reaction quotient |
| 4 | Faraday's mass law | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | g | $t$ in seconds |
| 5 | Energy density | $\rho_E = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{1 + (\text{mass of inactive components})}$ | Wh/kg | Practical cell ≈ 40–60% of theoretical |
| 6 | Power density | $\rho_P = \dfrac{V^2}{R_{internal} \cdot m}$ | W/kg | Lower $R_{int}$ = higher power |
| 7 | C-rate equivalence | $I = C\text{-rate} \times Q_{rated}$ | A | 1C = full discharge in 1 hour |
| 8 | Coulombic efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | % | Automotive target >99.95% |

### Boundary Conditions & Constants

- **Faraday constant:** $F = 96485 \;\text{C/mol}$
- **Molar mass of Li:** $M_{Li} = 6.941 \;\text{g/mol}$
- **Operating temperature window (liquid LiB):** −20 °C to +60 °C
- **Automotive voltage window per cell:** 2.5 V – 4.2 V (NMC); 2.5 V – 3.65 V (LFP)
- **Specific energy target (KTU reference data):** Pack level >250 Wh/kg for next-gen EVs

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Specific Capacity of an Electrode Material

We will derive the **specific capacity** $Q_{sp}$ of a material that reversibly transfers $n$ electrons per formula unit, with molar mass $M$.

### Step 1: Start with charge per mole

One mole of active material transfers $n$ moles of electrons.

$$q_{mole} = n \cdot F = n \cdot 96485 \;\text{C}$$

### Step 2: Convert charge to capacity in mAh

Capacity in Ah is $q_{mole}/3600$. In mAh, multiply by 1000:

$$Q_{mole} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3600} \;\text{Ah} = \frac{n \cdot F \times 1000}{3600} \;\text{mAh}$$

$$Q_{mole} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3.6} \;\text{mAh}$$

### Step 3: Divide by molar mass to get specific capacity

$$Q_{sp} = \frac{Q_{mole}}{M} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3.6 \cdot M}$$

### Step 4: Numerical evaluation — Graphite (LiC₆)

For graphite, $n = 1$ (one Li per 6 carbons), $M = 72.06$ g/mol:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{sp}^{graphite} &= \frac{1 \times 96485}{3.6 \times 72.06} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{96485}{259.42} \\[4pt]
&\approx 372 \;\text{mAh/g}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the **standard textbook value** of **372 mAh/g** for graphite. ✓

### Step 5: Numerical evaluation — Silicon (Li₄₄Si → Li₂₂Si₅)

For Si → Li₂₂Si₅, $n = 4.4$ per Si atom, $M_{Si} = 28.09$ g/mol:

$$Q_{sp}^{Si} = \frac{4.4 \times 96485}{3.6 \times 28.09} = \frac{424534}{101.12} \approx 4199 \;\text{mAh/g} \approx 4200 \;\text{mAh/g}$$

This explains why silicon is the **"holy grail" anode material** — over **11× the capacity** of graphite. The trade-off is the **~300% volumetric expansion** during lithiation, which the engineering community is solving with nano-Si, SiOx composites, and elastomeric binders.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Energy Density of a LiC₆ ‖ NMC Cell

### Step 1: Mid-point voltages

$$V_{anode}^{graphite} \approx 0.1 \;\text{V vs Li/Li}^+$$
$$V_{cathode}^{NMC} \approx 3.8 \;\text{V vs Li/Li}^+$$

### Step 2: Average cell voltage

$$V_{cell} = V_{cathode} - V_{anode} = 3.8 - 0.1 = 3.7 \;\text{V}$$

### Step 3: Capacities (assume anode-limited to demonstrate)

$$Q_{anode} = 372 \;\text{mAh/g}, \quad Q_{cathode}^{NMC} \approx 200 \;\text{mAh/g}$$

### Step 4: Gravimetric energy density of cell (theoretical, no inactives)

$$\rho_E = V_{cell} \times \min(Q_{anode}, Q_{cathode}) \times \frac{\text{mass ratio factor}}{1}$$

Using cathode-limited (real automotive design):

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_E &= 3.7 \;\text{V} \times 200 \;\text{mAh/g} \\[4pt]
&= 740 \;\text{mWh/g} = 740 \;\text{Wh/kg (theoretical electrode level)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Practical cell-level correction

Real cells include electrolyte (~15%), separator (~3%), current collectors (~10%), and packaging (~5%). Active material fraction is typically **~60–65%**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_E^{cell,practical} &\approx 740 \times 0.40 \;\text{to}\; 740 \times 0.65 \\[4pt]
&\approx 296 - 481 \;\text{Wh/kg (cell level, typical LiB)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Pack-level further de-rating (~25% module/pack overhead)

$$\rho_E^{pack} \approx 740 \times 0.30 \approx 222 \;\text{Wh/kg}$$

This is why **modern EV packs are rated 150–250 Wh/kg** at the pack level.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for LiCoO₂ ‖ Graphite Cell

### Step 1: Identify the equilibrium potential

At equilibrium, $E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell}$ when $a_{Li^+} = 1$ (standard state).

### Step 2: Apply Nernst for partial lithiation state $x$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cell}(x) &= E^\circ_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{a_{\text{products}}}{a_{\text{reactants}}} \\[4pt]
&= E^\circ_{cell} - \frac{RT}{F}\ln\frac{a_{Li_{1-x}CoO_2}}{a_{Li_xC_6}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: At 298 K, substitute $RT/F = 0.02569$ V

$$E_{cell}(x) = E^\circ_{cell} - 0.02569 \cdot \ln\frac{a_{Li_{1-x}CoO_2}}{a_{Li_xC_6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The Nernst equation tells us the **open-circuit voltage (OCV)** depends on the state of charge (SOC). This is the *theoretical foundation* of the **Battery Management System (BMS)** state-of-charge estimation algorithms used in every modern EV.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Battery Family Comparison

| Parameter | LiB (Li-ion) | SSB (Solid-State) | Na-ion | Li-S | Li-Air |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Theoretical energy** (Wh/kg) | 250–270 | 400–500 | 160 | 2600 | 11,000 |
| **Practical energy** (Wh/kg) | 150–250 | 250–400 (target) | 100–150 | 300–500 | 800 (lab) |
| **Cycle life** | 1000–3000 | 500–2000 (target) | 2000–5000 | 200–500 | <100 (lab) |
| **Operating temp.** | −20 to 60 °C | −40 to 100 °C | −20 to 60 °C | 20–60 °C | RT (R&D) |
| **Safety** | Moderate (liquid electrolyte flammable) | **High** (no flammable liquid) | High | Low (dendrites, shuttle) | Poor (air ingress) |
| **Cost** | $100–140/kWh | $200+/kWh (est.) | $60–80/kWh | $80/kWh (target) | Unknown |
| **Maturity** | Mature | Pilot (Toyota, QuantumScape) | Emerging (CATL, BYD) | R&D | Lab |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lithium-Ion Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ANODE: Graphite or Si Composite on Cu Current Collector"]
    nodeB["SEPARATOR: Microporous Polyethylene PE or Polypropylene PP 20-25 micrometers"]
    nodeC["CATHODE: NMC or LFP on Al Current Collector"]
    nodeD["ELECTROLYTE: LiPF6 in EC-EMC-DMC Carbonate Solvents"]
    nodeE["Li Ions Migrate from Anode to Cathode During Discharge"]
    nodeF["Electrons Flow Through External Load Producing Current"]
    nodeG["During Charging External Power Source Reverses Ion Flow"]
    nodeH["SEAL: Hermetic Aluminum Laminate or Steel Casing"]

    nodeA -- Li plus intercalation --> nodeB
    nodeB -- ionic conduction --> nodeC
    nodeC -- accepts Li plus --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- restores Li to anode --> nodeA
    nodeD -.fills pores of.-> nodeA
    nodeD -.fills pores of.-> nodeB
    nodeD -.fills pores of.-> nodeC
    nodeH -.encloses all.-> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solid-State vs Liquid Electrolyte Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Conventional_LiB["CONVENTIONAL LIQUID LiB ARCHITECTURE"]
        A1["Anode Graphite"]
        A2["Liquid Organic Electrolyte Flammable"]
        A3["Separator PE-PP"]
        A4["Cathode NMC"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    subgraph SSB_Architecture["SOLID STATE BATTERY SSB ARCHITECTURE"]
        B1["Anode Li Metal or Si"]
        B2["Solid Electrolyte Ceramic Oxide Sulfide or Polymer"]
        B3["Cathode NMC or Sulfur or Li rich"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    A2 -- Replaced By --> B2
    A1 -- Upgraded To --> B1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Battery Material Selection Decision Logic for EV Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Define EV Application Requirements"]
    q1{"Required range per charge?"}
    p1["LFP or Na-ion Energy 100-180 Wh/kg"]
    p2["NMC 811 Energy 200-260 Wh/kg"]
    p3["SSB or Li-S Energy more than 350 Wh/kg"]

    q2{"Operating temperature extreme?"}
    p4["LTO anode based pack"]
    p5["Solid state ceramic electrolyte"]

    q3{"Cost target per kWh?"}
    p6["LFP below 100 USD/kWh"]
    p7["Na-ion below 80 USD/kWh"]
    p8["NMC 120-150 USD/kWh"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- less than 300 km --> p1
    q1 -- 300 to 500 km --> p2
    q1 -- more than 500 km --> p3
    q1 --> q2
    q2 -- below minus 20 C --> p4
    q2 -- above 60 C --> p5
    q1 --> q3
    q3 -- low cost mass market --> p6
    q3 -- budget entry EVs --> p7
    q3 -- premium long range --> p8
```

```mermaid

    sensors["T V I Sensors inside Pack"]
    bms["Battery Management System BMS ECU"]
    bcu["Body Control Unit BCU"]

    hvac["Battery Thermal Management System"]

    cell --> sensors
    sensors --> bms
    bms -- SOC SOH estimation --> bcu
    bms -- cell balancing --> cell
    bms -- power limits --> inverter
    bms -- coolant flow setpoint --> hvac
    hvac -- heat extraction --> cell
```
