# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, B.Tech Automobile, Sem VII]`
**Define smart materials. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a piezoelectric knock sensor used in a SI engine.**

**Model Answer (3-mark structured):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A smart material is one that can sense an external stimulus (stress, temperature, light, magnetic field) and respond in a *controlled, reversible* manner. It couples two or more physical domains (e.g., mechanical ↔ electrical).
- **Working (2 marks):** A PZT-5A disc is mounted on the engine block. Engine knock (abnormal combustion, ~7 kHz pressure oscillations) induces dynamic stress $T$ on the disc. By the direct piezoelectric effect, $D = d_{33} \cdot T$, generating a charge $Q$ on the electrodes. This is converted to a voltage $V$ by a high-impedance charge amplifier, filtered (5–15 kHz bandpass), and compared against a threshold in the ECU. If the threshold is exceeded, the ECU retards the ignition timing by 2°–5° crank to suppress knock.
- **Key material:** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) — $d_{33} \approx 400$ pC/N, $g_{33} \approx 24 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, B.Tech Automobile, Sem VII]`
**List any three self-healing mechanisms in polymers. Compare microcapsule-based and intrinsic self-healing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three mechanisms (1.5 marks):**
1. Microcapsule-based (White & Sottos, Nature 2001): healing agent stored in microcapsules.
2. Vascular (Toohey, Sottos, White): hollow glass fibre / 3D-printed microchannel network.
3. Intrinsic: reversible chemical bonds (Diels–Alder, disulfide, hydrogen bonding).

**Comparison table (1.5 marks):**

| Property | Microcapsule | Intrinsic |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Mechanical rupture | Thermal / optical / chemical |
| Healing cycles | **Single-shot** | **Multiple (reversible)** |
| η (typical) | 75–90% | 60–85% |
| Cost | Low–medium | Higher (specialty chemistry) |
| Automotive use | Clear coats, structural adhesives | Reusable seals, O-rings |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of constitutive equations, derive the open-circuit voltage generated by a piezoelectric sensor element. A PZT disc of thickness 2 mm and area 25 mm² is subjected to a stress of 0.8 MPa. Given $g_{33} = 26 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N and $\varepsilon_r = 1200$, find the voltage output and the capacitance. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a schematic the microcapsule-based self-healing mechanism in an automotive polymer composite. Define and compute the healing efficiency $\eta$ for a composite where $\sigma_{\text{virgin}} = 95$ MPa, $\sigma_{\text{damaged}} = 28$ MPa, and $\sigma_{\text{healed}} = 82$ MPa. Comment on whether the material qualifies as "self-healing" by KTU standards. **[7 marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — Piezoelectric Sensor Derivation **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Constitutive equations (2 marks):**
The direct piezoelectric effect relates electric displacement to applied stress:

$$
D_3 = d_{33}\,T_3 + \varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,E_3
$$

For a thin disc poled in the 3-direction with electrodes perpendicular to the 3-axis, the open-circuit output voltage is derived as follows.

**Step 2 — Voltage derivation (2 marks):**
For a disc of thickness $t$, the electric field is $E_3 = V/t$ (open-circuit approximation $E_3 \to 0$ in the high-$Z$ limit). The voltage is therefore:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = g_{33}\,T_3\,t
$$

where $g_{33} = d_{33} / \varepsilon^{T}_{33}$ is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** (V·m/N).

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= (26 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.8 \times 10^{6}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})\,\text{V} \\[4pt]
&= 26 \times 0.8 \times 2 \times 10^{-3+6-3}\,\text{V} \\[4pt]
&= 41.6 \times 10^{0}\,\text{V} \approx 41.6\,\text{V (open-circuit peak)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is too high for direct ADC; in practice a charge-amplifier / voltage divider brings it to **0–5 V**.

**Step 4 — Capacitance (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1200 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8.854 \times 1200 \times 25 \times 10^{-12-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{265620 \times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 132.8 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F} \\[4pt]
&= 132.8\,\text{pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx 41.6\,\text{V} \quad ; \quad C \approx 133\,\text{pF}}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Microcapsule Self-Healing **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Schematic description (2 marks):**
The composite consists of (i) urea-formaldehyde microcapsules (Ø = 50–200 µm) loaded with dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) monomer, and (ii) dispersed Grubbs' 1st-generation catalyst (ruthenium-based). On crack propagation, the capsules rupture, releasing DCPD into the crack plane by capillary action. The DCPD contacts the catalyst, undergoes **ring-opening metathesis polymerization (ROMP)**, and forms a cross-linked poly-DCPD bridge that restores mechanical integrity.

**Step 2 — Efficiency formula (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sigma_{\text{healed}} - \sigma_{\text{damaged}}}{\sigma_{\text{virgin}} - \sigma_{\text{damaged}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{82 - 28}{95 - 28} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{54}{67} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 0.8060 \times 100\% \approx 80.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — KTU Compliance (2 marks):**
Since $\eta \approx 80.6\% \geq 75\%$, the composite **officially qualifies as a self-healing material** per the KTU/ASTM convention. The healed strength (82 MPa) is 86.3% of the virgin strength, indicating the microcapsule + Grubbs catalyst system effectively restores the load path. To push η above 95% in an optimized design, capsule loading should be increased to 20–25 wt%, average diameter reduced to ~50 µm, and a 0.5–1 wt% catalyst dispersion used.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Describe the shape memory effect in Nitinol. An automotive HVAC louvre uses a 60 mm pre-strained (5%) Nitinol wire with $A_s = 30\,°\text{C}$ and $A_f = 36\,°\text{C}$. At an ambient temperature of 33 °C, calculate (i) the austenite fraction, (ii) the recoverable strain, and (iii) the change in wire length. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss with examples three classes of conductive materials used in modern electric vehicles. Derive Matthiessen's rule and explain its importance in designing EV busbar materials. **[7 marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Shape Memory Effect in Nitinol **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Nitinol (Ni-Ti, near-equiatomic) exhibits the **Shape Memory Effect (SME)**: a thermoelastic martensitic transformation between a high-temperature **austenite (B2 cubic)** phase and a low-temperature **martensite (B19' monoclinic)** phase. When a component is deformed in the martensitic state, the *apparent* strain is recovered upon heating above $A_f$, because the crystal lattice reverts to the remembered austenitic configuration.

**Step 2 — Austenite fraction at T = 33 °C (2 marks):**
Since $A_s < T < A_f$:

$$
f_A = \frac{T - A_s}{A_f - A_s} = \frac{33 - 30}{36 - 30} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3 — Recoverable strain (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{rec}} = f_A \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{pre}} = 0.50 \times 0.05 = 0.025
$$

**Step 4 — Length change (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta L = \varepsilon_{\text{rec}} \cdot L_0 = 0.025 \times 60\,\text{mm} = 1.5\,\text{mm}
$$

So the wire contracts by **1.5 mm**, sufficient to actuate a 30° rotation of the HVAC louvre through a small lever/linkage.

$$
\boxed{f_A = 0.50 \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_{\text{rec}} = 2.5\% \quad ; \quad \Delta L = 1.5\,\text{mm}}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Conductive Materials in EVs **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Three classes with examples (3 marks):**

1. **Pure metals and alloys:** Copper (σ = 5.96 × 10⁷ S/m) for high-voltage busbars; aluminum (σ = 3.5 × 10⁷ S/m) for lightweight wiring harness.
2. **Carbon allotropes:** Graphene (σ ≈ 10⁸ S/m) and CNTs (σ ≈ 10⁶–10⁷ S/m) used as additives in polymer-matrix composites to create conductive structural panels (e.g., Tesla Model Y underbody).
3. **Conductive polymers:** PEDOT:PSS in flexible heaters (used in EV battery cold-start heaters) and EMI-shielding gaskets.

**Step 2 — Matthiessen's Rule derivation (2.5 marks):**
The total resistivity is the sum of contributions from different scattering mechanisms. From the Drude model:

$$
\sigma = \frac{n e^{2}\tau}{m^{*}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{m^{*}}{n e^{2}\tau}
$$

If two independent scattering processes (e.g., impurities and phonons) act simultaneously, their scattering rates add by Mathiessen's empirical rule:

$$
\frac{1}{\tau_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{imp}}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{ph}}(T)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho_{\text{total}} = \frac{m^{*}}{n e^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{\tau_{\text{imp}}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{ph}}(T)}\right) = \rho_0 + \rho_{\text{phonon}}(T)
$$

For a free-electron-like metal, $\rho_{\text{phonon}} \propto T$ above the Debye temperature. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{total}}(T) = \rho_0 + \alpha T}
$$

**Step 3 — EV Busbar design relevance (1.5 marks):**
An EV busbar must (a) carry 400–800 V at 200–500 A continuously, (b) weigh as little as possible, and (c) operate over −40 °C to +85 °C. By Matthiessen's rule, at the high-T end the resistivity rises by ≈ 0.4% per °C for copper, mandating current-derating curves. Designers prefer **C11000 (OFHC) copper** with $\rho_0 \le 1.7 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$ and a flat $\alpha = 3.93 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ for predictable thermal behavior. Aluminum 6101 is chosen for weight-critical applications despite higher $\rho$, requiring larger cross-sections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls (max 2-mark deduction per question):**
> 1. **Mixing up $d_{33}$ and $g_{33}$:** $d_{33}$ is the *charge coefficient* (C/N); $g_{33}$ is the *voltage coefficient* (V·m/N). Using $d_{33}$ in a voltage-calculation problem costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** All KTU numerical answers MUST carry SI units. Drop the units = lose ½ to 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong direction of piezo effect:** A *sensor* uses the **direct effect** ($D = dT$); an *actuator* uses the **converse effect** ($S = dE$). Confusing them is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Self-healing threshold:** Forgetting the 75% threshold and not justifying whether the material qualifies as self-healing = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **SMA direction of recovery:** Recovery occurs on **HEATING** (above $A_f$), not on cooling. Reversing the direction = 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Gauge factor confusion:** $G$ is dimensionless, but $\Delta R / R_0$ is *also* dimensionless; the strain $\varepsilon$ in the denominator must be a *pure number* (e.g., 0.005, not 0.5% or 5%).
> 7. **Matthiessen's rule misuse:** The rule is **empirical** and breaks down when scattering mechanisms are correlated (e.g., in Kondo alloys). Always mention the limitation for full marks.
> 8. **Conductor confusion:** Resistivity (Ω·m) vs Conductivity (S/m) — they are reciprocals. Conflating them = 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Pre-Exam Mental Trigger List:**

- [x] **Smart material = senses + responds** (bidirectional coupling); **Functional material = does a specific job** (often unidirectional).
- [x] **Self-healing threshold** = η ≥ 75% (KTU / ASTM convention).
- [x] **Three self-healing mechanisms:** Microcapsule, Vascular, Intrinsic.
- [x] **Microcapsule system:** DCPD (healing agent) + Grubbs' catalyst + urea-formaldehyde shell.
- [x] **Direct piezoelectric effect:** $D = d\,T$ (sensing mode).
- [x] **Converse piezoelectric effect:** $S = d\,E$ (actuation mode).
- [x] **Piezo voltage:** $V = g_{33}\,T\,t$ — remember units: (V·m/N)(Pa)(m) = V.
- [x] **PZT-5A** is the automotive workhorse; $d_{33} \approx 400$ pC/N, $g_{33} \approx 24 \times 10^{-3}$ V·m/N.
- [x] **Shape memory:** Austenite (high T) ↔ Martensite (low T); recovery on **heating above $A_f$**.
- [x] **Austenite fraction (linear interpolation):** $f_A = (T - A_s) / (A_f - A_s)$.
- [x] **Gauge factor:** $G = (\Delta R/R_0)/\varepsilon$ — typical metal: 2, semiconductor: 100–200, CNT: 2–8.
- [x] **Matthiessen's rule:** $\rho_{\text{total}} = \rho_0 + \rho_{\text{phonon}}(T)$ — used to derate conductors at high T.
- [x] **Best automotive metals:** Copper (σ = 5.96 × 10⁷ S/m) for busbars, Aluminum (σ = 3.5 × 10⁷ S/m) for wiring.
- [x] **Carbon allotropes for EVs:** Graphene (10⁸ S/m), CNTs (10⁶–10⁷ S/m), carbon black (for conductive polymers).
- [x] **ITO = Indium Tin Oxide** for transparent conductive windshields / HUD.
- [x] **Nitinol = Ni-Ti, 50:50 at%** — the canonical SMA.
- [x] **PVDF** = poly(vinylidene fluoride) — a *polymer* piezo, flexible, lower $d_{33}$ than PZT.
- [x] **EMI shielding** uses conductive polymers / metal-plated plastics in ECU enclosures.
- [x] **Automotive knock sensor** frequency: 5–15 kHz bandpass.
- [x] **HVAC louvre SMA:** $A_s$ ≈ 30–35 °C is tuned to engine-bay operating temperature.
- [x] **FBG = Fiber Bragg Grating** for embeddable structural strain sensing in CFRP.
- [x] **NTC = Negative Temperature Coefficient** thermistor — resistance falls as T rises.
- [x] **Magnetostrictive Terfenol-D** for non-contact torque sensing in steering systems.
- [x] **Gas sensor oxides** SnO₂, ZnO, WO₃ — for exhaust λ / NOₓ / cabin air quality.
- [x] **Self-healing efficiency formula** is a *fraction of property recovery* — not a % of the original property.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of smart materials in vehicle systems and components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration of smart materials in vehicle systems and components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Smart Materials? (Formal KTU Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Materials (Functional Materials):** Smart materials are a specialized class of engineered materials that possess the intrinsic ability to **sense** an external stimulus (such as stress, temperature, electric field, magnetic field, pH, or light) and **respond** in a controlled, reversible, and predictable manner by altering one or more of their intrinsic properties (shape, stiffness, viscosity, color, or electrical resistivity).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEAUT745 syllabus**, smart materials are positioned as the **bridge between passive structural materials and active mechatronic systems**. Unlike conventional materials (steel, aluminum, polymers) which merely carry load, smart materials *transduce energy* between domains — converting mechanical to electrical, thermal to mechanical, magnetic to mechanical, and so on.

### 1.2 The Five Functional Pillars of Smart Behavior

| # | Functional Pillar | Energy Domain Transduction | Automotive Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensing** | Mechanical → Electrical | Strain gauges, accelerometers, knock sensors |
| 2 | **Actuation** | Electrical → Mechanical | Fuel injectors, headlamp levelling |
| 3 | **Adaptation** | Thermal/Mechanical → Property change | Self-healing polymers, viscous dampers |
| 4 | **Energy Harvesting** | Vibration → Electrical | Battery-less TPMS sensors |
| 5 | **Memory / Recovery** | Thermal → Shape | SMA actuators, deployable spoilers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus stresses the *integration* aspect — meaning not just the material science, but **how these materials interface with ECU, sensors, CAN-bus networks, and existing mechanical subsystems**. Examiners reward answers that link material behavior to a real vehicle function.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Material as a Bodybuilder with Reflexes"

Imagine a **conventional car door steel panel** as a "dumb" bodybuilder — strong, but it cannot move on its own, cannot feel, and cannot adapt. Now imagine a **smart material panel** as a bodybuilder with **reflexes, nerves, and muscles**:

- **Reflex (Sensing):** Like a nerve ending that feels pressure, a piezoelectric patch *feels* vibration and converts it to an electrical signal the ECU can read.
- **Muscle (Actuation):** Like a bicep that flexes on command, a shape memory alloy wire *contracts* when heated by a current pulse, doing mechanical work.
- **Adaptive skin (Adaptation):** Like skin that tenses in cold weather, magnetorheological damper fluid *thickens* within milliseconds when a magnetic field is applied.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mnemonic for exam:** **"SAMR"** → **S**ensing, **A**ctuation, **M**emory, **R**ecovery. Use this in 3-mark definitions.

## 1.4 Why Automakers Integrate Smart Materials — The Industry Drivers

1. **Emission & Efficiency Mandates** — piezo injectors improve fuel atomization → better combustion → lower BS-VI/Euro 7 emissions.
2. **Electrification** — EVs need vibration harvesters, smart thermal interfaces, and silent NVH countermeasures.
3. **Active Safety** — SMA-driven airbags and active crumple zones outperform pyrotechnic systems in reusability.
4. **Weight Reduction** — SMA fasteners and morphing wings replace bulky hydraulic actuators.
5. **Passenger Comfort** — electrochromic glass, MR seat dampers, and active noise control via piezoelectric speakers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Modern luxury vehicles already integrate **30–50 smart-material-based subsystems**, and the global automotive smart materials market is projected to cross **USD 15 billion** by 2030, growing at a CAGR above **18%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Smart Materials Used in Vehicles

Smart materials are categorized by the **primary energy domain** they transduce. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups them into **six families**.

### A. Piezoelectric Materials (Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT)

**Operating Principle:** Non-centrosymmetric crystal lattices (e.g., PZT, BaTiO₃, PVDF) develop a charge **D** when mechanically stressed (**direct effect**), and deform (**S**) when subjected to an electric field (**converse effect**).

**Constitutive Equations (linear, IEEE standards):**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_i &= d_{ijk}\,\sigma_{jk} + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j \quad &\text{(Direct piezoelectric effect)} \\
S_{ij} &= s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{ijk}\,E_k \quad &\text{(Converse piezoelectric effect)}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $d_{ijk}$ → **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N or m/V)
- $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij}$ → permittivity at constant stress
- $s^{E}_{ijkl}$ → elastic compliance at constant electric field

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PZT in cars?** It has the **highest $d_{33}$ coefficient (~300–600 pC/N)** among ceramics, making it ideal for both knock sensors (sensing) and piezo fuel injectors (actuation).

### B. Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol — NiTi)

**Operating Principle:** Exhibits a **martensite ↔ austenite** phase transformation at a characteristic temperature. In the *martensitic* (low-temp) state, it is soft and easily deformed; upon heating above the **Austenite Finish (A_f)** temperature, it *remembers* and recovers its original parent shape with recoverable strains up to **8%**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Strain Recovery:} \quad \varepsilon_{rec} &= \frac{L_0 - L_f}{L_0} \times 100\% \\
\text{Generated Recovery Stress:} \quad \sigma_{rec} &= E_A \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}
\end{aligned}$$

**Typical automotive use:** Active grille shutters, SMA seatbelt pretensioners, deployable rear spoilers, smart fasteners for blind-bolt applications.

### C. Magnetostrictive Materials (Terfenol-D)

Magnetostrictive materials deform when subjected to a magnetic field, governed by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_s &= \frac{\Delta L}{L} \quad \text{(saturation magnetostriction)} \\
\lambda &\approx \frac{3}{2}\,\frac{\lambda_s}{M_s^2}\,M^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Terfenol-D** delivers $\lambda_s \approx 1000$–$1600$ ppm — about **10× greater than PZT strain** — making it suitable for high-force, low-stroke automotive actuators and active engine mounts.

### D. Magneto-Rheological (MR) and Electro-Rheological (ER) Fluids

These are **controllable fluids** whose viscosity changes reversibly under an external field.

**Bingham Plastic Model (used by KTU examiners):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \tau_y(H) + \eta \dot{\gamma} \\
\tau_y(H) &= \tau_{y0} + k \cdot H^{\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $\tau_y(H)$ is the **field-dependent yield stress**, $\eta$ is the plastic viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate, $H$ is magnetic field strength, and $\alpha \approx 1$ for MR fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lord Corporation MRF-132LD** (used in **Cadillac CTS, Audi TT, Ferrari**) can vary $\tau_y$ from **~50 kPa to ~100 kPa** within **~5 ms** — that is the magic figure examiners love.

### E. Electrochromic Materials (Smart Glass)

Tungsten oxide (WO₃) thin films that reversibly switch between transparent and dark blue states by ion intercalation:

$$\begin{aligned}
WO_3 + xM^+ + xe^- &\leftrightarrow M_xWO_3 \quad \text{(colored)} \\
\text{Switching Time} &\approx 30\text{–}120 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Used in **Mercedes-Benz Magic Sky Control**, **Boeing 787 dreamliner windows** (similar principle), and increasingly in sunroof applications.

### F. Self-Healing Polymers & Nanomaterials

Microcapsule-based or vascular-based systems release a healing agent upon crack propagation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{heal} &= \frac{K_{IC,healed}}{K_{IC,virgin}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Typical } \eta_{heal} &\approx 75\text{–}95\%
\end{aligned}$$

Used in **paint coatings, tire inner liners**, and composite body panels.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize this table — almost every KTU question on Module 4 expects one formula, one numerical value, and one application.

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Key Coefficient | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric Sensing | $D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^T E$ | $d_{33}$ | 300–600 | pC/N |
| Piezoelectric Actuation | $S = s^E \sigma + d \cdot E$ | $d_{33}$ | 300–600 | pm/V |
| Shape Memory Recovery | $\varepsilon_{rec} = \Delta L / L_0$ | $\varepsilon_{rec}$ | up to 8% | % |
| SMA Recovery Stress | $\sigma_{rec} = E_A \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}$ | $E_A$ | 30–80 | GPa |
| Magnetostriction | $\lambda = \Delta L / L$ | $\lambda_s$ (Terfenol-D) | 1000–1600 | ppm |
| MR Fluid Yield Stress | $\tau_y = \tau_{y0} + k H$ | $k$ | 0.5–2.0 | kPa/(kA/m) |
| Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta$ | 0.1–1.0 | Pa·s |
| Electrochromic Switch | $WO_3 + M^+ + e^- \leftrightarrow MWO_3$ | Switching time | 30–120 | s |
| Self-Healing Efficiency | $\eta = K_{IC,healed}/K_{IC,virgin}$ | $\eta_{heal}$ | 75–95 | % |
| Damping Coefficient | $c = c_{visc} + c_{MR}$ | $c_{MR,max}/c_{min}$ | 5–10 | ratio |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Modern Vehicles

| Vehicle Subsystem | Smart Material Used | Function Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| Diesel Common-Rail Injector | PZT stack | 5–7 injection events per combustion cycle |
| Active Suspension Damper | MR fluid | Real-time stiffness control (semi-active) |
| Engine Knock Sensor | PZT ring | Cylinder pressure sensing |
| Active Grille Shutter | SMA actuator | Aerodynamic drag reduction (up to 4%) |
| Smart Sunroof | Electrochromic glass | Solar heat load reduction |
| Tire Pressure Monitoring | Piezo energy harvester | Battery-free sensor |
| Active Engine Mount | Magneto/electrorheological | NVH reduction up to 12 dB |
| Seatbelt Pretensioner | SMA wire | Pre-crash tightening (5× faster than pyrotechnic) |
| Self-Healing Paint | Microcapsule polymer | Scratch repair (90% efficiency) |
| Battery Thermal Management | Thermoelectric (Peltier) | Active heating/cooling of EV pack |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Piezoelectric Fuel Injector Response Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (frequently asked in KTU):** A piezo fuel injector stack consists of **n = 250** PZT layers, each of thickness $t = 80 \mu m$, with a piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Determine the **free displacement** and **blocking force** when a **voltage of 200 V** is applied across the stack.

### Given Data (clearly listed)
- Number of layers: $n = 250$
- Layer thickness: $t = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Piezo coefficient: $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V
- Applied voltage: $V = 200$ V
- Young's Modulus (PZT-5H): $Y = 5 \times 10^{10}$ N/m²
- Cross-sectional area: $A = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m² (= 10 mm²)

### Step 1 — Electric Field Across a Single Layer

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{V}{n \cdot t} \\
E &= \frac{200}{250 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}} \\
E &= \frac{200}{20 \times 10^{-3}} \\
E &= 10{,}000 \text{ V/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Because all $n$ layers are connected in series electrically, the total voltage divides equally. Hence the field per layer is $V$ divided by the *total stack thickness*.

### Step 2 — Free Strain per Layer (Converse Effect)

$$\begin{aligned}
S_3 &= d_{33} \cdot E \\
S_3 &= (400 \times 10^{-12}) \times (10{,}000) \\
S_3 &= 4 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{(dimensionless strain)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Free Displacement of the Entire Stack

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{free} &= n \cdot S_3 \cdot t \\
\Delta L_{free} &= 250 \times (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (80 \times 10^{-6}) \\
\Delta L_{free} &= 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
\Delta L_{free} &= 80 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** An 80 µm stroke is sufficient to open a fuel injector needle against typical return spring pressures (~25 MPa).

### Step 4 — Blocking Force (Maximum Theoretical Force at Zero Displacement)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{block} &= Y \cdot A \cdot S_3 \\
F_{block} &= (5 \times 10^{10}) \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) \times (4 \times 10^{-6}) \\
F_{block} &= 2{,}000 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Energy Density and Comparison

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Volumetric Energy Density } &= \frac{1}{2}\,Y\,S_3^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^{10} \times (4 \times 10^{-6})^2 \\
&= 0.4 \; \text{J/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Style:** Present a calculation, then close with a one-line *engineering interpretation*. That's how a 7-mark sub-part is converted into a 14-mark full answer.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Shape Memory Alloy Spring Force Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An SMA (Nitinol) compression spring is used in an *active seatbelt pretensioner*. The spring wire diameter $d = 1.5$ mm, mean coil diameter $D = 8$ mm, number of active coils $N = 8$, and the recovery strain at full transformation is $\varepsilon_{rec} = 0.06$. Compute the **recovery stroke** and the **recovery force** generated when the spring is heated past $A_f$.

### Given Data
- Wire diameter: $d = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Mean coil diameter: $D = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Active coils: $N = 8$
- Recovery strain: $\varepsilon_{rec} = 0.06$ (i.e., 6%)
- Austenite Young's Modulus: $E_A = 75 \text{ GPa}$
- Spring Index: $C = D/d$

### Step 1 — Spring Index

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{D}{d} \\
C &= \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
C &= 5.33
\end{aligned}$$

Since $4 \leq C \leq 12$, the spring is mechanically stable.

### Step 2 — Solid Length of the Spring

$$\begin{aligned}
L_s &= N \cdot d \\
L_s &= 8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \\
L_s &= 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 12 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Mean Coil Pitch and Free Length (assumed)

$$\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= N \cdot p \quad \text{where } p = 2.5\,d \\
L_0 &= 8 \times 2.5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \\
L_0 &= 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 30 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Recovery Stroke

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \varepsilon_{rec} \cdot L_0 \\
\Delta L &= 0.06 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \\
\Delta L &= 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Recovery Stress (single wire under axial recovery)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{rec} &= E_A \cdot \varepsilon_{rec} \\
\sigma_{rec} &= (75 \times 10^9) \times 0.06 \\
\sigma_{rec} &= 4.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 4.5 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Wire Cross-Sectional Area

$$\begin{aligned}
A_w &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
A_w &= \frac{\pi (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \\
A_w &= 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Recovery Force in Wire

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{wire} &= \sigma_{rec} \cdot A_w \\
F_{wire} &= (4.5 \times 10^9) \times (1.767 \times 10^{-6}) \\
F_{wire} &\approx 7{,}951 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Correction with Wahl Factor (curvature correction)

For helical springs, the **Wahl correction factor** $K_w$ accounts for curvature and direct shear:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_w &= \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C} \\
K_w &= \frac{4(5.33) - 1}{4(5.33) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{5.33} \\
K_w &= \frac{20.32}{17.32} + 0.1154 \\
K_w &= 1.173 + 0.1154 \\
K_w &= 1.288
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9 — Corrected Recovery Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{corrected} &= \frac{F_{wire}}{K_w} \\
F_{corrected} &= \frac{7951}{1.288} \\
F_{corrected} &\approx 6{,}174 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 10 — Engineering Interpretation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Typical seatbelt pretensioner force} &\approx 4000\text{–}6000 \text{ N} \\
\therefore F_{corrected} \text{ is sufficient} &\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for KTU:** Always apply the Wahl factor in helical spring derivations. Most students lose 2 marks here.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — MR Damper Force Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A magnetorheological damper is modeled as a Bingham plastic in the post-yield regime. The piston velocity is $v_p = 0.5$ m/s, the field-dependent yield stress is $\tau_y = 80$ kPa, the plastic viscosity is $\eta = 0.2$ Pa·s, and the effective piston area is $A_p = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m² with an effective gap length $L_g = 1$ mm. Calculate the **damping force**.

### Step 1 — Shear Rate in the Annular Gap

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{v_p}{L_g} \\
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{0.5}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \\
\dot{\gamma} &= 500 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Viscous Shear Stress

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{visc} &= \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} \\
\tau_{visc} &= 0.2 \times 500 \\
\tau_{visc} &= 100 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Total Shear Stress (Bingham Model)

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{total} &= \tau_y + \tau_{visc} \\
\tau_{total} &= 80{,}000 + 100 \\
\tau_{total} &\approx 80{,}100 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Damping Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{damp} &= \tau_{total} \cdot A_p \\
F_{damp} &= 80{,}100 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\
F_{damp} &\approx 160.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** In MR dampers, the *field-dependent yield stress dominates* — the viscous term is usually negligible by comparison. Use this to your advantage when justifying approximations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Piezoelectric (PZT) | Shape Memory Alloy (NiTi) | MR Fluid | Electrochromic (WO₃) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stimulus** | Voltage / Stress | Temperature | Magnetic field | Voltage + ions |
| **Response Time** | $\mu$s–ms | 10 ms–1 s | 5–10 ms | 30–120 s |
| **Strain Range** | 0.1–0.2% | up to 8% | N/A (fluid) | N/A |
| **Energy Density** | ~0.4 J/m³ | ~10⁷ J/m³ | N/A | ~5 Wh/m² |
| **Reversibility** | Excellent | Good (fatigue limited) | Excellent | Good |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High | Moderate–High | High |
| **Best Vehicle Use** | Injectors, sensors | Pretensioners, spoilers | Dampers | Sunroofs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Smart Material Subsystem Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input and Road Excitation] --> B{ECU and Sensor Fusion Layer}
    B --> C1[Vehicle CAN Bus Network]
    B --> C2[ADAS Control Unit]
    C1 --> D1[Piezo Knock Sensor]
    C1 --> D2[Piezoelectric Fuel Injector Stack]
    C1 --> D3[MR Damper Controller]
    C1 --> D4[SMA Pretensioner Driver]
    C1 --> D5[Electrochromic Glass Switch]
    D1 --> E1[Engine Combustion Feedback]
    D2 --> E2[Combustion Chamber]
    D3 --> E3[Suspension Wheel Assembly]
    D4 --> E4[Seatbelt Reel Mechanism]
    D5 --> E5[Roof and Side Glass]
    E1 --> F[Emission and Efficiency Compliance]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[Vehicle Dynamics and Comfort]
    E4 --> H[Crash Safety System]
    E5 --> I[Cabin Thermal Comfort]
    F --> J[BS-VI and Euro 7 Compliance]
    G --> K[Passenger Comfort Index]
    H --> L[NCAP Safety Rating]
    I --> K
    J --> M[Vehicle Performance Output]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The driver/road disturbance enters the *ECU & Sensor Fusion* layer (top), then is routed via CAN bus to five parallel smart-material subsystems, each of which acts on a distinct vehicle function (engine, suspension, safety, comfort). The bottom row consolidates the resulting compliance, comfort, and safety outcomes. This is the **block-level functional architecture** examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

## 4.2 Process Flow — SMA Activation in a Pretensioner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Crash Detected by G Sensor] --> P2[ECU Sends Current Pulse to SMA Wire]
    P2 --> P3[Joule Heating Raises Temperature above A_f]
    P3 --> P4[Martensite to Austenite Phase Transformation]
    P4 --> P5[Shape Recovery Generates Tensile Force]
    P5 --> P6[Force Applied to Seatbelt Reel Mechanism]
    P6 --> P7[Belt Tightens in approximately 5 ms]
    P7 --> P8[Occupant Restrained before Forward Whiplash Motion]
```

## 4.3 Control Loop Block Diagram — Semi-Active MR Suspension

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Road Profile Input] --> R2[Suspension Relative Velocity]
    R2 --> R3[Controller Algorithm Skyhook or Groundhook]
    R3 --> R4[Commanded Current from ECU]
    R4 --> R5[Current Driver and Coil in MR Damper]
    R5 --> R6[Magnetic Field Activates MR Fluid Yield Stress]
    R6 --> R7[Adjusted Damping Force on Piston]
    R7 --> R8[Wheel and Body Motion Updated]
    R8 --> R2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The MR damper is *semi-active* — it does not add energy; it only dissipates it. Hence the negative feedback nature of the loop. The control algorithm typically chosen is **Skyhook** because it gives the best ride-comfort compromise.

## 4.4 Schematic — Piezo Fuel Injector Stack Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Top Electrode Contact] --> S2[PZT Disc Layer 1]
    S2 --> S3[PZT Disc Layer 2]
    S3 --> S4[PZT Disc Layer 3]
    S4 --> S5[PZT Disc Layer N equals 250]
    S5 --> S6[Bottom Electrode Contact]
    S6 --> S7[Coupler and Hydraulic Amplifier]
    S7 --> S8[Needle Valve and Nozzle]
    S8 --> S9[Fuel Spray into Combustion Chamber]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Design Principle:** Each disc is *electrically in series* but *mechanically in parallel*. So total displacement adds up but voltage divides. This is the trick to getting micrometer-range strokes from a low-voltage source.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define smart materials and give two examples used in modern automotive systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**
> Smart materials are engineered substances that **sense** an external stimulus and **respond** in a controlled, reversible manner by altering a material property such as shape, stiffness, or color. They function as both sensors and actuators, making them ideal for active automotive subsystems.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Piezoelectric ceramic (PZT)** — used in diesel common-rail fuel injectors to achieve multiple precise injections per combustion cycle, improving BS-VI compliance.
> 2. **Shape memory alloy (Nitinol)** — used in seatbelt pretensioners that contract on joule heating to restrain occupants within ~5 ms of crash detection.
>
> Both materials enable lighter, faster, and more efficient vehicle subsystems compared to purely mechanical or hydraulic alternatives. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish between piezoelectric and electrostrictive effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
> The **piezoelectric effect** is a *linear*, *reversible* coupling between mechanical stress and electric polarization that occurs only in **non-centrosymmetric** crystals such as PZT, quartz, and BaTiO₃. The induced polarization is directly proportional to the applied stress.
>
> The **electrostrictive effect**, in contrast, is a *quadratic* coupling between strain and electric field that exists in **all dielectric materials**, including those with centrosymmetric lattices. The induced strain is proportional to the square of the applied field, $S = Q \cdot E^2$, where $Q$ is the electrostriction coefficient.
>
> Practically, piezoelectric materials are preferred in automotive sensors and injectors due to their linear, predictable response and higher coupling coefficients. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operating principle of **magnetorheological (MR) fluids** and derive the Bingham plastic model equation. Discuss how MR fluids are integrated into semi-active automotive suspension dampers with a suitable block diagram.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **construction and working of a piezoelectric common-rail fuel injector**. Show how a stack of 250 PZT discs generates an 80 µm stroke when energized.

#### (a) Model Solution

**Operating Principle of MR Fluids:**
MR fluids are suspensions of micron-sized, soft-magnetic particles (typically carbonyl iron, 3–10 µm diameter) in a carrier fluid such as mineral oil or silicone. When **no magnetic field** is applied, the particles are randomly distributed and the fluid behaves like a Newtonian liquid with low viscosity. When a **magnetic field H** is applied, the particles align along the field lines forming chain-like columnar structures that resist flow. This produces a **field-dependent yield stress $\tau_y(H)$** within milliseconds.

**Bingham Plastic Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-yield (elastic solid):} \quad \tau < \tau_y(H) \Rightarrow \dot{\gamma} = 0 \\
\text{Post-yield (viscous flow):} \quad \tau \geq \tau_y(H) \Rightarrow \tau = \tau_y(H) + \eta\,\dot{\gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

Where the field-dependent yield stress follows:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_y(H) = \tau_{y0} + k \cdot H^{\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

For typical MR fluids, $\alpha \approx 1$ in the operating range, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_y(H) \approx k \cdot H
\end{aligned}$$

**Integration in Semi-Active Suspension:**

The damper consists of a piston moving in an MR fluid-filled cylinder. An **electromagnetic coil** wound around the piston rod generates an axial magnetic field in the annular gap. The ECU measures body acceleration, computes the optimal damping coefficient using a **Skyhook control algorithm**, and drives a current $I$ through the coil (typically 0–2 A). The resulting field $H$ produces a $\tau_y$ change, altering the damping force $F_{damp} = \tau_y \cdot A_p$ in real time.

> **Block Diagram** — Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for the full Skyhook control loop.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 marks for principle, 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for integration + diagram)**

#### (b) Model Solution

**Construction:** A piezo common-rail injector consists of a multi-layer **PZT stack actuator** (typically 200–300 discs of ~50–80 µm thickness each, electrically in series, mechanically in parallel), housed in a stainless-steel body. The stack is connected via a **coupler** to a hydraulic intensifier that amplifies the small stack displacement into a larger needle-lift stroke. The needle valve controls fuel flow from the high-pressure rail (~2000 bar) into the combustion chamber.

**Working:**
1. ECU commands a voltage pulse (100–200 V) across the PZT stack.
2. Each disc experiences the converse piezoelectric effect: $S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E$.
3. Displacements add up across 250 layers producing **80 µm** of stack motion.
4. The hydraulic coupler amplifies this stroke to ~100–200 µm at the needle.
5. Needle lifts, allowing fuel to spray as 5–7 precisely timed injections per cycle.

**Numerical Verification (as derived in Section 3.1):**
- Total stack thickness = $250 \times 80\;\mu m = 20$ mm
- Field per layer: $E = 10{,}000$ V/m
- Strain per layer: $S_3 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$
- Total stroke: $\Delta L = n \cdot S_3 \cdot t = 80\;\mu m$ ✓
- Blocking force: $F_{block} = 2{,}000$ N (sufficient to overcome return spring)

> **Neat Sketch** — Refer to **Section 4.4** of these notes for the schematic.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 marks for construction, 3 marks for working + sketch, 2 marks for numerical proof)**

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **shape memory effect** in Nickel-Titanium (Nitinol) alloys. With suitable diagrams, describe the **martensite-to-austenite transformation** and its use in an **active seatbelt pretensioner** system.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the principle of **electrochromic materials** in automotive smart glass applications. Compare its switching characteristics with **suspended particle devices (SPD)** and **polymer-dispersed liquid crystal (PDLC)** technologies.

#### (a) Model Solution

**Shape Memory Effect:**
Nitinol is an intermetallic compound of Nickel (55%) and Titanium (45%). It exhibits a reversible solid-state phase transformation between two crystal structures:

- **Martensite (low-temp phase):** monoclinic, soft, easily deformed up to 8% strain.
- **Austenite (high-temp phase):** cubic B2 structure, rigid, recovers original shape.

The transformation is characterized by four temperatures:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_s < M_f < A_s < A_f
\end{aligned}$$

Where $M_s$ and $M_f$ are martensite start/finish, $A_s$ and $A_f$ are austenite start/finish.

**Transformation Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Cooling from Austenite] --> T2[Reaches M_s Temperature]
    T2 --> T3[Martensite Nucleation Begins]
    T3 --> T4[Below M_f Full Martensite Forms]
    T4 --> T5[Apply Stress and Deform]
    T5 --> T6[Heat above A_f]
    T6 --> T7[Reverse Transformation to Austenite]
    T7 --> T8[Original Shape Recovered with Stress]
```

**Active Seatbelt Pretensioner:**

A typical pretensioner uses a **Nitinol wire (~1 mm diameter, ~400 mm long)** wound around a pulley attached to the seatbelt retractor. In normal operation, the wire is in martensitic state (low temp, soft, slack). On crash detection:
1. G-sensor triggers ECU.
2. ECU fires a **current pulse (~30 A, ~100 ms)** through the wire.
3. Joule heating raises the wire temperature from ~25 °C to ~80–90 °C (above $A_f$).
4. Wire undergoes **martensite → austenite** transformation, contracts by ~6%.
5. The contraction rotates the pulley, tightening the seatbelt by ~80 mm within **5 ms**.
6. Belt tension reaches ~4 kN, restraining occupant before secondary motion.

> **Numerical Verification:** As derived in Section 3.2, a Nitinol spring with $\varepsilon_{rec} = 0.06$, $d = 1.5$ mm, $N = 8$ coils generates ~6.2 kN of recovery force after Wahl correction — sufficient for pretensioner duty.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 marks for shape memory effect, 3 marks for transformation + diagram, 2 marks for pretensioner application)**

#### (b) Model Solution

**Electrochromic Smart Glass:**
Electrochromic devices use a thin (200–500 nm) layer of **tungsten trioxide (WO₃)** sandwiched between two transparent conductive electrodes (typically ITO) with an ion-storage layer (NiO or CeO₂) and an electrolyte containing lithium ions (Li⁺) or hydrogen ions (H⁺).

**Operating Reaction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
WO_3 \text{(transparent)} + xLi^+ + xe^- &\leftrightarrow Li_xWO_3 \text{(deep blue)}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying a low DC voltage (1–5 V) drives Li⁺ ions into the WO₃ layer, forming lithium tungsten bronze (LiₓWO₃), which absorbs visible light and darkens the glass. Reversing the polarity bleaches it back to transparency.

**Comparison of Three Smart-Glass Technologies:**

| Parameter | Electrochromic (EC) | Suspended Particle Device (SPD) | Polymer-Dispersed Liquid Crystal (PDLC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Material** | WO₃ thin film | Rod-like particles in liquid | Liquid crystal droplets in polymer matrix |
| **Activation Mechanism** | Ion intercalation | Particle alignment under E-field | LC director reorientation |
| **Voltage** | 1–5 V DC | 100 V AC | 30–100 V AC |
| **Power Consumption** | Very low (< 1 W/m², only during switching) | Moderate (continuous to maintain) | Moderate (continuous to maintain) |
| **Switching Time** | 30–120 s (slow) | 1–3 s (medium) | < 0.1 s (fast) |
| **States** | Variable tint (continuous) | Clear ↔ Opaque (binary-ish) | Clear ↔ Opaque (binary) |
| **Durability** | > 10⁶ cycles | ~10⁵ cycles | ~10⁵ cycles |
| **Best Automotive Use** | Sunroof, panoramic roof | Privacy partition | Concept cars / VR windows |
| **Cost** | High | Moderate | Moderate–High |

> **Conclusion:** Electrochromic glass is best for **thermal-load reduction** in sunroofs (slower switching is acceptable), while SPD and PDLC are favored for **privacy and partition applications** where faster switching matters.

**(7 marks awarded: 3 marks for EC principle + reaction, 4 marks for comparison table + application reasoning)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in piezoelectric derivations — always write `C/N` or `pm/V` next to coefficients. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing direct vs. converse piezoelectric effect** — direct = mechanical → electrical (sensor); converse = electrical → mechanical (actuator). **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Omitting the Wahl correction factor** in helical spring force calculations — students always compute raw $F$ but forget $K_w$. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Writing $d_{33}$ without specifying the direction subscript convention** — IEEE standards require $d_{33}$ meaning field in '3' direction, strain in '3' direction. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Not linking material behavior to a vehicle function** — Module 4 questions *always* want you to say *why* the material is used, not just *what* it does. **(–2 marks)**
> 6. **Confusing ER and MR fluids** — ER fluids respond to *electric* fields (~kV/mm), MR fluids respond to *magnetic* fields (~kA/m). **(–1 mark)**
> 7. **Skipping the blank line before and after `$$` blocks** — breaks markdown rendering and makes equations unreadable. **(–1 mark in presentation)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section the night before the exam — it covers > 80% of expected questions.**

- [x] **Smart Material Definition:** Material that senses a stimulus AND responds reversibly by changing a property (shape, stiffness, color, viscosity).
- [x] **PZT** = Lead Zirconate Titanate; highest $d_{33}$ (~400 pC/N); used in **injectors and knock sensors**.
- [x] **Direct piezo effect:** $D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^T E$ (sensor mode).
- [x] **Converse piezo effect:** $S = s^E \sigma + d \cdot E$ (actuator mode).
- [x] **SMA / Nitinol:** Martensite ↔ Austenite; recoverable strain **up to 8%**; used in **pretensioners, active spoilers**.
- [x] **Magnetostriction:** Terfenol-D gives ~1600 ppm; used in active engine mounts.
- [x] **MR Fluid:** Viscosity varies with magnetic field in **~5 ms**; used in **semi-active dampers** (Cadillac, Audi, Ferrari).
- [x] **Bingham Plastic Model:** $\tau = \tau_y(H) + \eta \dot{\gamma}$; $\tau_y$ dominates over $\eta$.
- [x] **Electrochromic glass:** WO₃ + Li⁺ → LiₓWO₃; switching time 30–120 s; used in **smart sunroofs**.
- [x] **Self-healing polymers:** Microcapsule-based; healing efficiency 75–95%.
- [x] **Piezo stack displacement:** $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V / (n \cdot t) \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V$ (simplified form).
- [x] **SMA recovery stress:** $\sigma_{rec} = E_A \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}$ (~75 GPa × 0.06 ≈ 4.5 GPa).
- [x] **Wahl factor:** $K_w = (4C-1)/(4C-4) + 0.615/C$ — always apply for helical springs.
- [x] **CAN bus integration:** All smart-material subsystems communicate via ECU + CAN, not via direct analog wiring.
- [x] **Skyhook control** is preferred for MR semi-active suspension.
- [x] **Industry metrics to memorize:** MR fluid yield stress ~50–100 kPa; SMA recovery strain ~6%; PZT stack voltage ~200 V; sunroof switching time ~60 s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Take-Away:** Smart materials are not "exotic" anymore — they are *production-grade* and integrated into every modern vehicle subsystem. Your answer should always link the **material → mechanism → vehicle function → regulatory/performance benefit** chain. This four-link chain is the *signature of a top-scoring KTU answer*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future directions in automotive materials: trends in innovation, sustainability, and manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future directions in automotive materials: trends in innovation, sustainability, and manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining the "Future of Automotive Materials"

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Future Automotive Materials** refer to the next generation of engineering substances — metals, polymers, composites, ceramics, and hybrids — that are being designed, processed, and recycled to meet three converging global megatrends:

1. **Innovation** — Multi-material design, smart (functional) materials, nano-engineered alloys.
2. **Sustainability** — Low-carbon footprint, bio-based feedstocks, end-of-life recyclability, circular economy integration.
3. **Manufacturing Transformation** — Adoption of Industry 4.0, additive manufacturing (AM), digital twins, and AI-driven process optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Future automotive materials are *engineered material systems that simultaneously optimize performance (lightweighting, safety, durability), environmental impact (CO₂ lifecycle emissions, recyclability), and manufacturability (formability, joinability, scalability) for next-generation mobility platforms including electric vehicles (EVs), hybrids, and autonomous vehicles.*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Smartphone of Cars":**
> Think of a 1990s car as a basic *Nokia 3310* — heavy steel, simple electronics, no connectivity. Now imagine the future car as a modern *smartphone*: a slim, multi-material chassis (glass-aluminum-polymer), embedded sensors (smart materials), rechargeable battery (new electrochemical materials), and designed to be *eventually disassembled and recycled*. The "future materials" are essentially the **circuit boards, OLED displays, and lithium-ion cells** of this automotive smartphone — each engineered for a specific function while contributing to an overall lightweight, efficient, and recyclable system.

---

### 1.3 The Three Pillars Framework

| Pillar | Core Driver | Key Target by 2030 (Global Avg.) |
|--------|------------|----------------------------------|
| **Innovation** | Performance + Functionality | Specific strength > 200 MPa·cm³/g |
| **Sustainability** | Net-Zero Commitments | ≥ 25% recycled content in new vehicles |
| **Manufacturing** | Industry 4.0 / AM | 30% of structural parts via AM |

> [!NOTE]
> The European Union's **End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive** mandates that by 2030, **95% of a vehicle's mass** must be recyclable, reusable, or recoverable. This single regulation is reshaping the entire automotive material supply chain.

---

### 1.4 Key Conceptual Vocabulary (KTU Terminology)

- **Lightweighting** — Reducing vehicle mass to improve fuel economy (ICE) or range (EV). Roughly **10% mass reduction ≈ 6–8% efficiency gain** for ICE vehicles.
- **Multi-Material Design (MMD)** — Strategic use of *the right material in the right place* (e.g., carbon fiber roof, aluminum hood, steel B-pillar).
- **Circular Economy** — A closed-loop material flow where vehicles are designed for disassembly, remanufacturing, and recycling.
- **Smart Materials** — Materials that respond to external stimuli (stress, temperature, magnetic field) — e.g., Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), Piezoelectric polymers.
- **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** — Design philosophy where every output (waste) becomes an input for another product cycle.
- **Specific Property Ratio** — A normalized material index (e.g., strength/density) used in Ashby-style material selection.
- **Mass De-compounding** — In EVs, every 1 kg saved on structure allows ~1.1 kg reduction in battery mass (because battery capacity can be reduced).

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics (Bolded)

- Density of reference steel: **7850 kg/m³**
- Density of reference aluminum: **2700 kg/m³**
- Density of reference CFRP: **1550–1600 kg/m³**
- Density of reference magnesium: **1740 kg/m³**
- 1 ton of CO₂ ≈ driving an average ICE car **~4500 km**
- Specific stiffness benchmark: **E/ρ > 25 GPa·cm³/g** is considered "aerospace-grade"
- Vehicle CO₂ target (EU 2025–2030): **< 49 g CO₂/km** (fleet average)

---

### 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Context

The KTU 2024 Automobile Engineering syllabus explicitly emphasizes that future automotive engineers must be *material-literate across all three pillars*. The 14-mark and 3-mark questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) frequently test the student's ability to:
- Compare material families using Ashby indices.
- Justify material selection for a specific automotive sub-system.
- Evaluate the sustainability trade-offs (e.g., CFRP vs. Al vs. AHSS).
- Discuss manufacturing compatibility (e.g., can this material be 3D-printed?).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students who *only memorize material properties* lose marks. You must connect every material to its **sustainability metric** AND its **manufacturing process** to score full marks in Part B questions.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Innovation Frontier — What is "New" in Automotive Materials?

The innovation pillar is driven by three converging sub-trends:

#### 2.1.1 Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS) — 3rd Generation
- Tensile strength: **1200–2000 MPa**
- Used in **~30%** of modern BIW (Body-in-White).
- Enables *thinning* of panels (1.0 mm → 0.7 mm) while maintaining crash energy absorption.

#### 2.1.2 Aluminum Alloys (5xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series)
- **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)**: Body panels, hoods, doors — *age-hardenable, good formability*.
- **7xxx (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu)**: Aerospace-grade, used in premium EV battery enclosures.
- Audi A8 pioneered *aluminum spaceframe* in 1994.

#### 2.1.3 Magnesium Alloys (Lightest Structural Metal)
- Density: **1740 kg/m³** (~35% lighter than Al, ~78% lighter than steel).
- Challenge: **Poor corrosion resistance** (galvanic coupling with steel) and **limited cold formability**.

#### 2.1.4 Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymers (CFRP)
- Specific strength: ~**5× that of mild steel**.
- Cost: ~**10–20×** that of steel — a major adoption barrier.
- Used in BMW i3, i8, Alfa Romeo 4C, and supercar monocoques.

#### 2.1.5 Natural Fiber Composites (NFC) — Sustainability Play
- Flax, hemp, jute fibers in PP/PET matrix.
- Used in **door panels, parcel shelves, seat backs** of Mercedes A-Class, BMW 7-Series.
- **CO₂ footprint: 60–80% lower** than glass fiber composites.

#### 2.1.6 Smart / Functional Materials
| Material Type | Stimulus | Response | Automotive Use |
|---------------|----------|----------|----------------|
| Shape Memory Alloy (Nitinol) | Temperature | Shape recovery | Active crumple zones |
| Piezoelectric polymers | Mechanical strain | Voltage | Energy harvesting mats |
| Magnetostrictive materials | Magnetic field | Strain | Active vibration dampers |
| Self-healing polymers | Crack initiation | Microcapsule release | Anti-corrosion coatings |

---

### 2.2 The Sustainability Frontier — Metrics that Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainability is NOT just "recycling".** A KTU Board Examiner expects you to evaluate materials across **all four dimensions** of the *4R framework*: **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover.**

#### 2.2.1 Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) — The Cradle-to-Grave View

A typical automotive material is evaluated through five LCA stages:

1. **Raw material extraction** (mining, refining)
2. **Material processing** (smelting, polymerization)
3. **Component manufacturing** (stamping, casting, forging)
4. **Use phase** (fuel/electricity consumption over 200,000 km)
5. **End-of-life** (disposal, recycling, incineration)

The *Embedded Carbon* metric is what students most often get tested on:

$$
\text{Embedded Carbon (kg CO}_2\text{eq/kg)} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions of stage } i
$$

#### 2.2.2 Embodied Energy Comparison (Reference Table)

| Material | Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | CO₂ Footprint (kg CO₂/kg) |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Mild steel | **20–25** | **1.8–2.2** |
| Aluminum (primary) | **200–220** | **11–13** |
| Aluminum (recycled) | **8–12** | **0.5–0.8** |
| Magnesium | **250–300** | **18–25** |
| CFRP (virgin) | **180–240** | **15–22** |
| CFRP (recycled) | **50–80** | **4–6** |
| Natural fiber composite | **15–30** | **1–2** |
| Engineering plastic (PA66) | **100–115** | **6–7** |

> [!NOTE]
> The 4th column values are the ones KTU examiners LOVE to test. A common trap: students claim "aluminum is greener" without realizing primary aluminum is **6× more carbon-intensive** than steel — *only recycled aluminum wins on LCA.*

---

### 2.3 The Manufacturing Frontier — Industry 4.0 + AM

#### 2.3.1 Additive Manufacturing (AM) Classifications (ISO/ASTM 52900)

| AM Process | Material Form | Typical Use |
|------------|----------------|-------------|
| Powder Bed Fusion (PBF / SLM) | Metal powders (AlSi10Mg, 316L, Ti6Al4V) | EV motor housings, complex brackets |
| Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | Wire or powder | Large structural repair |
| Binder Jetting | Sand, metal powder | Sand casting cores, prototypes |
| Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) | Thermoplastics (ABS, PLA, PA) | Jigs, fixtures, interior trim |
| Material Extrusion (pellet) | Reinforced polymers | Structural components (UD tapes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bugatti's 3D-printed brake caliper** is the canonical exam example: made from **Ti6Al4V** via SLM, it is **40% lighter** than the aluminum equivalent and was the first 3D-printed functional automotive safety component in production.

#### 2.3.2 Digital Twin + AI Integration
- Real-time monitoring of material microstructure during forming.
- Closed-loop control of welding parameters in multi-material joints.
- Predictive quality: defect detection using CNN-based vision systems.

---

### 2.4 The Ashby Material Selection Logic (Foundational Theory)

For a stiffness-critical (e.g., suspension arm) component, the **material index** is:

$$
M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}
$$

For a strength-critical (e.g., crash rail) component:

$$
M = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2/3}}{\rho}
$$

For an *energy-absorbing* crash structure:

$$
M = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** A higher M value means you get more stiffness/strength per unit mass. The Ashby charts visually rank materials on these indices, and the chosen index guides you to the optimal material family for the application.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are testable in KTU ESE — both 3-mark and 14-mark problems.**

| # | Formula / Index | Expression | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Specific Strength | $SS = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$ | Crash structures, fasteners |
| 2 | Specific Stiffness | $SSt = E / \rho$ | Chassis, suspension |
| 3 | Stiffness Index (beam) | $E^{1/3}/\rho$ | Lightweight beams |
| 4 | Strength Index (panel) | $\sigma_{y}^{2/3}/\rho$ | Body panels |
| 5 | Crash Energy Index | $\sigma_{y}/\rho$ | Energy absorbers |
| 6 | Mass Reduction | $\Delta m = m_{steel} - m_{new}$ | Lightweighting gain |
| 7 | % Mass Reduction | $\%m = (\Delta m / m_{steel}) \times 100$ | Lightweighting gain |
| 8 | CO₂ Saved (Use Phase) | $\Delta CO_2 = \text{CO}_{2,base} \cdot \%m \cdot \text{factor}$ | Sustainability metric |
| 9 | Ashby Plot Slope (log) | $n = \log M / \log \rho$ | Material ranking |
| 10 | Material Cost Index | $C_{ind} = C_{v,m} / M$ | Cost-performance trade-off |

*(where $C_{v,m}$ = cost per unit volume per unit mass, and $M$ = relevant material index)*

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEMs use Specific Strength/Stiffness** to rank candidate materials for body-in-white (BIW) panels.
- **Regulators use CO₂/km** (g/km) — this is why lightweighting is *legally* enforced.
- **Process engineers use the Ashby index** to shortlist 3–5 candidate materials from 100,000+ before doing detailed FEA.
- **Procurement teams use Cost Index** to negotiate with suppliers when two materials perform similarly.

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU 14-mark answer, always follow this structure: **(1) State the design requirement → (2) Derive the relevant Ashby index → (3) Compare 3–4 candidate materials on a table → (4) Justify the final selection with cost + sustainability + manufacturing constraints.**

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Reduction Calculation Using Specific Strength

**Problem Context (KTU Standard 7-mark sub-question):**
A 1200 kg steel-intensive BIW uses 35% mild steel (ρ = 7850 kg/m³) by mass. If the steel is replaced by 6061-T6 aluminum (ρ = 2700 kg/m³, σ_y = 240 MPa) and the required strength is identical, calculate the % mass reduction.

> **Note on stiffness-equivalence:** The Ashby "panel" index $\sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$ governs the *thickness-scaled* performance. We will derive both the *strength-only* and *stiffness-equivalent* cases.

#### Step 1 — Identify the original steel mass

$$
m_{steel} = 0.35 \times 1200 = 420 \text{ kg}
$$

The volume occupied by the steel is:

$$
V_{steel} = \frac{m_{steel}}{\rho_{steel}} = \frac{420}{7850} = 0.0535 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the "Strength-Only" Sub-stitution Logic

If we require the **same yield force** in the new material as the original, the cross-sectional area of the new material must scale as:

$$
A_{new} = A_{steel} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{y,steel}}{\sigma_{y,Al}}
$$

*Textual explanation:* The yield force of a part is $F_y = A \cdot \sigma_y$. To keep $F_y$ constant, the new area must compensate for the change in yield strength.

For the steel baseline, $\sigma_{y,steel} = 250$ MPa (typical mild steel).

$$
A_{new} = A_{steel} \cdot \frac{250}{240} = 1.0417 \cdot A_{steel}
$$

So the aluminum needs ~4.17% *more* cross-sectional area than the steel to carry the same load.

#### Step 3 — Calculate the New Volume

$$
V_{new} = V_{steel} \cdot \frac{A_{new}}{A_{steel}} = 0.0535 \times 1.0417 = 0.0557 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate the New Mass

$$
m_{new} = V_{new} \times \rho_{Al} = 0.0557 \times 2700 = 150.4 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate the Mass Reduction

$$
\Delta m = m_{steel} - m_{new} = 420 - 150.4 = 269.6 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\%m_{reduction} = \frac{\Delta m}{m_{steel}} \times 100 = \frac{269.6}{420} \times 100 \approx 64.2\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Even though aluminum has a lower yield strength, the 65% density reduction far outweighs the 4% strength deficit. This is *why* lightweighting with aluminum is so attractive.

#### Step 6 — Apply De-compounding (for EV context)

If this is an EV, the saved structural mass allows the battery to be reduced too. Using a de-compounding factor $f = 1.1$ (industry standard for EVs):

$$
m_{total,EV} = m_{structure} \times 1.1 - 0.1 \times m_{battery,ref}
$$

For a reference 500 kg battery pack:

$$
m_{saved,total} = 269.6 \times 1.1 = 296.6 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** For ICE vehicles, use a de-compounding factor of **1.0** (no battery). For EVs, use **1.1–1.3** depending on the powertrain architecture.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Embedded CO₂ Savings for a Material Switch

**Problem Context (KTU Standard 7-mark sub-question):**
A 420 kg mild steel component (CO₂ footprint: 1.99 kg CO₂/kg) is replaced by 150.4 kg of aluminum (CO₂ footprint: 12.5 kg CO₂/kg primary, 0.6 kg CO₂/kg recycled). Calculate the embodied CO₂ change.

#### Step 1 — Steel Embedded CO₂

$$
CO_{2,steel} = 420 \times 1.99 = 835.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq}
$$

#### Step 2 — Primary Aluminum Embedded CO₂

$$
CO_{2,Al,primary} = 150.4 \times 12.5 = 1880 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq}
$$

#### Step 3 — Recycled Aluminum Embedded CO₂

$$
CO_{2,Al,recycled} = 150.4 \times 0.6 = 90.24 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq}
$$

#### Step 4 — CO₂ Change — Primary vs. Recycled

For **primary aluminum**:

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 1880 - 835.8 = +1044.2 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq (WORSENED)}
$$

For **recycled aluminum**:

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 90.24 - 835.8 = -745.56 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq (IMPROVED)}
$$

#### Step 5 — Use-Phase Recovery (to break even with primary Al)

If the EV is driven for 150,000 km and saves 0.05 L/km per 100 kg of mass saved (typical for EV), the use-phase savings are:

$$
\text{Fuel saved} = 0.05 \times 2.696 = 0.1348 \text{ L/100km}
$$

$$
\text{Liters over 150,000 km} = 0.1348 \times 1500 = 202.2 \text{ L}
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2\text{ saved (use phase)} = 202.2 \times 2.31 = 467.1 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{eq}
$$

**Break-even time** (primary Al):

$$
t_{break-even} = \frac{1044.2}{467.1 / 150000} \text{ km} = 335{,}400 \text{ km}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** A car driven 150,000 km *will not* break even with primary aluminum. Only **recycled aluminum** provides a net lifecycle benefit. This is the *single most-tested* sustainability concept in KTU exams.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ashby Material Index for a Lightweight Beam

**Problem Context (KTU Standard 7-mark sub-question):**
A cantilever spring (automotive suspension leaf) must be **as light as possible** while meeting a stiffness requirement. Derive the material index and rank 4 candidate materials.

#### Step 1 — Stiffness Constraint

For a beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$, the deflection under load $F$ is:

$$
\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}, \quad I = \frac{b t^3}{12}
$$

Combining these and solving for the required volume (or mass) gives the standard Ashby derivation.

$$
\delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E b t^3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E b \delta} \right)^{1/3}
$$

#### Step 2 — Mass Equation

The mass of the beam is:

$$
m = \rho \cdot b \cdot t \cdot L = \rho b L \left( \frac{4 F L^3}{E b \delta} \right)^{1/3}
$$

Simplifying by gathering the design constants:

$$
m = \left( \frac{4 F L^4}{\delta} \right)^{1/3} \cdot b^{2/3} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Identify the Material Index

Since $(4FL^4/\delta)^{1/3}$ and $b^{2/3}$ are design constants, **minimizing $m$ is equivalent to minimizing:**

$$
\frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}} \quad \text{or equivalently maximizing} \quad M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** This derivation shows that for a stiffness-limited lightweight beam, the relevant index is $E^{1/3}/\rho$ — *not* simply $E/\rho$. This is a classic KTU trap question.

#### Step 4 — Numerical Comparison of 4 Materials

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $E^{1/3}$ (GPa$^{1/3}$) | $M = E^{1/3}/\rho$ | Rank |
|----------|-----------|----------------|-------------------------|--------------------|------|
| Mild Steel | 200 | 7850 | 5.848 | 0.000745 | 3 |
| 6061-T6 Al | 69 | 2700 | 4.102 | 0.001519 | 1 |
| Ti6Al4V | 114 | 4430 | 4.848 | 0.001094 | 2 |
| CFRP (UD) | 135 | 1600 | 5.130 | 0.003206 | ★ Best |

**Result:** CFRP wins on this index, but in practice the cost factor (CFRP is ~10–20× steel) often down-ranks it for mass-market vehicles. The KTU 14-mark answer should always include a *cost-sensitivity check*.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Circular Material Flow Rate (Recyclability)

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):**
A car has 1500 kg total mass. 25% is aluminum (primary), 50% is steel, 10% is plastic, 8% is glass, 7% is rubber/elastomers. If the recycling rates are: Al = 75%, Steel = 90%, Plastic = 30%, Glass = 70%, Rubber = 10%, calculate the total recovered material mass.

#### Step 1 — Mass of Each Material Stream

$$
m_{Al} = 0.25 \times 1500 = 375 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{steel} = 0.75 \times 1500 = 750 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{plastic} = 0.15 \times 1500 = 150 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{glass} = 0.08 \times 1500 = 120 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{rubber} = 0.07 \times 1500 = 105 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 2 — Mass Recovered from Each Stream

$$
m_{Al,rec} = 0.75 \times 375 = 281.25 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{steel,rec} = 0.90 \times 750 = 675 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{plastic,rec} = 0.30 \times 150 = 45 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{glass,rec} = 0.70 \times 120 = 84 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{rubber,rec} = 0.10 \times 105 = 10.5 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 3 — Total Recovery and Recovery Rate

$$
m_{total,rec} = 281.25 + 675 + 45 + 84 + 10.5 = 1095.75 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{1095.75}{1500} \times 100 = 73.05\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** A common mistake is summing the percentages (75+90+30+70+10 = 275%) and then dividing by 5. The correct approach is the **mass-weighted** average, exactly as shown above. The KTU 2024 Scheme values students who know the difference between *mass-weighted* and *unweighted* averages.

---

### 3.5 Summary of Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam day, memorize these four derivations verbatim — they cover ~80% of Module 4 numerical questions.**

| # | Topic | Key Formula | KTU Test Probability |
|---|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Mass reduction by material switch | $m_{new} = V_{steel} \cdot (\sigma_{y,steel}/\sigma_{y,new}) \cdot \rho_{new}$ | **High** |
| 2 | CO₂ lifecycle break-even | $t_{BE} = \Delta CO_{2,embed} / \Delta CO_{2,use-km}$ | **High** |
| 3 | Ashby index for a beam | $M = E^{1/3}/\rho$ | **High** |
| 4 | Mass-weighted recyclability | $\%R = \sum (m_i \cdot r_i) / \sum m_i$ | **Medium** |
| 5 | Specific strength ranking | $SS = \sigma/\rho$ | **High** |
| 6 | De-compounding factor (EV) | $m_{total,saved} = \Delta m \cdot f_{decompound}$ | **Medium** |
