## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Specific Strength" of an automotive material. Why is it considered the most important material index for crash energy-absorbing structures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Specific strength is the ratio of a material's ultimate tensile strength to its density:

$$
SS = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}
$$

It is measured in **MPa·cm³/g** (or **kN·m/kg**). It is the dominant index for crash structures because:
1. Energy absorption per unit mass is governed by $\sigma_{y}/\rho$ — a high specific strength allows thinner, lighter sections to absorb the same impact energy.
2. Crash structures must be **light (for fuel economy)** AND **strong (for occupant safety)** — specific strength is the only index that captures both.

For example, CFRP has a specific strength of **~1250 kN·m/kg**, vs. mild steel at **~63 kN·m/kg** — about 20× higher, justifying its use in F1 chassis and EV battery enclosures.

*(1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for "why" in crash context, 1 mark for example/quantification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four advanced automotive materials used for lightweighting. Compare their typical density ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | % Lighter than Steel |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|
| Mild Steel | **7850** | Baseline (0%) |
| AHSS | **7800–7850** | ~0–1% (lightweighting via thinning) |
| Aluminum 6xxx | **2700** | ~66% lighter |
| Magnesium AZ91 | **1740** | ~78% lighter |
| CFRP | **1550–1600** | ~80% lighter |

Other materials for full credit: Natural fiber composites (~1200–1400), Engineering plastics (PA66 = 1140), Carbon-fiber SMC (~1450).

*(1 mark for naming 4 materials, 1 mark for density table, 1 mark for comparison or "% lighter" computation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides an internal choice. Students must attempt one full question (7 + 7 = 14 marks). Sub-part (a) and (b) are **compulsory and linked** — you cannot mix.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *With a neat sketch, explain the Ashby material selection methodology. Using this methodology, derive the material index for a **stiffness-limited lightweight beam**. Rank the following four materials using this index: Mild Steel, 6061-T6 Aluminum, Ti-6Al-4V, and unidirectional CFRP. Discuss the manufacturing and sustainability implications of your top-ranked material in an automotive context.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Compare the embodied energy and CO₂ footprint of primary aluminum, recycled aluminum, mild steel, and CFRP (virgin). Why is recycled aluminum considered a "double-win" material in automotive sustainability? Include a numerical example where a 400 kg steel component is replaced by 100 kg of recycled aluminum, and show that the net CO₂ is reduced. Discuss the EU ELV Directive's impact on automotive material selection.* **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Solution to (a) — Ashby Methodology + Beam Selection

**Step 1 — Methodology (3 marks):**
Ashby material selection is a 4-step process:
1. **Translate** the design requirement into a *function*, *constraints*, *objectives*, and *free variables*.
2. **Screen** candidates using property limits (e.g., $T_{max}$, corrosion resistance).
3. **Rank** candidates using a *material index* derived from the objective + constraints.
4. **Document** the final choice, factoring in cost, manufacturability, and sustainability.

**Step 2 — Derive the Material Index (2 marks):**
For a stiffness-limited beam of length $L$ and rectangular cross-section, the deflection is:

$$
\delta = \frac{FL^3}{3EI}, \quad I = \frac{bt^3}{12}
$$

Solving for the thickness:

$$
t = \left( \frac{4FL^3}{Eb\delta} \right)^{1/3}
$$

The mass of the beam is $m = \rho b t L$. Substituting and simplifying:

$$
m = \left( \frac{4FL^4}{\delta} \right)^{1/3} \cdot b^{2/3} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}
$$

Since the bracketed term and $b^{2/3}$ are design constants, the mass is minimized when:

$$
M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho} \text{ is MAXIMIZED}
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the Four Materials (2 marks):**

| Material | E (GPa) | ρ (kg/m³) | E^(1/3) | M = E^(1/3)/ρ | Rank |
|----------|---------|-----------|---------|---------------|------|
| Mild Steel | 200 | 7850 | 5.85 | 7.45 × 10⁻⁴ | 3 |
| 6061-T6 Al | 69 | 2700 | 4.10 | 1.52 × 10⁻³ | 2 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 114 | 4430 | 4.85 | 1.09 × 10⁻³ | 4 |
| CFRP (UD) | 135 | 1600 | 5.13 | 3.21 × 10⁻³ | **1** |

**CFRP wins on index, but the manufacturing + sustainability check (qualifying point — 0 marks allocated, 0 marks deducted) flags the following:**

- **Manufacturing:** CFRP is hard to recycle, difficult to repair, and slow to produce (autoclave cure time 4–8 hours). Joining to steel/Al requires adhesive bonding or hybrid fasteners.
- **Sustainability:** Virgin CFRP has 15–22 kg CO₂/kg. Without a recycling stream, it fails the EU ELV Directive's 95% recovery target.

In practice, **6061-T6 aluminum** is the *real-world* optimal choice for a mass-market automotive beam, balancing performance, cost, and recyclability.

---

#### Solution to (b) — CO₂ Comparison + Numerical Example

**Step 1 — Embodied Energy & CO₂ Table (3 marks):**

| Material | Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | CO₂ (kg CO₂/kg) |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Mild Steel | 20–25 | 1.99 |
| Primary Aluminum | 200–220 | 12.5 |
| Recycled Aluminum | 8–12 | 0.6 |
| CFRP (virgin) | 180–240 | 18 |

**Step 2 — "Double-Win" Argument (2 marks):**
Recycled aluminum is a "double-win" because:
1. **Performance win:** Same density and mechanical properties as primary Al → full lightweighting benefit (~65% mass reduction).
2. **Environmental win:** Only 5% of the energy and 5% of the CO₂ of primary Al → huge LCA improvement.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (2 marks):**
- Steel component: 400 kg × 1.99 kg CO₂/kg = **796 kg CO₂eq**
- Recycled Al replacement: 100 kg × 0.6 kg CO₂/kg = **60 kg CO₂eq**
- Net reduction: 796 − 60 = **736 kg CO₂eq saved (per car, embodied phase only)**

This is roughly equivalent to driving an ICE car **~3300 km** (at 220 g CO₂/km).

**Step 4 — EU ELV Directive Impact:**
- Requires **95% recovery** (recycling + energy recovery) of vehicle mass by 2030.
- Forces OEMs to choose materials with established recycling streams — favoring steel and recycled Al over CFRP.
- Drives "design for disassembly" and recycled-content mandates (25% by 2030).

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *Explain the four pillars of the Industry 4.0 framework (interconnectivity, information transparency, technical assistance, decentralized decisions). Discuss how each pillar transforms automotive material selection and manufacturing. Include specific examples of additive manufacturing (AM) processes — name at least 3 (e.g., SLM, DED, FDM) and identify the typical material and automotive component for each.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A 1500 kg passenger car has the following material composition: 30% AHSS, 25% primary aluminum, 15% mild steel, 10% engineering plastic, 10% rubber/elastomer, 5% glass, 5% other. Calculate the total recovered mass if the recycling rates are: AHSS = 88%, primary Al = 75%, mild steel = 90%, plastic = 30%, rubber = 10%, glass = 70%, other = 5%. If the OEM targets a 90% recovery rate, by how much does the current design fall short? Suggest two design changes to close the gap.* **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Solution to (a) — Industry 4.0 Pillars in Automotive Materials

**Step 1 — Four Pillars (3 marks):**

1. **Interconnectivity** — Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) connect machines, sensors, and products. *Example:* Real-time monitoring of sheet metal formability via in-die strain gauges.
2. **Information Transparency** — Data from sensors creates a digital copy of the physical world. *Example:* Material traceability using blockchain-based "material passports" for every batch of steel/Al.
3. **Technical Assistance** — Systems support humans in decision-making. *Example:* AI-based defect detection in CFRP layup using CNN vision (96%+ accuracy).
4. **Decentralized Decisions** — Cyber-physical systems make decisions autonomously. *Example:* Self-optimizing welding robots adjusting current/voltage in real-time for AHSS-Al hybrid joints.

**Step 2 — Three AM Processes (4 marks):**

| AM Process | Material | Automotive Component |
|------------|----------|----------------------|
| Selective Laser Melting (SLM) | AlSi10Mg, 316L, Ti6Al4V | EV motor housing, Bugatti brake caliper |
| Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | Wire-fed steel, Ti | Large structural repair, custom axle shafts |
| Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) | ABS, PA, CF-reinforced PA | Interior trim, jigs and fixtures, prototypes |

A fourth process for extra credit: **Binder Jetting** (sand cores, prototype engine blocks).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Mass-Weighted Recovery Calculation

**Step 1 — Mass Per Stream:**

| Material | % | Mass (kg) |
|----------|----|----------|
| AHSS | 30 | 450 |
| Primary Al | 25 | 375 |
| Mild Steel | 15 | 225 |
| Plastic | 10 | 150 |
| Rubber | 10 | 150 |
| Glass | 5 | 75 |
| Other | 5 | 75 |

**Step 2 — Recovered Mass (4 marks):**

| Material | Mass (kg) | Rate (%) | Recovered (kg) |
|----------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| AHSS | 450 | 88 | 396.0 |
| Primary Al | 375 | 75 | 281.25 |
| Mild Steel | 225 | 90 | 202.5 |
| Plastic | 150 | 30 | 45.0 |
| Rubber | 150 | 10 | 15.0 |
| Glass | 75 | 70 | 52.5 |
| Other | 75 | 5 | 3.75 |
| **Total** | **1500** | — | **996.0** |

**Step 3 — Recovery Rate:**

$$
\%R = \frac{996.0}{1500} \times 100 = 66.4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Gap Analysis (2 marks):**
Target = 90% = 1350 kg
Shortfall = 1350 − 996 = **354 kg**

**Step 5 — Design Changes to Close the Gap (1 mark):**

1. **Switch primary aluminum to recycled aluminum (recovery = 95%):** Saves $(95 - 75)/100 \times 375 = 75$ kg.
2. **Replace virgin engineering plastic with bio-based / recycled-content plastic (recovery = 60%):** Saves $(60 - 30)/100 \times 150 = 45$ kg.
3. **Adopt modular rubber components** with thermoplastic elastomers (TPE) instead of cross-linked rubber — improves recovery from 10% to ~60%. Saves $(60 - 10)/100 \times 150 = 75$ kg.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not average the percentages** (75 + 90 + 30...)/7 — this gives ~52%, which is wrong. Always use **mass-weighted** average.
> 2. **Do not forget the 5% "other" category** — some students omit it, losing 0.5 marks.
> 3. **For the 7-mark derivation, show every multiplication step** — KTU 2024 valuation penalizes "calculator-jump" steps.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision — read this list before the exam.**

- ✅ **Specific Strength = σ/ρ** and **Specific Stiffness = E/ρ** are the two foundational indices.
- ✅ **Ashby index for stiffness-limited beam** is $E^{1/3}/\rho$, **NOT** $E/\rho$ — common trap.
- ✅ **De-compounding factor** for EV: 1.1 to 1.3; for ICE: 1.0.
- ✅ **Primary aluminum** is 6× more CO₂-intensive than steel — only **recycled Al** is a sustainability win.
- ✅ **Mass-weighted recyclability** = Σ(mass × rate) / Σ(mass). Do not use simple % averages.
- ✅ **EU ELV Directive 2030 target:** 95% recovery, 85% recycling, 25% recycled content.
- ✅ **Bugatti's 3D-printed Ti6Al4V brake caliper** is the canonical "AM in production" example.
- ✅ **Natural fiber composites** (flax, hemp) have 60–80% lower CO₂ than glass fiber composites.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 = CPS + IoT + AI + Digital Twin.** Memorize the 4 pillars.
- ✅ **AM processes:** SLM (metal powder bed), DED (wire/powder feed), FDM (thermoplastic), Binder Jetting (sand/metal).
- ✅ **Smart materials examples:** Nitinol (SMA), PVDF (Piezoelectric), Terfenol-D (Magnetostrictive).
- ✅ **Lightweighting rule of thumb:** 10% mass reduction ≈ 6–8% efficiency gain (ICE) or 5–6% range gain (EV).
- ✅ **CFRP** is ~20× stronger (specific) than steel but ~10–20× costlier and *hard to recycle*.
- ✅ **Magnesium** is the lightest structural metal but suffers from **galvanic corrosion** in multi-material joints.
- ✅ **Multi-Material Design (MMD)** is the *central philosophy* of future BIW — never say "all-aluminum" or "all-steel" for future cars.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4 | KTU 2024 SCHEME | PEAUT745**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Research and development opportunities in new materials and technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT745 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE MANUFACTURING MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Research and development opportunities in new materials and technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Research and Development (R&D) opportunities in new materials and technologies** within the automotive domain refer to the systematic, multidisciplinary pursuit of novel material compositions, microstructural architectures, processing methodologies, and integration strategies that enable next-generation vehicles to satisfy concurrent performance imperatives: **mass reduction (lightweighting)**, **electrification compatibility**, **enhanced crashworthiness**, **sustainability through recyclability and bio-based sourcing**, and **functional intelligence (sensing, self-repair, adaptivity)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of *Materials Science*, *Manufacturing Process Engineering*, and *Sustainable Design*. It is the capstone of Module 4 and consolidates knowledge from Modules 1–3 (metals, polymers, composites) into a future-oriented framework.

The R&D landscape is typically classified into four intersecting pillars:

1. **Material Innovation** – discovery and engineering of new alloy systems, polymer matrices, composites, and hybrids.
2. **Process Innovation** – additive manufacturing, friction stir processing, severe plastic deformation, in-situ alloying.
3. **Functional Innovation** – smart materials, embedded sensors, self-healing systems.
4. **Sustainability Innovation** – bio-based feedstocks, closed-loop recycling, hydrogen economy materials.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Evolving Kitchen" of an Automotive Chef**
>
> Imagine a master chef preparing a futuristic dish. The **classic recipe** (mild steel body, cast iron engine) is heavy, energy-hungry, and wasteful. The chef is now:
> - **Substituting ingredients** (steel $\rightarrow$ carbon-fiber composites, lead-acid $\rightarrow$ lithium-ion solid-state batteries).
> - **Inventing new cooking techniques** (layered deposition like 3D printing instead of casting).
> - **Adding flavour modifiers** (smart coatings that heal scratches, sensors that warn of fatigue).
> - **Sourcing locally** (bio-based polymers, recycled aluminum).
>
> R&D in automotive materials is exactly this — continuously rewriting the recipe book while honouring the same taste (safety, performance, cost).

### 1.3 Defining Metrics of the R&D Domain

- **Specific Strength (SS):** ratio of tensile strength to density, $\sigma/\rho$, with units **MPa·cm³/g** (or equivalently kN·m/kg).
- **Specific Stiffness (SM):** ratio of Young's modulus to density, $E/\rho$, with units **GPa·cm³/g**.
- **Mass Reduction Potential (MRP):** percentage weight saved when replacing baseline mild steel.
- **Technology Readiness Level (TRL):** a 1–9 scale (NASA/DoD standard) denoting maturity from basic research (**TRL 1**) to commercial deployment (**TRL 9**).
- **Carbon Footprint (CFP):** kg CO₂-equivalent emitted per kg of material produced.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** When asked about *R&D opportunities*, always frame the answer using the **TRL framework** and the **lightweighting–electrification–sustainability triad**. Examiners reward structured, multi-axis answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars — Structured Analytical Breakdown

#### **Pillar 1: Material Innovation**

- **Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS) — Gen 3 & Gen 4**
  - *Why:* Maintains existing steel manufacturing infrastructure; reduces mass by 25–35 % vs. mild steel.
  - *How:* Multi-phase microstructures (ferrite, bainite, martensite, retained austenite) tailored via **quenching and partitioning (Q&P)** and **TRIP-assisted annealing**.

- **Aluminium Alloys (5xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series)**
  - *Why:* Density = **2.7 g/cm³** (≈ 1/3 of steel); excellent corrosion resistance.
  - *How:* Tailored precipitation hardening; recent focus on **Al-Mg-Si (6xxx)** for body-in-white (e.g., Ford F-150).

- **Magnesium Alloys (AZ91, WE43, AM-series)**
  - *Why:* Density = **1.74 g/cm³** — the lightest structural metal.
  - *How:* Cast Mg alloys dominate; wrought Mg is the active R&D frontier (improved ductility via rare-earth additions).

- **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymers (CFRP)**
  - *Why:* Specific strength up to **5× that of steel**; specific stiffness up to **8× that of steel**.
  - *How:* Pyrolysis of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) precursor; alignment of graphite crystallites along fibre axis.

- **Natural Fibre Composites (NFC)**
  - *Why:* Renewable, low embodied energy, end-of-life biodegradable.
  - *How:* Flax, hemp, jute, kenaf fibres embedded in PP, PLA, or epoxy matrices.

#### **Pillar 2: Process Innovation**

- **Additive Manufacturing (AM) / 3D Printing**
  - *Why:* Topology-optimized, near-net-shape parts; minimal material waste (buy-to-fly ratio $\rightarrow$ **~1**).
  - *How:* Powder-bed fusion (SLM, EBM), directed energy deposition (DED), binder jetting for sand moulds.

- **Friction Stir Processing (FSP)**
  - *Why:* Grain refinement to sub-micron levels; in-situ composite fabrication.
  - *How:* Rotating non-consumable tool locally plasticizes the workpiece without melting.

- **Severe Plastic Deformation (SPD)** — ECAP, HPT
  - *Why:* Bulk nanostructuring for ultra-high strength without compositional change.

#### **Pillar 3: Functional Innovation**

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)** — *Nitinol (Ni-Ti)*: recovers strain up to **8 %** upon heating.
- **Piezoelectric Materials** — PZT, PVDF: convert mechanical stress $\rightarrow$ electrical signal (energy harvesting, structural health monitoring).
- **Self-Healing Polymers** — microencapsulated healing agents (dicyclopentadiene + Grubbs catalyst) autonomously repair cracks.
- **Magnetorheological Fluids (MRF)** — viscosity changes **1000×** under magnetic field; used in adaptive dampers.

#### **Pillar 4: Sustainability Innovation**

- **Closed-Loop Recycling:** End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive mandates **95 % recyclability** by mass.
- **Bio-Based Polymers:** PLA, PHB, cellulose acetate from agricultural waste.
- **Hydrogen Economy Materials:** Metal hydrides (LaNi₅, MgH₂) for solid-state storage; proton-exchange membranes (Nafion™) for fuel cells.
- **Solid-State Battery Materials:** Sulfide/oxide electrolytes (e.g., Li₆PS₅Cl, LLZO) replacing liquid electrolytes — enables higher energy density (**> 400 Wh/kg**) and intrinsic safety.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the dimensionless ratios and the Ashby material indices. KTU frequently tests comparative material selection.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbolic Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_u / \rho$ | Strength per unit weight | kN·m/kg |
| 2 | Specific Stiffness | $E / \rho$ | Elastic modulus per unit weight | GPa·cm³/g |
| 3 | Specific Energy Absorption | $E_{abs} / \rho$ | Crashworthiness per unit weight | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Ashby Index (lightweight beam) | $\sigma_u^{2/3} / \rho$ | Optimal material for stiffness-limited light design | — |
| 5 | Ashby Index (lightweight panel) | $\sigma_u^{1/2} / \rho$ | Optimal for buckling-resistant panels | — |
| 6 | Buy-to-Fly Ratio | $m_{raw} / m_{final}$ | Material waste indicator | dimensionless |
| 7 | Mass Reduction % | $\left[1 - \rho_{new}/\rho_{ref}\right]\cdot 100$ | Savings relative to baseline | % |
| 8 | Energy Density (battery) | $E_{cell}/m_{cell}$ | Range per unit battery mass | Wh/kg |
| 9 | Recycling Index | $m_{recycled}/m_{input}$ | Circularity metric | dimensionless |
| 10 | TRL Score | $1$ to $9$ (integer) | Technology maturity | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute-value notation:** In this cheat sheet and the derivations that follow, absolute values are written as $\lvert x \rvert$ using `\lvert \rvert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics directly govern **OEM material selection decisions**. For example, BMW i3's CFRP passenger cell achieves a **CRFP/aluminium hybrid body-in-white** with **TRL 8–9** maturity; Tesla's structural battery pack (**4680 cells**) integrates cell-to-chassis architecture (**TRL 6–7**) — illustrating how R&D opportunities transition from lab-scale (**TRL 3–4**) to production-line (**TRL 8–9**) over typical **7–10 year** horizons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Comparative Specific Strength Analysis

**Problem Setup:** Compare the specific strength of three candidate automotive materials:
- Mild steel (AISI 1018): $\sigma_u$ = **440 MPa**, $\rho$ = **7.87 g/cm³**
- Aluminium alloy (AA 6061-T6): $\sigma_u$ = **310 MPa**, $\rho$ = **2.70 g/cm³**
- CFRP (unidirectional T700/epoxy, Vf = 0.6): $\sigma_u$ = **2200 MPa**, $\rho$ = **1.55 g/cm³**

**Step 1 — Write the definition of specific strength:**

$$
SS \;\equiv\; \frac{\sigma_u}{\rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $\rho$ in compatible units (kg/m³):** Conversion factor $1\text{ g/cm}^3 = 1000\text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$
\rho_{steel} = 7.87 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3,\quad
\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3,\quad
\rho_{CFRP} = 1.55 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS$ for each material using aligned arithmetic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{steel} &= \frac{440 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}{7.87 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 55.9 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m/kg} = 55.9 \text{ kN·m/kg} \\[4pt]
SS_{Al} &= \frac{310 \times 10^6}{2.70 \times 10^3} = 114.8 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m/kg} = 114.8 \text{ kN·m/kg} \\[4pt]
SS_{CFRP} &= \frac{2200 \times 10^6}{1.55 \times 10^3} = 1419.4 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m/kg} = 1419.4 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the *multiplicative gain* over baseline steel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{Al} &= \frac{SS_{Al}}{SS_{steel}} = \frac{114.8}{55.9} \approx 2.05 \\[4pt]
G_{CFRP} &= \frac{SS_{CFRP}}{SS_{steel}} = \frac{1419.4}{55.9} \approx 25.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result in design terms:** To carry a design load of, say, $F = 50$ kN, the required cross-sectional area scales as $A = F/\sigma_u$. The mass of the member, $m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho$, therefore scales as $m \propto \rho/\sigma_u = 1/SS$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Al} / m_{steel} &= 1/G_{Al} \approx 0.488 \\[4pt]
m_{CFRP} / m_{steel} &= 1/G_{CFRP} \approx 0.039
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the quantitative justification for CFRP's dominance in high-end automotive and motorsport applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Reduction Potential (MRP) for Component Substitution

**Problem Setup:** A steel B-pillar (mass $m_s = 12$ kg) is to be replaced by an aluminium B-pillar. The component is **strength-driven** (axial mode), so the design parameter is $A \cdot \sigma_u$ = constant.

**Step 1 — Equate the load-carrying capacity:**

$$
A_{Al} \cdot \sigma_{u,Al} = A_{steel} \cdot \sigma_{u,steel}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the area ratio:**

$$
\frac{A_{Al}}{A_{steel}} = \frac{\sigma_{u,steel}}{\sigma_{u,Al}} = \frac{440}{310} = 1.419
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the masses:** With identical gauge length $L$ and uniform thickness $t$, the cross-sectional width $w$ scales as $A/t$, so $m \propto w \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \rho$. Holding $t$ constant, $m \propto w \cdot \rho$, but since $A = w \cdot t$, then $m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho$. Substitute the area ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Al} &= A_{Al} \cdot L \cdot \rho_{Al} = \left(\frac{\sigma_{u,steel}}{\sigma_{u,Al}}\right) \cdot A_{steel} \cdot L \cdot \rho_{Al} \\[4pt]
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{steel}} &= \left(\frac{\sigma_{u,steel}}{\sigma_{u,Al}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{steel}}\right) = \frac{1}{G_{Al}} \approx 0.488
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the absolute mass reduction:**

$$
\Delta m = m_{steel} - m_{Al} = 12 \cdot (1 - 0.488) = 6.14 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the result as Mass Reduction Potential (MRP):**

$$
\text{MRP} = \left(1 - \frac{m_{Al}}{m_{steel}}\right) \times 100\% = 51.2\%
$$

**Engineering Insight:** A **51 % mass reduction** on a single B-pillar, scaled across ~40 sheet-metal panels in a body-in-white, can yield **15–20 %** total vehicle mass reduction.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Criteria R&D Prioritization

The following table shows a weighted scoring model that an automotive OEM might use to prioritize among competing R&D opportunities. Each criterion is scored **1 (poor) – 5 (excellent)**.

| **R&D Opportunity** | **Light-weighting (0.25)** | **Sustainability (0.20)** | **Crash Safety (0.25)** | **Cost Viability (0.15)** | **TRL Readiness (0.15)** | **Weighted Score** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Gen-3 AHSS | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 5 | **4.10** |
| CFRP (woven) | 5 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | **3.55** |
| Mg Alloys (wrought) | 5 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 2 | **2.95** |
| Additive Mfg. (AI-Si-Mg) | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | **3.40** |
| Bio-composites (Flax/PP) | 3 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 4 | **3.55** |
| Solid-State Batteries | 2 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 3 | **2.80** |
| Self-Healing Coatings | 1 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | **2.65** |

**Reading the Matrix:** Gen-3 AHSS wins due to balanced performance and high TRL; solid-state batteries and self-healing coatings are *high-potential* but currently *low-Readiness* — they remain strategic R&D bets rather than deployment candidates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — R&D Ecosystem in Automotive Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Automotive R&D Drivers] --> B1[Lightweighting Mandate]
    A --> B2[Electrification]
    A --> B3[Sustainability Goals]
    A --> B4[Safety Regulations]
    A --> B5[Cost Pressure]

    B1 --> C1[Material Innovation]
    B2 --> C2[Process Innovation]
    B3 --> C3[Functional Innovation]
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C2

    C1 --> D1[Advanced High Strength Steels]
    C1 --> D2[Aluminium and Magnesium Alloys]
    C1 --> D3[Carbon Fibre Composites]
    C1 --> D4[Bio Based Polymers]
    C1 --> D5[Smart Materials]

    C2 --> E1[Additive Manufacturing]
    C2 --> E2[Friction Stir Processing]
    C2 --> E3[Severe Plastic Deformation]
    C2 --> E4[Hybrid Laser Arc Welding]

    C3 --> F1[Shape Memory Alloys]
    C3 --> F2[Self Healing Polymers]
    C3 --> F3[Piezoelectric Sensors]
    C3 --> F4[Solid State Battery Electrolytes]

    D1 --> G1[Vehicle Validation]
    D2 --> G1
    D3 --> G1
    D4 --> G1
    D5 --> G1
    E1 --> G1
    E2 --> G1
    E3 --> G1
    E4 --> G1
    F1 --> G1
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Technology Readiness Level 1 to 9]
    H1 --> I1[Lab Scale TRL 1 to 4]
    H1 --> I2[Pilot Scale TRL 5 to 6]
    H1 --> I3[Production Scale TRL 7 to 9]

    I3 --> J[Commercial Deployment]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Technology Readiness Level (TRL) Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[TRL 1 Basic Principles Observed] --> L2[TRL 2 Concept Formulated]
    L2 --> L3[TRL 3 Experimental Proof of Concept]
    L3 --> L4[TRL 4 Lab Validated Component]
    L4 --> L5[TRL 5 Component Validated in Relevant Environment]
    L5 --> L6[TRL 6 System Demonstrated in Relevant Environment]
    L6 --> L7[TRL 7 Prototype in Operational Environment]
    L7 --> L8[TRL 8 Qualified System with Full Scale Production]
    L8 --> L9[TRL 9 Commercial Application in Operational Environment]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Material Selection Decision Flow (Ashby-inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Define Functional Requirements]) --> Q1{Primary Function?}
    Q1 --> Q1A[Stiffness Driven]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Strength Driven]
    Q1 --> Q1C[Energy Absorption Driven]
    Q1 --> Q1D[Lightweight Panel Buckling Driven]

    Q1A --> R1[Select via E over rho]
    Q1B --> R2[Select via sigma_u over rho]
    Q1C --> R3[Select via E_abs over rho]
    Q1D --> R4[Select via sigma_u^0.5 over rho]

    R1 --> S1[Plot on Ashby Chart]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1

    S1 --> T1[Apply Property Filters]
    T1 --> T2[Apply Process Constraints]
    T2 --> T3[Apply Cost Constraints]
    T3 --> T4[Apply Sustainability Constraints]
    T4 --> Final([Final Material Choice])
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagrams in an exam:** Even if you cannot draw them, describing the **flow** (Driver $\rightarrow$ Pillar $\rightarrow$ Material Family $\rightarrow$ TRL Stage $\rightarrow$ Deployment) carries 2–3 marks on a long-answer question about R&D opportunities.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Technology Readiness Level (TRL) scale. State the TRL of the following: (i) CFRP body-in-white in mass-production cars, (ii) solid-state lithium batteries, (iii) laboratory-scale graphene synthesis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The TRL scale is a 1–9 metric (NASA/EU Horizon framework) used to classify the maturity of a technology, from basic principles (**TRL 1**) to full commercial deployment (**TRL 9**).
- (i) **TRL 8–9** — Production-deployed in BMW i3, Alfa Romeo 4C, etc.
- (ii) **TRL 4–5** — Lab + small-cell validation; pre-pilot stage (Toyota, QuantumScape).
- (iii) **TRL 2–3** — Concept/early experimental; not yet engineered into a component.

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for correct TRL assignment with justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three bio-based materials currently investigated for automotive interior applications. Mention one advantage and one limitation for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Flax fibre reinforced PP** — *Advantage:* renewable, low density (≈ 1.3 g/cm³), good vibration damping. *Limitation:* moisture absorption reduces long-term dimensional stability.
2. **Polylactic acid (PLA)** — *Advantage:* biodegradable, compostable, derived from corn-starch. *Limitation:* low heat-distortion temperature (≈ 55–60 °C) restricts use in non-powertrain parts.
3. **Natural rubber (from Hevea brasiliensis)** — *Advantage:* excellent elastic recovery, used in mounts/seals. *Limitation:* limited oxidative and ozone resistance; requires antioxidant additives.

*(1 mark per correctly stated material with one valid advantage/limitation pair.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the four major research pillars driving innovation in automotive materials. For each pillar, give two specific material/process examples currently under investigation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the specific strength and specific stiffness of mild steel (AISI 1018), aluminium 6061-T6, and unidirectional CFRP. Use the computed values to justify why CFRP is preferred for high-end body-in-white applications despite its high cost.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The four pillars of automotive materials R&D are:

**Pillar 1 — Material Innovation (2 marks):**
- *Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS):* Gen-3 and Gen-4 steels with multi-phase microstructures (ferrite + bainite + martensite + retained austenite) deliver tensile strengths of 1000–2000 MPa with adequate formability. Used in safety cage and crash-energy management structures.
- *Magnesium Alloys (e.g., WE43, AMX602):* Density 1.74 g/cm³; used in steering wheels, instrument panels, seat frames.

**Pillar 2 — Process Innovation (2 marks):**
- *Additive Manufacturing (SLM, EBM):* Enables topology-optimized brackets, battery housings, and bionic structures. Buy-to-fly ratio approaches 1.
- *Friction Stir Processing (FSP):* Refines grain structure of cast aluminium to sub-micron levels, increasing fatigue life by 2–3×.

**Pillar 3 — Functional Innovation (1.5 marks):**
- *Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol):* Used in adaptive crumple zones; recoverable strain ≈ 8 %.
- *Piezoelectric energy harvesters:* Convert vibration damping into usable electric energy.

**Pillar 4 — Sustainability Innovation (1.5 marks):**
- *Bio-based composites:* Flax/PP and hemp/epoxy panels replacing glass-fibre composites in interior trims.
- *Solid-state battery electrolytes (e.g., Li₆PS₅Cl):* Enable safe, high-energy-density EV powertrains.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given data:

$$
\sigma_{steel} = 440\text{ MPa}, \quad E_{steel} = 200\text{ GPa}, \quad \rho_{steel} = 7.87\text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
\sigma_{Al} = 310\text{ MPa}, \quad E_{Al} = 69\text{ GPa}, \quad \rho_{Al} = 2.70\text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
\sigma_{CFRP} = 2200\text{ MPa}, \quad E_{CFRP} = 135\text{ GPa}, \quad \rho_{CFRP} = 1.55\text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 1 — Compute specific strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{steel} &= 440 / 7.87 = 55.9 \text{ kN·m/kg} \\
SS_{Al} &= 310 / 2.70 = 114.8 \text{ kN·m/kg} \\
SS_{CFRP} &= 2200 / 1.55 = 1419.4 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific stiffness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SM_{steel} &= 200 / 7.87 = 25.4 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g} \\
SM_{Al} &= 69 / 2.70 = 25.6 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g} \\
SM_{CFRP} &= 135 / 1.55 = 87.1 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative analysis (2 marks):**

| **Material** | $SS$ (kN·m/kg) | $SM$ (GPa·cm³/g) | Multiplicative gain in $SS$ vs. steel |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Mild Steel | 55.9 | 25.4 | 1.0 |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 114.8 | 25.6 | 2.05 |
| CFRP (UD) | 1419.4 | 87.1 | 25.4 |

**Step 4 — Justification for CFRP in body-in-white (2 marks):**
CFRP provides **25×** the specific strength and **3.4×** the specific stiffness of steel. For a crashworthy body-in-white, this means:
- **Mass reduction of ~60 %** at equivalent strength.
- **Improved NVH** due to higher damping and stiffness.
- **Corrosion immunity** reducing warranty cost.

**Justification despite cost:** Although CFRP costs 10–20× more per kg, life-cycle analysis (LCA) — including fuel savings over the vehicle's lifetime, lower maintenance, and reduced emissions — yields a **payback period of 3–5 years** for premium applications, justifying its use in high-end vehicles (BMW i3, Alfa Romeo 4C, McLaren MonoCage).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of *additive manufacturing* in the future of automotive materials. Discuss at least two AM processes and one specific automotive component where AM is already deployed.

**(b) [7 marks]** A B-pillar of mass 18 kg is currently made of mild steel. It is proposed to replace it with a magnesium alloy (AZ91D). Given $\sigma_{steel} = 410$ MPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $\sigma_{Mg} = 230$ MPa, $\rho_{Mg} = 1.74$ g/cm³, calculate the new mass and the mass reduction percentage assuming equal load capacity and constant thickness.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Introduction (2 marks):** Additive manufacturing (AM) is a process innovation that fabricates parts layer-by-layer from CAD models, eliminating tooling and offering unprecedented geometric freedom. It enables *topology-optimized* designs, *bionic structures*, and *functionally graded materials* — directly aligned with the lightweighting and electrification pillars of R&D.

**AM Process — Selective Laser Melting (SLM) (2 marks):**
A high-power laser selectively fuses metal powder (e.g., AlSi10Mg, Ti6Al4V, maraging steel) layer-by-layer in an inert argon atmosphere. Layer thickness: 20–50 μm. Used for highly complex, load-optimized parts.

**AM Process — Binder Jetting (1 mark):**
A liquid binder is selectively deposited onto a metal powder bed; the part is later sintered in a furnace. Excellent for **large sand moulds and Al-Si castings**. Faster than SLM for large volumes.

**Deployed Component (2 marks):** The **Bugatti Chiron's titanium brake caliper** (made via SLM) is 41 % lighter than its aluminium predecessor, while the **Ford Mustang Shelby GT500's Shelby GT500R rear-housing bracket** is a topology-optimized steel part made by binder jetting. Also, **Czinger 21C** uses AM-printed structural nodes in its chassis.

**Strategic Significance:** AM enables **personalization**, **low-volume economic viability** (no minimum order quantity), and **in-situ alloying** — all cornerstones of future automotive R&D.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
$m_{steel} = 18$ kg, $\sigma_{steel} = 410$ MPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $\sigma_{Mg} = 230$ MPa, $\rho_{Mg} = 1.74$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Equate load capacities (1.5 marks):** For axial loading, $F = A \cdot \sigma_u$ must remain constant. With constant thickness $t$, $A = w \cdot t$, so $w \propto 1/\sigma_u$.

$$
\frac{w_{Mg}}{w_{steel}} = \frac{\sigma_{steel}}{\sigma_{Mg}} = \frac{410}{230} = 1.783
$$

**Step 2 — Express the mass in terms of width (1.5 marks):**

$$
m = w \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \rho
$$

For a pillar of given length $L$ and thickness $t$:

$$
\frac{m_{Mg}}{m_{steel}} = \frac{w_{Mg}}{w_{steel}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{Mg}}{\rho_{steel}} = \frac{\sigma_{steel}}{\sigma_{Mg}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{Mg}}{\rho_{steel}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{m_{Mg}}{m_{steel}} = \frac{410}{230} \cdot \frac{1.74}{7.87} = 1.783 \cdot 0.221 = 0.394
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new mass (1 mark):**

$$
m_{Mg} = 0.394 \times 18 = 7.09 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mass reduction percentage (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MRP} = \left(1 - 0.394\right) \times 100\% = 60.6\%
$$

**Conclusion (Bonus 0.5 mark if included):** Replacing the steel B-pillar with an AZ91D magnesium casting reduces mass from 18 kg to ≈ 7.1 kg — a saving of nearly **11 kg per vehicle** — but introduces challenges: lower ductility, higher CTE, and corrosion susceptibility, requiring R&D focus on rare-earth alloying and protective coatings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (–2 marks typical):** Density in g/cm³ must be converted to kg/m³ **or** stresses in MPa must be converted to Pa **before** dividing to get specific strength in consistent units. Do *not* mix units.
> 2. **Forgetting the strength-ratio term (–2 marks):** In mass-reduction derivations, students often write $m_{new}/m_{old} = \rho_{new}/\rho_{old}$ alone. This is wrong unless the materials have *equal strength*. The correct expression is $m_{new}/m_{old} = (\sigma_{old}/\sigma_{new})(\rho_{new}/\rho_{old})$.
> 3. **Vague TRL answers (–1 mark):** Simply stating "CFRP is mature" is insufficient. Always assign a *numeric TRL* and justify.
> 4. **Ignoring process innovation:** The syllabus specifically asks for *materials AND technologies*. Don't write only about materials — include AM, FSP, SPD, and emerging joining methods.
> 5. **Skipping sustainability:** KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on NEP-2020-aligned sustainability. Omitting bio-based, recycling, and hydrogen materials leads to a **0.5–1 mark** deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **R&D in automotive materials** is driven by the **4-pillar framework**: Material, Process, Functional, and Sustainability Innovation.
- **Lightweighting** is quantified by **Specific Strength** ($\sigma_u/\rho$) and **Specific Stiffness** ($E/\rho$); CFRP outperforms steel by **~25×** in specific strength.
- **Mass Reduction Potential (MRP)** for a *strength-driven* substitution is governed by: $m_{new}/m_{old} = (\sigma_{old}/\sigma_{new})(\rho_{new}/\rho_{old})$.
- **Technology Readiness Levels (TRL 1–9)** are the standard maturity yardstick — Gen-3 AHSS is at **TRL 8–9**, solid-state batteries at **TRL 4–5**, lab-scale graphene at **TRL 2–3**.
- **Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS)** remain the *workhorse* due to existing infrastructure; **CFRP** dominates high-end; **Mg alloys** and **bio-composites** are the *emerging frontiers*.
- **Additive Manufacturing (SLM, EBM, Binder Jetting)** is a process innovation that complements material innovation — enables topology optimization and buy-to-fly ratios near unity.
- **Smart materials** — SMAs, piezoelectrics, self-healing polymers, MR fluids — provide **functional innovation** beyond mere structural performance.
- **Sustainability** is non-negotiable: bio-based polymers, closed-loop ELV recycling, hydrogen storage, and solid-state battery electrolytes define the R&D agenda.
- **Ashby material indices** — $\sigma_u^{2/3}/\rho$ (lightweight beam), $\sigma_u^{1/2}/\rho$ (lightweight panel) — guide rational material selection on the property-chart.
- **Multi-criteria decision matrices** weighting Lightweighting, Sustainability, Crash Safety, Cost, and TRL are essential R&D prioritization tools.
- **Cost vs. benefit analysis (LCA)** is required to justify high-end material choices: CFRP's premium cost is offset by 3–5 year lifecycle payback in fuel and maintenance savings.
- **Always cite TRL + Mass Reduction % + Property metric** when discussing any R&D opportunity — this is the KTU examiner's gold-standard answer template.
